docs.title: CodeGraph
docs.text: Welcome to the CodeGraph documentation. This project provides a graph-based context selection engine for AI coding agents.

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
Instead of searching for textually similar code (keyword matching, embeddings), this system understands how code is structurally connected — through function calls, imports, and inheritance — and uses those connections to find what's relevant.

In one sentence: Parse a codebase into a graph of dependencies, use Personalized PageRank to find structurally relevant code given a task, and expose this through the Model Context Protocol (MCP) so any AI agent can use it.

docs.title: The Core Insight
docs.text:
Code is a graph, not text.

  • Nodes = code entities: functions, classes, methods, files
  • Edges = relationships: function A calls function B, file X imports file Y, class C inherits from class D

docs.title: Graph-Based Context Selection for AI Coding Agents
Overview
docs.text:
This project builds a tool that helps AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) find the right code context when working on a task.

Instead of searching for textually similar code (keyword matching, embeddings), this system understands how code is structurally connected — through function calls, imports, and inheritance — and uses those connections to find what's relevant.

In one sentence: Parse a codebase into a graph of dependencies, use Personalized PageRank to find structurally relevant code given a task, and expose this through the Model Context Protocol (MCP) so any AI agent can use it.

docs.title: The Core Insight
docs.text:
Code is a graph, not text.

  • Nodes = code entities: functions, classes, methods, files
  • Edges = relationships: function A calls function B, file X imports file Y, class C inherits from class D

Graph-based search follows structural connections to find relevant code regardless of textual similarity. A function BaseHasher in a distant file with different variable names is still found if it's connected to authenticate() through the call chain.

→ Deep dive: Problem & Motivation

docs.title: System Pipeline
docs.text:
The system has four stages, each feeding into the next:

STAGE 1: PARSE
  Input:  A Python repository (source code files)
  Action: Parse each file into an AST using tree-sitter
  Output: Syntax tree per file
    │
    ▼
STAGE 2: EXTRACT & RESOLVE
  Input:  Syntax trees from Stage 1
  Action: Extract entities (functions, classes, imports),
          resolve cross-file connections (imports → files, calls → definitions)
  Output: Lists of nodes (entities) and edges (relationships)
    │
    ▼
STAGE 3: BUILD GRAPH & RUN PPR
  Input:  Nodes and edges from Stage 2
  Action: Load into Neo4j, create in-memory projection,
          run Personalized PageRank from seed nodes
  Output: Ranked list of code entities with relevance scores
    │
    ▼
STAGE 4: SERVE
  Input:  The graph database + PPR algorithm (on-demand)
  Action: Expose as an MCP server that AI agents can call
  Output: Top-k most relevant code snippets with source

docs.title: Component Map
docs.text: Stage Component Deep Dive Parse tree-sitter parsing + entity extraction tree-sitter & Parsing Resolve Import resolution + call resolution Import & Call Resolution Graph Neo4j storage, schema, projections Neo4j & Graph Schema Algorithm Personalized PageRank Personalized PageRank Seeds Multi-signal seed selection Seed Node Selection Post-processing IDF weighting, token budgeting Post-Processing Serve MCP server + tools MCP Server Evaluate SWE-bench benchmarks, ablations Evaluation

docs.title: Performance Expectations
docs.text:
Operation Expected Time tree-sitter parsing (100k LOC) 0.5–2 seconds Entity extraction + resolution 1–5 seconds Neo4j graph loading (batched) 2–15 seconds GDS projection creation < 1 second PPR execution 1–10 milliseconds Source code reading + formatting 50–200 milliseconds Full pipeline (parse → result) 5–30 seconds Query only (graph already built) < 250 milliseconds

The graph is built once per repository state. Subsequent queries take under 250ms.

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
1. Static analysis only — Dynamic behaviors (monkey-patching, eval()) are invisible.
  2. Python only — Architecture is language-extensible, but this thesis covers Python.
  3. Imperfect import resolution — Custom resolver handles ~80% of imports; complex patterns may be missed.
  4. Imperfect call resolution — Without type inference, duck-typed method calls are unresolvable.
  5. Utility function noise — IDF weighting reduces but may not eliminate highly-connected generic functions.
  6. No incremental updates — Graph must be rebuilt when code changes (5–30 seconds).
  7. PPR may not always win — BM25 or 1-hop ego-graphs may perform comparably for some tasks.

docs.title: Research Foundation
docs.text:
This project combines ideas from three recent papers, adding PPR and MCP as novel contributions.

→ Deep dive: Research Papers

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: → Glossary of all terms

docs.title: Why This Project Exists
docs.text:
AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) need to see the right parts of a codebase to do their job. They have a limited context window (100k–200k tokens), but a medium-sized project might have 500k+ tokens of code. The assistant must choose which code to look at for each task.

This selection process is called context retrieval. The quality of the AI's output depends directly on whether it sees the right code.

docs.title: Embedding-Based Retrieval (Cursor, Copilot)
docs.text:
1. Every code chunk gets converted into a list of numbers (a vector or embedding) representing its meaning — like a fingerprint for each piece of code.
  2. When you ask a question, your question also gets converted into a vector.
  3. The tool finds code chunks whose vectors are most similar to your question's vector.
  4. Those "similar" chunks become the AI's context.

This is essentially keyword search on steroids — it finds code that talks about similar things as your question.

docs.title: Tool-Based Exploration (Claude Code)
docs.text: Claude Code doesn't pre-build an index. Instead, it uses tools (grep, file reading) to search the codebase on-the-fly, deciding what to look at as it goes. This avoids stale indexes but relies on the AI making good search decisions.

docs.title: Why These Approaches Fail for Code
docs.text:
Code is not like a book or a Wikipedia article. Consider this scenario:

You need to fix a bug in authenticate(). This function calls hash_password(), which uses BCryptHasher, which inherits from BaseHasher. The BaseHasher class is defined in a completely different file with different variable names and different comments.

Embedding-based search will miss BaseHasher because it looks nothing like authenticate() textually. But structurally, it's critically important — if you change how hashing works, BaseHasher is part of the chain.

docs.title: The Fundamental Issue
docs.text:
Embeddings find code that looks similar in text, but code relevance is determined by structural connections — what calls what, what imports what, what inherits from what.

These connections form a graph. A graph requires graph-based search, not text-based search.

docs.title: Academic Evidence
docs.text:
• A Google DeepMind paper (August 2025) formally proved that embedding-based retrieval has mathematical limitations: there exist relevant documents that cannot be retrieved by any query in an embedding space. This is a theoretical ceiling, not just a practical one.

  • Five major papers from top conferences in 2024–2025 (NAACL, ICLR, ACL, ASE) independently showed that using code's graph structure for retrieval substantially outperforms text-based approaches, with improvements ranging from +3% to +33%.

docs.title: What This Project Does Differently
docs.text:
Instead of text similarity, this project:

  1. Parses the codebase into a structured graph of dependencies
  2. Runs a graph algorithm (Personalized PageRank) to find structurally relevant code
  3. Exposes this through a standard protocol (MCP) any AI agent can plug into

The result: context retrieval that understands code structure, not just keywords.

→ Next: How tree-sitter parsing works

docs.title: tree-sitter & Parsing
docs.text: This document covers two tightly coupled steps: parsing source code into syntax trees (tree-sitter) and extracting entities from those trees.

docs.title: What tree-sitter Is
docs.text:
tree-sitter is a parser library. A parser reads source code (plain text) and converts it into a structured tree representing the code's syntax. This tree is called an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).

Analogy: Parsing is like diagramming a sentence in English class. "The dog chased the cat" becomes: subject ("The dog"), verb ("chased"), object ("the cat"). An AST does the same for code: this is a function definition, that's a function call, this is an if-statement.

docs.title: What tree-sitter Produces
docs.text:
Given this Python code:

def authenticate(username, password):
    user = find_user(username)
    if user and check_password(user, password):
        return create_token(user)
    return None

tree-sitter produces a tree like:

function_definition
  name: "authenticate"
  parameters: ["username", "password"]
  body:
    assignment
      left: "user"
      right: call(name: "find_user", args: ["username"])
    if_statement
      condition: ...
      body:
        return_statement
          value: call(name: "create_token", args: ["user"])
    return_statement
      value: None

This tells us: there's a function called authenticate, and it calls find_user, check_password, and create_token.

docs.title: Why tree-sitter
docs.text: Property Detail Fast ~5–10ms per 1,000 lines. A 100,000-line codebase takes under 2 seconds. Multi-language Supports 100+ languages (Python, JS, TS, Java, Go, Rust…). Industry standard Used by GitHub, Cursor, and many other tools. Error-tolerant Handles broken code — marks broken parts and keeps going.

docs.title: What tree-sitter Cannot Do
docs.text:
This is critical:

  • Cannot resolve imports — Sees from utils import helper as text, doesn't know which file utils refers to.
  • Cannot resolve types — Sees obj.method() but doesn't know which class obj is.
  • Cannot look across files — Only parses one file at a time.

tree-sitter gives us the raw material. Connecting entities across files is handled by Import & Call Resolution.

docs.title: How tree-sitter Is Used in This Project
docs.text:
import tree_sitter_python as tspython
from tree_sitter import Language, Parser

parser = Parser(Language(tspython.language()))

with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
    source = f.read()

tree = parser.parse(source)
# tree.root_node gives access to the entire AST

tree-sitter also provides a query language for pattern matching:

(function_definition name: (identifier) @function.name)

This says: "find every function_definition node and capture its name child." The @function.name part is like naming a capture group in regex.

docs.title: Entity Extraction
docs.text: After tree-sitter produces a syntax tree per file, we walk through it and pull out the entities that become nodes in our graph.

docs.title: What We Extract
docs.text: Entity Example Captured Properties Function (standalone) def validate_email(email): name, file_path, line_number, end_line, signature, docstring Class class AuthService(BaseService): name, file_path, line_number, end_line, bases (parent classes) Method (inside a class) class AuthService: → def authenticate(self, ...): Same as Function + class_name Import from src.utils.helpers import validate_email module_path, imported_names, is_relative Function call (inside bodies) user = find_user(username) caller_name, callee_name, line_number

docs.title: What We Skip (and Why)
docs.text: Skipped Reason Standard library imports (import json) Part of Python, not the project — adds noise without structural value. Third-party library calls (requests.get()) Same — we only care about project-internal connections. Variables and constants Not included as nodes for simplicity. Functions and classes capture the meaningful relationships. Decorators, type annotations, comments Not extracted as separate entities (decorators noted as properties).

docs.title: Extraction Output
docs.text:
For each file, the extractor produces:

{
    "file": "src/services/auth_service.py",
    "classes": [
        {"name": "AuthService", "line": 10, "end_line": 85, "bases": ["BaseService"]},
    ],
    "functions": [],
    "methods": [
        {"name": "authenticate", "class": "AuthService", "line": 12, "end_line": 30,
         "signature": "(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool"},
        {"name": "refresh_token", "class": "AuthService", "line": 32, "end_line": 50,
         "signature": "(self, token: str) -> str"},
    ],
    "imports": [
        {"module": "src.models.user", "names": ["User"], "is_relative": False},
        {"module": "src.utils.crypto", "names": ["hash_password"], "is_relative": False},
    ],
    "calls": [
        {"caller": "AuthService.authenticate", "callee": "hash_password", "line": 22},
        {"caller": "AuthService.authenticate", "callee": "User.find_by_username", "line": 18},
    ],
}

After processing all files, the raw data is passed to the resolution step.

docs.title: Qualified Names
docs.text:
To uniquely identify entities across the codebase, we use qualified names — the full path to an entity:

Entity Qualified Name Function validate_email in src/utils/helpers.py src.utils.helpers.validate_email Method authenticate on AuthService in src/services/auth_service.py src.services.auth_service.AuthService.authenticate Class User in src/models/user.py src.models.user.User

This prevents ambiguity when multiple files have functions with the same name.

→ Next: How cross-file resolution works

docs.title: Import & Call Resolution
docs.text: This is the trickiest part of the pipeline. tree-sitter gives us raw entities per file, but it doesn't connect them across files. Resolution fills in those cross-file connections.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
tree-sitter parses from src.utils.helpers import validate_email and gives us the string "src.utils.helpers" and the name "validate_email". But tree-sitter does not tell us which actual file on disk "src.utils.helpers" refers to.

Import resolution turns "src.utils.helpers" → /project/src/utils/helpers.py.

Without this, we can't create IMPORTS edges in the graph because we don't know which files are connected.

docs.title: How Python Imports Work (Simplified)
docs.text:
When Python sees from src.utils.helpers import validate_email:

  1. Take the dotted path src.utils.helpers
  2. Convert dots to directory separators: src/utils/helpers
  3. Look for either:
  4. A file: src/utils/helpers.py
  5. A package: src/utils/helpers/__init__.py
  6. Inside that file, look for validate_email

Relative imports start with dots: from ..models import User. Each dot means "go up one directory." In src/services/auth_service.py, ..models means src/models.

docs.title: Easy Cases (~80% of imports in well-structured projects)
docs.text:
from src.utils.helpers import validate_email
# → Find src/utils/helpers.py in the project. Done.

Straightforward string manipulation + filesystem check.

docs.title: Medium Cases (~15%)
docs.text:
Relative imports: 

from ..models import User
# → Need current file's location, count dots, go up directories,
#   find models.py or models/__init__.py

Re-exports through __init__.py: 

# src/models/__init__.py contains:
from .user import User
from .post import Post

# So "from src.models import User" actually resolves to src/models/user.py

For the initial implementation, resolving to the __init__.py file itself is acceptable — the connection between src/models/ and the importing file is still captured.

docs.title: Impossible Cases (~5% — skipped)
docs.text:
module = importlib.import_module(config.module_name)   # runtime string
exec("from " + module_name + " import something")      # generated code

These depend on values only known at runtime. No static analysis tool can resolve them. We log them as unresolved and move on.

docs.title: The Resolver
docs.text:
A custom resolver (~150–200 lines of Python) handles easy and medium cases:

def resolve_import(import_path: str, current_file: str, project_root: str) -> str | None:
    if import_path.startswith('.'):
        # Relative import: count leading dots, resolve relative to current file
        dots = len(import_path) - len(import_path.lstrip('.'))
        remaining = import_path.lstrip('.')
        base_dir = current_file_directory
        for _ in range(dots):
            base_dir = parent(base_dir)
        relative_path = remaining.replace('.', '/')
    else:
        # Absolute import: resolve from project root
        relative_path = import_path.replace('.', '/')
        base_dir = project_root

    # Try as a module file
    candidate = base_dir / (relative_path + '.py')
    if exists(candidate):
        return candidate

    # Try as a package
    candidate = base_dir / relative_path / '__init__.py'
    if exists(candidate):
        return candidate

    return None  # Unresolved — log it and continue

Why custom over jedi: The custom resolver is educational (good for thesis) and sufficient for well-structured SWE-bench repos (Flask, Django, scikit-learn). If resolution falls below ~75%, jedi can be added as a fallback — it understands Python's full import system.

Reporting: The import resolution rate (e.g., "resolved 87% of 610 imports in Flask") is included in the thesis as an honesty metric.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Entity extraction says: "function authenticate calls something named hash_password." But which hash_password? There might be multiple definitions with that name across the codebase.

Call resolution connects call sites (where a function is called) to function definitions (where it's defined).

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Call resolution uses the information from import resolution:

# In auth_service.py:
from src.utils.crypto import hash_password    # ← import resolution → src/utils/crypto.py

def authenticate(self, username, password):
    hashed = hash_password(password)           # ← call resolves to src.utils.crypto.hash_password

The resolution logic — when we see a call to some_function() inside a file:

Check Resolution Was some_function imported from another file? Resolve to that file's definition Is some_function defined in the same file? Resolve to the local definition Is it a self.method() call? Resolve to the method on the same class Can't determine? Log as unresolved

For method calls on objects (user.save()), the challenge is knowing the type of user. Heuristics used: - If user was assigned from User.find_by_id(...) → guess User instance - If type hints are present (user: User = ...) → use the type hint - Otherwise → log as unresolved

docs.title: What Gets Created
docs.text:
Each resolved call becomes a CALLS edge in the graph:

authenticate() ──CALLS──▶ hash_password()
authenticate() ──CALLS──▶ User.find_by_username()
process_login() ──CALLS──▶ authenticate()

Expected resolution rates: For Python projects with good structure and type hints, expect 60–80% resolution. This is acceptable — PPR works with incomplete graphs. It just won't find connections through the missing edges.

→ Next: How the graph is stored in Neo4j

docs.title: Neo4j & Graph Schema
docs.text: This document covers the graph database, the schema design, data loading, and in-memory projections for algorithm execution.

docs.title: Why Neo4j
docs.text:
Neo4j is a graph database — a database specifically designed to store and query graph-structured data (nodes and relationships).

Reason Detail Natural fit Our data is a graph (code entities with relationships) Built-in algorithms Graph Data Science (GDS) library includes PPR out of the box Query language Cypher — an expressive language for graph queries Free for development Neo4j Desktop provides a free Enterprise license Validated by prior work The CodexGraph paper (NAACL 2025) used Neo4j for the same kind of code graph

docs.title: Node Types (4)
docs.text: Node Type What It Represents Example Key Properties File A .py source file auth_service.py file_path, name, line_count Class A class definition AuthService name, file_path, line_number, end_line Function A standalone function validate_email() name, file_path, line_number, end_line, signature Method A function inside a class AuthService.authenticate() name, file_path, line_number, signature, class_name

docs.title: Edge Types (4)
docs.text: Edge Type Connects Meaning Example CONTAINS File → Class, Class → Method "This file defines this class" / "This class has this method" auth_service.py CONTAINS AuthService IMPORTS File → File "This file imports from that file" auth_service.py IMPORTS user_model.py CALLS Function/Method → Function/Method "This function calls that function" authenticate() CALLS hash_password() INHERITS_FROM Class → Class "This class extends that class" AuthService INHERITS_FROM BaseService

docs.title: Why This Schema
docs.text:
This follows LocAgent (ACL 2025), which used 4 entity types + 4 relation types and achieved the best results among reviewed papers. The insight: you need enough relationship types to capture important structural connections, but too many types adds noise.

CONTAINS, IMPORTS, CALLS, and INHERITS_FROM capture the vast majority of meaningful structural relationships in a Python codebase.

docs.title: Loading Data into Neo4j
docs.text:
After entity extraction and resolution, nodes and edges are loaded using batched operations:

# Loading function nodes in a batch (thousands per second)
tx.run("""
    UNWIND $nodes AS node
    MERGE (n:Function {qualified_name: node.qualified_name})
    SET n.name = node.name,
        n.file_path = node.file_path,
        n.line_number = node.line_number,
        n.end_line = node.end_line,
        n.signature = node.signature
""", nodes=function_list)

MERGE means "create if it doesn't exist, or match if it does" — making the operation idempotent (safe to run multiple times).

Performance: Loading 10,000–50,000 nodes takes 2–15 seconds. For thesis-sized repositories (Flask ~5,000 LOC, Django ~300,000 LOC), this is fast enough.

docs.title: Edge Weight Configuration
docs.text:
Edges carry weights that reflect how strong the coupling is. The weight system combines a base weight per relationship type with an IDF adjustment:

final_weight(A → B) = base_weight(edge_type) / log₂(in_degree(B) + 2)

docs.title: Base Weights
docs.text:
Edge Type Base Weight Rationale INHERITS_FROM 1.0 Strongest coupling — changing a base class affects all subclasses CALLS 0.8 Direct dependency — callee changes may break the caller IMPORTS 0.5 File-level connection — weaker than function-level call CONTAINS 0.4 Structural containment — relevant but not a dependency

The IDF adjustment is explained in detail in Post-Processing.

docs.title: GDS Graph Projection
docs.text:
Before running algorithms, Neo4j's Graph Data Science (GDS) library needs an in-memory projection — a copy of the graph loaded into RAM for fast computation:

CALL gds.graph.project(
  'codeGraph',                                    -- name for this projection
  ['Function', 'Method', 'Class', 'File'],        -- node types to include
  {
    CALLS:         {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    IMPORTS:       {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    INHERITS_FROM: {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    CONTAINS:      {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'}
  }
)

Why UNDIRECTED: For context selection, you want to see both what a function uses (to understand it) and what uses it (to know what might break). See PPR directionality for the full rationale.

Memory: A 50,000-node graph with 200,000 edges needs ~5.2 MB in-memory. Trivial for modern laptops.

→ Next: How Personalized PageRank works

docs.title: Personalized PageRank (PPR)
docs.text: This is the core algorithm of the project. This document explains it from scratch — no prior knowledge assumed.

docs.title: Starting with Regular PageRank
docs.text:
PageRank was invented by Larry Page and Sergey Brin (Google founders, 1998) to rank web pages.

The intuition: Imagine a person randomly browsing the web. They start on a random page, randomly click a link, then another, and so on. Pages they visit most often are the most "important."

Why? Important pages have many incoming links from other important pages. The random surfer ends up at these pages frequently.

The teleportation trick: What if the surfer reaches a dead end or a loop? With some probability (typically 15%), the surfer "teleports" — jumps to a completely random page. This ensures they eventually visit every page.

The result: a score for every page representing visit frequency. Higher score = more important.

docs.title: What Makes PPR Different
docs.text:
In regular PageRank, teleportation goes to any random page with equal probability → global importance ranking.

In Personalized PageRank (PPR), teleportation goes back to specific pages you choose — called seed nodes. This biases the walk toward a particular region of the graph.

Algorithm Teleports to Answers PageRank Any random node "What are the most important nodes globally?" PPR Specific seed nodes "What are the most important nodes relative to my seeds?"

For code: PageRank tells you the most important functions in the entire codebase. PPR from authenticate() tells you the most important functions relative to authenticate() — the ones most structurally connected to it.

docs.title: How PPR Works Step by Step
docs.text:
Given a graph and a seed node (e.g., authenticate()):

  1. Start at the seed. Place all probability mass (1.0 units of "paint") on the seed.

  2. Walk step. From each node with paint, spread it along edges. If a node has 3 edges, each gets 1/3 of the paint. But only spread (1 − α) of the paint — keep α at the seed.

  3. α (alpha) = restart probability (typically 0.15). Controls how far paint spreads. Higher α = stays closer to seed.

  4. Repeat ~20 iterations. The paint distribution converges.

  5. Read scores. The paint at each node = its PPR score = its relevance to the seed.

docs.title: Concrete Example
docs.text:
Seed: authenticate()

After PPR:
  authenticate()          → 0.150  (high — it's the seed)
  hash_password()         → 0.082  (high — directly called by seed)
  check_token()           → 0.067  (high — directly called by seed)
  User.find_by_name()     → 0.045  (medium — called by seed)
  BaseService.init()      → 0.023  (medium — inherited by seed's class)
  process_login()         → 0.018  (medium — calls the seed)
  log_error()             → 0.012  (lower — connected but generic)
  send_email()            → 0.001  (very low — barely connected)

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text:
Parameter Controls Default Effect of Increasing α (alpha) / restart probability How close results stay to seed 0.15 Results cluster tighter around seed maxIterations Precision of scores 20 More precise (diminishing returns after ~20) tolerance Early stopping threshold 1e-7 Lower = more precise but slower top-k Number of results returned 10–20 More context but more noise / tokens used

In Neo4j GDS, dampingFactor = 1 − α. So dampingFactor = 0.85 means α = 0.15 (15% teleport chance).

docs.title: Why PPR Is the Right Algorithm
docs.text: Approach How It Works Limitation 1-hop neighbors Return everything directly connected Misses indirect connections (A→B→C — C is missed) k-hop ego-graph (RepoGraph) Return everything within k edges k=1 too narrow; k=2 explodes; no ranking within results Shortest path Find closest entities by edge count Doesn't account for multiple paths or connection strength PPR (this project) Probabilistic relevance via all paths Slightly more complex — but Neo4j GDS handles it

docs.title: PPR's Advantages
docs.text:
• Multi-hop: Finds indirectly connected but important code
  • Ranked: Every node gets a score → take exactly top-k
  • Multiple paths matter: Node X connected through 5 paths scores higher than Y through 1 path
  • Mathematically principled: Guaranteed convergence, provably correct
  • Fast: 1–10ms on 50,000-node graphs

docs.title: Directionality
docs.text:
Edges have a natural direction. authenticate() CALLS hash_password() is an edge from authenticate to hash_password. PPR can follow edges in different directions:

Direction What PPR Finds Example Natural (follow edge direction) Things the seed depends on (downstream) authenticate → finds hash_password Reverse (follow edges backward) Things that depend on the seed (upstream) authenticate → finds process_login Undirected (both ways) Everything structurally connected Finds both hash_password AND process_login

Decision: UNDIRECTED as default. An AI agent fixing authenticate() needs to see hash_password() (to understand the implementation) AND process_login() (to check if the fix affects callers).

Evaluation includes directed and reversed PPR as ablation experiments to measure which direction works best.

docs.title: Running PPR in Neo4j
docs.text:
-- Find the seed node
MATCH (seed:Function {name: 'authenticate', file_path: 'src/services/auth_service.py'})

-- Run PPR from that seed
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codeGraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [seed],
  relationshipWeightProperty: 'weight'
})
YIELD nodeId, score

-- Return top 20 results
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS name,
       gds.util.asNode(nodeId).file_path AS file,
       score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 20

docs.title: Multiple Seeds
docs.text:
PPR can accept multiple seed nodes with different weights:

MATCH (a:Function {name: 'authenticate'}), (b:Function {name: 'refresh_token'})
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codeGraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [a, b]
})

Paint is distributed equally across seeds. GDS also supports weighted seeds to give more importance to one over another.

→ Next: How seeds are chosen

docs.title: Seed Node Selection
docs.text: PPR starts from seed nodes and spreads outward. The quality of the seeds directly determines the quality of the results. Good seeds → relevant context. Bad seeds → useless context.

docs.title: The Core Question
docs.text: How does the system decide where to start PPR?

docs.title: Why "Current File" Is a Bad Default
docs.text:
A naïve approach: "the developer is editing auth_service.py, so use all functions in that file as seeds." This fails because:

  • The developer might have been searching for an hour and landed on a random file
  • The developer might be reading code, not editing the part that matters
  • The AI agent might be exploring multiple files in a multi-step task

"Where the cursor is" ≠ "what the developer cares about."

docs.title: The Actual Approach: Agent-Provided Context
docs.text:
The MCP server doesn't guess the seed — the AI agent calling the tool tells it what the task is. When a developer says "fix the authentication timeout bug" to Claude Code, the agent already understands the task. It passes that understanding to our tool via the tool parameters.

@mcp.tool()
def get_relevant_context(
    task_description: str,                  # "fix the authentication timeout bug"
    mentioned_entities: list[str] = None,   # ["authenticate", "TokenExpiredError"]
    current_file: str = None                # optional low-weight hint
) -> str:

docs.title: The Seed Extraction Pipeline
docs.text: Seeds come from multiple signals with different confidence levels:

docs.title: Step 1: Explicit Entity Extraction (HIGH weight)
docs.text:
Match explicitly mentioned names against graph nodes.

Input:  task_description = "fix the authentication timeout in AuthService"
        mentioned_entities = ["authenticate", "AuthService", "TokenExpiredError"]
Action: Match these names against nodes in the graph
Result: AuthService (exact match), authenticate method (exact match)

docs.title: Step 2: BM25 Keyword Search (MEDIUM weight)
docs.text:
Search the task description text against all function signatures and docstrings.

Input:  task_description text
Action: BM25 ranks functions by textual relevance to the task
Result: Top 3–5 functions mentioning "timeout", "token", "auth", etc.

What is BM25? A text search algorithm. Given a query ("authentication timeout") and a set of documents (all function signatures + docstrings), it ranks documents by how well they match. It considers: does the document contain the query words? How many times? How long is the document? BM25 is the standard baseline in information retrieval — simple, fast, and effective.

docs.title: Step 3: Current File as Weak Hint (LOW weight)
docs.text:
If provided, add functions from the current file as seeds.

Input:  current_file = "src/services/auth_service.py"
Action: Add functions from this file as seeds

docs.title: Step 4: Build the Personalization Vector
docs.text:
Combine all seeds with their weights, normalize to sum to 1.0:

{
  AuthService.authenticate: 0.35,
  AuthService: 0.25,
  timeout_handler: 0.15,
  token_validator: 0.10,
  AuthService.refresh_token: 0.08,
  AuthService.logout: 0.07
}

This becomes the personalization vector for PPR.

docs.title: Why Multi-Signal Matters
docs.text:
Different scenarios benefit from different signals:

Scenario Best Signal Why "Fix bug in authenticate()" Explicit entity extraction Clear function name mentioned "The login flow is broken" BM25 keyword search No specific function, but keywords help "Continue working on the auth module" Current file + BM25 Vague task, file hint narrows scope SWE-bench evaluation (bug report text) Entity extraction + BM25 Bug reports often mention specific classes

By combining signals with weights, the system degrades gracefully — even when one signal is weak, the others compensate.

→ Next: How PPR results are refined

docs.title: Post-Processing
docs.text: PPR returns a ranked list of nodes with scores. Before sending results to the AI agent, two post-processing steps improve quality.

docs.title: What Happens
docs.text:
A function like log_error() is called by 200 other functions across the codebase. Because it's connected to everything, PPR from any seed will rank it highly. But log_error() is not useful context — it's generic infrastructure code.

Analogy: In Google Search, if "Wikipedia" appeared as #1 for every query, that's technically correct (Wikipedia is linked everywhere) but not helpful.

docs.title: Solution: IDF-Inspired Edge Weight Adjustment
docs.text:
IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) is a concept from text search: terms that appear in many documents are less informative than terms that appear in few. "The" appears everywhere → low weight. "Authenticate" appears rarely → high weight.

Applied to our graph: functions called by many others are less informative than functions called by few. We adjust edge weights:

adjusted_weight(A → B) = base_weight(edge_type) / log₂(in_degree(B) + 2)

Where in_degree(B) = number of incoming edges to node B.

docs.title: The Effect
docs.text:
Function in_degree Weight Adjustment Effect log_error() 200 base / log₂(202) ≈ base / 7.7 Heavily downweighted hash_password() 3 base / log₂(5) ≈ base / 2.3 Slightly downweighted calculate_token_expiry() 1 base / log₂(3) ≈ base / 1.6 Barely affected

PPR naturally reduces utility function scores while preserving scores for specific, task-relevant functions.

docs.title: Base Weights by Edge Type
docs.text:
Edge Type Base Weight Rationale INHERITS_FROM 1.0 Strongest coupling — base class changes affect all subclasses CALLS 0.8 Direct dependency — callee changes may break caller IMPORTS 0.5 File-level connection — weaker than function-level CONTAINS 0.4 Structural containment — relevant but not a dependency

These weights are applied when creating the GDS graph projection and used by PPR during the random walk.

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text: PPR returns node names with scores. The AI agent needs actual source code, not just names. But we can't send unlimited code — there's a token budget.

docs.title: The Process
docs.text:
1. Take the top-k results from PPR (e.g., top 20)
  2. For each result, read the actual source code using stored file_path, line_number, end_line
  3. Accumulate results until reaching the token budget
  4. Format and return
def format_context(ppr_results, token_budget=6000):
    context_parts = []
    tokens_used = 0

    for node, score in ppr_results:
        source_code = read_lines(node.file_path, node.line_number, node.end_line)
        source_tokens = count_tokens(source_code)

        if tokens_used + source_tokens > token_budget:
            break  # Budget filled

        context_parts.append({
            "entity": node.name,
            "file": node.file_path,
            "lines": f"{node.line_number}-{node.end_line}",
            "relevance_score": round(score, 4),
            "source_code": source_code
        })
        tokens_used += source_tokens

    return context_parts

docs.title: Why 4,000–8,000 Tokens
docs.text:
The AI agent has its own context window to manage (100K–200K tokens). Our context is one of many inputs. 4,000–8,000 tokens of highly relevant code is more useful than 50,000 tokens of loosely relevant code — the agent can focus on what matters.

→ Next: How the MCP server exposes this

docs.title: MCP Server
docs.text: This document covers the Model Context Protocol, the tool design, and how AI agents interact with the system.

docs.title: What MCP Is
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 that defines how AI applications communicate with external tools and data sources.

Analogy: Before USB, every device had its own unique connector. USB standardized this — any USB device plugs into any USB port. MCP does the same for AI tools: any MCP-compliant tool plugs into any MCP-aware AI application.

Without MCP: you'd need separate integrations for Claude Desktop, Cursor, Copilot, etc. With MCP: build one server, it works with all of them.

docs.title: How MCP Works
docs.text:
MCP follows a client-server model:

Component Role Examples Server (our code) Provides tools — functions the AI can call This project's MCP server Host Connects to the server, lets the AI use tools Claude Desktop, Cursor Protocol JSON-RPC over STDIO (local) or HTTP (remote) Request/response messages

Each tool has: - A name — what the tool is called - A description — what it does (the AI reads this to decide when to use it) - A parameter schema — what inputs it accepts (JSON Schema) - A return value — what it sends back

docs.title: Tool 1: get_relevant_context (Primary)
docs.text:
The main entry point. Runs the full pipeline: seed extraction → PPR → post-processing.

@mcp.tool()
def get_relevant_context(
    task_description: str,
    mentioned_entities: list[str] = None,
    current_file: str = None,
    top_k: int = 15,
    token_budget: int = 6000
) -> str:
    """
    Find the most structurally relevant code for a given task.

    Uses Personalized PageRank on the code dependency graph to find
    functions, classes, and methods that are structurally connected
    to the entities involved in the task.
    """
    seeds = extract_seeds(task_description, mentioned_entities, current_file)
    ppr_results = run_ppr(seeds, top_k=top_k)
    context = format_context(ppr_results, token_budget=token_budget)
    return json.dumps(context)

docs.title: Tool 2: query_dependencies (Supporting)
docs.text:
Returns raw dependency information — no PPR ranking, just direct connections.

@mcp.tool()
def query_dependencies(
    entity_name: str,
    direction: str = "both",   # "upstream", "downstream", or "both"
    depth: int = 1             # 1 = direct only, 2 = include indirect
) -> str:
    """
    Get direct dependencies for a specific code entity.
    What does it call? What calls it? What does it import?
    """

docs.title: Tool 3: get_graph_stats (Diagnostic)
docs.text:
Returns statistics about the code graph — total nodes, edges, most connected files, etc. Helps the AI agent understand codebase structure.

@mcp.tool()
def get_graph_stats() -> str:
    """Return statistics about the code graph."""

docs.title: How an AI Agent Uses These Tools
docs.text:
When a developer asks Claude Code to "fix the authentication timeout bug":

  1. Claude Code reads the task description
  2. Claude decides it needs code context and calls our tool: 
    {
  "tool": "get_relevant_context",
  "arguments": {
    "task_description": "fix the authentication timeout bug",
    "mentioned_entities": ["authenticate", "timeout", "AuthService"]
  }
}
  3. Our MCP server receives this, runs seed extraction + PPR, returns relevant code
  4. Claude includes that code in its context and works on the fix

The AI agent decides when and how to call the tool. We provide the tool and let the agent use it.

docs.title: MCP Adoption Status
docs.text:
As of February 2026: - Supported by Claude Desktop - Adopted by OpenAI - Supported in Cursor through extensions - The system also provides a REST API fallback for tools that don't yet support MCP natively

→ Next: How we evaluate the system

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text: This document covers how the system is tested: the benchmark, the metrics, the baselines, and the ablation studies.

docs.title: What We're Measuring
docs.text:
The fundamental question: Does PPR retrieve the right code?

We answer this with real bug reports where we know which files were actually changed to fix the bug. If PPR's top results include those files, PPR "got it right."

docs.title: The Benchmark: SWE-bench Lite
docs.text:
SWE-bench Lite is a dataset of 300 real bug reports from popular Python repositories (Django, Flask, scikit-learn, matplotlib, etc.).

For each bug, the dataset provides:

Component Description Repository state before the fix A specific git commit Bug description The GitHub issue text Gold patch The actual code changes — which files and functions were changed

We don't fix bugs or run an LLM. We just ask: given this bug description, does PPR point to the files that were actually changed?

docs.title: Evaluation Process
docs.text:
For each of the 300 bug reports:
  1. Check out the repository at the "before fix" commit
  2. Build the code graph (tree-sitter → Neo4j)
  3. Extract seed nodes from the bug description text
  4. Run PPR from the seeds, get top-k results
  5. Check: do the top-k results include the files from the gold patch?
  6. Record hit/miss and rank of first correct result

docs.title: Recall@k
docs.text:
"Out of all files that needed changing, what percentage appeared in the top k results?"

Example: Bug required changing 3 files. PPR's top 10 included 2 of them → Recall@10 = 66.7%

We report Recall@5 and Recall@10.

docs.title: MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)
docs.text:
"On average, where did the first correct file appear in the ranking?"

First correct at #1 → 1.0 | at #3 → 0.33 | at #7 → 0.14

MRR = average across all 300 bug reports. Higher = correct files appear earlier.

docs.title: Baselines
docs.text: Baseline How It Works Why Include It Random Pick k random files Floor — any method must beat this BM25 Keyword search against code contents Standard text-retrieval baseline 1-hop ego-graph Return direct graph neighbors (no PPR) Tests whether multi-hop adds value

docs.title: Ablation Studies
docs.text:
An ablation study removes or changes one component to measure its contribution.

Experiment What Changes What It Reveals Remove CALLS edges Only IMPORTS, INHERITS, CONTAINS Importance of call relationships Remove IMPORTS edges Only CALLS, INHERITS, CONTAINS Importance of imports Remove INHERITS edges Only CALLS, IMPORTS, CONTAINS Importance of inheritance Change α α ∈ Optimal PPR spread distance Change direction Directed vs reversed vs undirected Best edge direction IDF weighting on/off Utility function handling toggle Value of downweighting common functions PPR vs 1-hop PPR vs simple neighbor lookup Algorithm sophistication value Change top-k k ∈ Results needed for good recall

Each ablation is a separate run with one variable changed — valuable thesis content regardless of outcome.

→ Next: Research papers this builds on

docs.title: Research Papers
docs.text: This project combines ideas from three recent papers, adding PPR and MCP as novel contributions.

docs.title: The Three Foundation Papers
docs.text: RepoGraph (ICLR 2025) LocAgent (ACL 2025) CodexGraph (NAACL 2025) What it does Builds a code graph, retrieves k-hop ego-graphs as context Builds a code graph, provides agent tools for search + traversal Builds a code graph in Neo4j, uses LLM to write Cypher queries Graph storage In-memory (custom) In-memory (custom) Neo4j Schema 2 edge types (contain, invoke) 4 edge types (contain, import, invoke, inherit) 5 edge types (contains, inherits, has_method, has_field, uses) Retrieval k-hop ego-graph (grab everything within k edges) BFS + BM25 search LLM generates Cypher queries Key limitation No ranking; k=2 explodes in size Relies on agent making good search queries Drops if LLM writes bad Cypher

docs.title: What This Project Takes from Each
From RepoGraph
docs.text:
• tree-sitter parsing approach
  • SWE-bench evaluation methodology
  • Plug-in architecture concept

docs.title: From LocAgent
docs.text:
• Graph schema (4 entity types + 4 relation types)
  • Seed extraction via keyword search

docs.title: From CodexGraph
docs.text:
• Neo4j as graph storage
  • Graph construction pipeline

docs.title: How This Project Improves on Each
docs.text: Paper Improvement RepoGraph PPR gives ranked results instead of unranked ego-graphs LocAgent MCP server instead of custom agent tools; PPR instead of BFS CodexGraph PPR is automatic (no LLM in retrieval); no fragile query translation

docs.title: The Novel Combination
docs.text:
1. From RepoGraph: Graph-based retrieval as a plug-in module for existing agents
  2. From LocAgent: Comprehensive 4-type graph schema + keyword search for seeds
  3. From CodexGraph: Neo4j as the graph database
  4. New: PPR as the retrieval algorithm (none of these papers use PPR)
  5. New: MCP as the integration protocol (none use MCP)

This is a valid Bachelor's thesis contribution: taking established techniques, combining them in a new way, and evaluating the result systematically. No breakthrough required.

→ Next: Glossary

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
All terms used across the documentation, defined in one place.

Term Definition AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) A tree-shaped representation of source code's structure. Each node represents a code construct (function, class, expression). Created by a parser. BM25 A text search algorithm that ranks documents by keyword relevance. Standard baseline in information retrieval research. Context window The maximum amount of text an LLM can process at once (typically 100K–200K tokens). Context retrieval The process of selecting which parts of a codebase the AI should see for a given task. Cypher Neo4j's query language (like SQL for graphs). Used to create, read, update, and delete nodes and relationships. Damping factor In PageRank, the probability of following a link (vs. teleporting). Typically 0.85. In PPR terms: damping = 1 − α. Edge A connection between two nodes in a graph. In this project: CALLS, IMPORTS, INHERITS_FROM, CONTAINS. Embedding A list of numbers (vector) representing the "meaning" of text or code. Used by Cursor/Copilot for similarity search. Entity A code element that becomes a node in the graph: a function, class, method, or file. GDS (Graph Data Science) Neo4j's plugin library providing graph algorithms (PageRank, PPR, community detection, etc.). Gold patch In SWE-bench, the actual code changes a developer made to fix a bug. The "correct answer" for evaluation. Graph projection An in-memory copy of part of the Neo4j graph, optimized for running algorithms. Created before running PPR. IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) A weighting technique giving lower weight to terms/nodes that appear frequently (they're less informative). Import resolution Determining which actual file a Python import statement refers to. MCP (Model Context Protocol) An open standard by Anthropic for connecting AI applications to external tools. Like USB-C for AI tools. MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) Evaluation metric: average of (1 / rank of first correct result) across all queries. Node An entity in a graph. In this project: a File, Class, Function, or Method. PPR (Personalized PageRank) A graph algorithm ranking nodes by relevance to specific seed nodes, using a random walk with restart. Qualified name The full unique identifier for a code entity (e.g., src.services.auth.AuthService.authenticate). Recall@k Evaluation metric: what fraction of all correct answers appeared in the top k results. Seed node The starting point for PPR. The node(s) you're computing relevance relative to. SWE-bench A benchmark of real GitHub bug reports with known fixes, used to evaluate code retrieval and fixing tools. Token A unit of text for LLMs. Roughly ¾ of a word. A "token budget" limits how much text you send. tree-sitter An open-source parser library converting source code into syntax trees. Fast, supports 100+ languages.

docs.title: CodeGraph: Implementation Plan for Remaining Stages
Context
docs.text:
What's done: Stages 1-3 (parsing, resolution, Neo4j graph, PPR) are fully implemented and tested (144 tests). The system can parse Python repos, build dependency graphs in Neo4j, and run Personalized PageRank.

What's missing: The "brain" that connects the task description to the graph (seed selection), the quality refinements (IDF + token budgeting), and the interface layer (MCP server). Without these, the system has all the components but no way for an AI agent to use them end-to-end.

Goal: Implement stages 4-5 so the full pipeline works: task description -> seed extraction -> PPR -> post-processing -> formatted source code exposed via MCP.

docs.title: The thinking
docs.text:
PPR needs starting nodes ("seeds"). The quality of seeds determines the quality of results. The question is: given a task like "fix the authentication timeout bug", how do we pick the right graph nodes to start from?

Three signals, ranked by confidence:

  1. Explicit entity matching (HIGH) — If the task mentions "AuthService" or "authenticate", match those directly against node names in the graph. This is the most reliable signal because the agent explicitly told us what matters.

  2. BM25 keyword search (MEDIUM) — Search the task text against function signatures and docstrings stored in Neo4j. BM25 is a standard text-ranking algorithm (like a smarter ctrl+F). It finds functions whose descriptions are textually similar to the task, even when no exact entity name is mentioned.

  3. Current file hint (LOW) — If the agent says "I'm currently in auth_service.py", add entities from that file as weak seeds. Low weight because "where the cursor is" does not always equal "what matters".

These signals are combined into a personalization vector — a dictionary mapping node IDs to weights that sum to 1.0 — which PPR uses as its teleportation distribution.

docs.title: New module: src/retrieval/seed_selection.py
docs.text:
Why a new retrieval/ package? Seed selection and post-processing are about retrieving relevant context from the graph. They sit between the graph layer (storage/algorithms) and the MCP layer (interface). Grouping them in src/retrieval/ keeps each module single-purpose per CLAUDE.md guidelines.

Data model:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SeedNode:
    """A single seed with its source signal and raw weight."""
    node_id: int
    qualified_name: str
    weight: float
    source: str  # "entity_match", "bm25", "current_file"

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PersonalizationVector:
    """Normalized seed weights ready for PPR."""
    seeds: dict[int, float]  # node_id -> normalized weight (sums to 1.0)

Functions:

extract_seeds(
    driver, task_description, mentioned_entities, current_file,
    signal_weights
) -> PersonalizationVector

_match_entities(driver, mentioned_entities) -> list[SeedNode]
_bm25_search(driver, task_description, top_n) -> list[SeedNode]
_current_file_seeds(driver, current_file) -> list[SeedNode]
_normalize_seeds(all_seeds) -> PersonalizationVector

Dependencies to add: rank-bm25 (already listed in CLAUDE.md tech stack but not in pyproject.toml yet).

How BM25 works here: We fetch all Function/Method nodes with their signature and docstring from Neo4j, build a BM25 index in memory, query it with the task description tokens, and take the top N results. This is fast (< 50ms for 10k nodes) and doesn't require any external service.

docs.title: Config additions to config.yaml:
docs.text:
seed_selection:
  entity_match_weight: 0.6    # weight multiplier for exact entity matches
  bm25_weight: 0.3            # weight multiplier for BM25 results
  current_file_weight: 0.1    # weight multiplier for current file entities
  bm25_top_n: 5               # how many BM25 results to include

docs.title: Test file: tests/test_seed_selection.py
docs.text:
• Test entity matching: known names resolve to correct node IDs
  • Test BM25: task description "fix authentication" ranks auth-related functions higher
  • Test current file: entities from specified file appear as seeds
  • Test normalization: weights sum to 1.0
  • Test graceful degradation: empty mentioned_entities still produces seeds from BM25
  • Uses the user_auth fixture graph (requires Neo4j)

docs.title: The thinking
docs.text:
PPR returns raw scores, but two problems remain:

Problem 1: Utility functions bubble up. A function like log_error() called by 200 others appears highly ranked for any seed because it has so many incoming edges. It's structurally important but not task-relevant.

Solution: IDF-inspired edge reweighting. Before running PPR, adjust edge weights based on the target node's in-degree:

adjusted_weight = base_weight / log2(in_degree + 2)

The +2 prevents division by zero and keeps single-connection nodes nearly unchanged. Functions with many callers get heavily downweighted. This is applied when creating the GDS projection — we update the weight property on edges in Neo4j before projecting.

Problem 2: Token budget. PPR returns node names and scores, but the AI agent needs actual source code. We can't send everything — we need to stay within a token budget (4,000-8,000 tokens).

Solution: Walk the ranked results top-down, read source code for each, accumulate until the budget is reached.

docs.title: New module: src/retrieval/post_processing.py
docs.text:
Functions:

apply_idf_weights(driver) -> int
    # Updates edge weights in Neo4j based on target node in-degree.
    # Returns the number of edges updated.
    # Must be called BEFORE project_graph() in the pipeline.

format_context(
    ppr_results, project_root, token_budget
) -> list[ContextResult]
    # Reads source code for each PPR result, accumulates until budget.

count_tokens(text) -> int
    # Simple whitespace-based token estimation (len(text.split())).
    # No need for tiktoken — rough estimate is sufficient.

Data model:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ContextResult:
    """A single code entity with its source code, ready to serve."""
    entity_name: str
    qualified_name: str
    file_path: str
    line_start: int
    line_end: int
    relevance_score: float
    source_code: str
    token_count: int

docs.title: Why whitespace tokenization is fine
docs.text: The doc suggests 4,000-8,000 tokens. We're using this as a rough budget, not billing. len(text.split()) correlates ~0.75x with actual LLM tokens. Close enough for budgeting — and zero dependencies.

docs.title: Test file: tests/test_post_processing.py
docs.text:
• Test IDF: high in-degree nodes get lower edge weights
  • Test token counting: basic correctness
  • Test format_context: respects budget, returns results in score order
  • Test edge case: budget smaller than first result still returns one result
  • IDF tests require Neo4j; token counting/formatting tests are pure functions (no Neo4j)

docs.title: The thinking
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is how AI agents discover and call tools. Our server exposes three tools:

  1. get_relevant_context — The main tool. Full pipeline: seeds -> PPR -> post-processing -> source code. This is what the agent calls when it needs to understand code relevant to a task.

  2. query_dependencies — A simpler tool. Given an entity name, return its direct dependencies (what it calls, what calls it). No PPR — just raw graph traversal. Useful for focused exploration.

  3. get_graph_stats — Diagnostic. Returns node counts, edge counts, most connected files. Helps the agent understand the codebase shape.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The MCP server is a single-file entry point (src/mcp_server.py) that: 1. On startup: loads config, connects to Neo4j, verifies connectivity 2. On first get_relevant_context call: applies IDF weights and creates GDS projection (lazy, since the graph may not be built yet) 3. On tool call: runs the pipeline and returns formatted JSON

Why single file? The MCP server is thin glue — it calls into retrieval/ and graph/. Each tool handler is ~15-20 lines. No need for a package.

docs.title: Tool signatures:
docs.text:
@mcp.tool()
async def get_relevant_context(
    task_description: str,
    mentioned_entities: list[str] | None = None,
    current_file: str | None = None,
    top_k: int = 15,
    token_budget: int = 6000,
) -> str:
    """Find structurally relevant code for a task using graph-based ranking."""

@mcp.tool()
async def query_dependencies(
    entity_name: str,
    direction: str = "both",
    depth: int = 1,
) -> str:
    """Get direct dependencies for a code entity."""

@mcp.tool()
async def get_graph_stats() -> str:
    """Return statistics about the code dependency graph."""

docs.title: Dependencies to add: mcp (Python SDK, already in CLAUDE.md tech stack)
Config additions to config.yaml:
docs.text:
seed_selection:
  entity_match_weight: 0.6
  bm25_weight: 0.3
  current_file_weight: 0.1
  bm25_top_n: 5

mcp:
  server_name: "codegraph"
  default_token_budget: 6000
  default_top_k: 15

docs.title: Test file: tests/test_mcp_server.py
docs.text:
• Test tool registration: all 3 tools are registered
  • Test get_relevant_context: returns valid JSON with source code
  • Test query_dependencies: returns correct neighbors
  • Test get_graph_stats: returns correct counts
  • Tests use the user_auth fixture graph

docs.title: The thinking
docs.text:
The MCP server shouldn't contain pipeline logic. A separate pipeline.py orchestrates the full flow so it can be tested independently of MCP.

def run_retrieval_pipeline(
    driver, gds, task_description, mentioned_entities,
    current_file, ppr_config, token_budget, project_root,
) -> list[ContextResult]:
    """Full pipeline: seed extraction -> PPR -> post-processing."""
    seeds = extract_seeds(driver, task_description, mentioned_entities, current_file)
    ppr_results = run_ppr_from_node_ids(gds, driver, list(seeds.seeds.keys()), ppr_config)
    return format_context(ppr_results, project_root, token_budget)

This function is what the MCP tool calls. It's also what the evaluation harness will call.

docs.title: What's NOT in this plan
docs.text:
• Evaluation (Stage 6) — SWE-bench benchmarking. This is a separate effort that should come after the pipeline is stable. It involves cloning repos, running the pipeline on 300 bug reports, computing metrics. Worth its own plan.
  • REST API fallback — Mentioned in docs but not essential. MCP is the primary interface.
  • CLI entry point — A python -m codegraph command for building graphs from CLI. Nice to have but not blocking.

docs.title: Graph-Based Context Selection for AI Coding Agents
Project Reference Documentation
docs.text: This document explains every part of the thesis project from the ground up. It is written to be a reference you can come back to at any point during development. Every technical term is explained when first introduced. Every design decision includes the reasoning behind it.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. What This Project Is
  2. The Problem: How AI Coding Tools Pick Context Today
  3. The Core Idea: Code Is a Graph, Not Text
  4. System Overview: The Full Pipeline
  5. Component 1: Parsing Code with tree-sitter
  6. Component 2: Entity Extraction
  7. Component 3: Import Resolution
  8. Component 4: Call Resolution
  9. Component 5: Building the Graph in Neo4j
  10. Component 6: Personalized PageRank (PPR)
  11. Component 7: Seed Node Selection
  12. Component 8: Post-Processing the Results
  13. Component 9: The MCP Server
  14. How Evaluation Works
  15. Research Papers This Project Builds On
  16. Known Limitations
  17. Glossary

docs.title: 1. What This Project Is
docs.text:
This project builds a tool that helps AI coding assistants (like Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) find the right code context when working on a task.

When an AI assistant needs to fix a bug or add a feature, it needs to "see" the relevant parts of the codebase. Today's tools find relevant code by searching for textually similar code — like googling for keywords. This project replaces that with structural search — understanding how code is actually connected through function calls, imports, and inheritance, then using those connections to find what's relevant.

The output is an MCP server — a standardized plugin that any AI coding agent can connect to and ask: "Given this task, what code should I look at?"

In one sentence: Parse a codebase into a graph of dependencies, use a graph algorithm (Personalized PageRank) to find structurally relevant code given a task, and expose this through a standard protocol any AI agent can use.

docs.title: 2. The Problem: How AI Coding Tools Pick Context Today
What "context" means
docs.text:
When an AI assistant works on your code, it can't see your entire codebase at once. It has a limited window (called a context window — the amount of text the AI can process at once, typically 100,000-200,000 tokens). A medium-sized project might have 500,000+ tokens of code. So the AI has to choose: which parts of the codebase are worth "seeing" for this particular task?

This selection process is called context selection or context retrieval. The quality of the AI's output depends heavily on whether it sees the right code.

docs.title: How current tools do it
docs.text:
Cursor and GitHub Copilot use a technique called embedding-based retrieval. Here's how it works in plain terms:

  1. Every chunk of code gets converted into a list of numbers (a "vector" or "embedding") that represents its meaning. Think of it like a fingerprint for each piece of code.
  2. When you ask a question or edit code, your question also gets converted into a similar list of numbers.
  3. The tool finds code chunks whose number-lists are most similar to your question's number-list.
  4. Those "similar" chunks become the context the AI sees.

This is essentially keyword search on steroids — it finds code that talks about similar things as your question.

Claude Code takes a different approach: it doesn't pre-build an index at all. Instead, it uses tools (like grep and file reading) to search the codebase on-the-fly, deciding what to look at as it goes. This avoids stale indexes but relies on the AI making good search decisions.

docs.title: Why this approach fails for code
docs.text:
Code is not like a book or a Wikipedia article. Consider this scenario:

You need to fix a bug in authenticate(). This function calls hash_password(), which uses BCryptHasher, which inherits from BaseHasher. The BaseHasher class is defined in a completely different file with completely different variable names and completely different comments.

Embedding-based search will miss BaseHasher because it looks nothing like authenticate() textually. But structurally, it's critically important — if you change how hashing works, BaseHasher is part of the chain.

The fundamental issue: embeddings find code that looks similar in text, but code relevance is determined by structural connections — what calls what, what imports what, what inherits from what. These connections form a graph, and a graph requires graph-based search, not text-based search.

A Google DeepMind paper from August 2025 formally proved that embedding-based retrieval has mathematical limitations: there exist relevant documents that cannot be retrieved by any query in an embedding space. This is a theoretical ceiling, not just a practical one.

Five major papers from top conferences in 2024-2025 (NAACL, ICLR, ACL, ASE) independently showed that using code's graph structure for retrieval substantially outperforms text-based approaches, with improvements ranging from +3% to +33%.

docs.title: 3. The Core Idea: Code Is a Graph, Not Text
What "code is a graph" means
docs.text:
A graph is a data structure with two things: nodes (things) and edges (connections between things). Think of a social network: people are nodes, friendships are edges.

Code has the same structure:

  • Nodes are code entities: functions, classes, files, methods
  • Edges are relationships between them: function A calls function B, file X imports file Y, class C inherits from class D

For example, in a web application:

  [app.py] ---IMPORTS---> [auth_service.py] ---IMPORTS---> [user_model.py]
      |                         |                              |
  has function             has class                      has class
      |                         |                              |
  [main()]               [AuthService]                    [User]
      |                     |       |                         |
   CALLS              has method   has method             INHERITS_FROM
      |                  |              |                      |
      +--------> [authenticate()]  [refresh_token()]     [BaseModel]
                       |
                    CALLS
                       |
                 [hash_password()]

This is a code dependency graph. Every codebase has one, whether or not anyone has explicitly built it. The relationships exist in the source code — you just have to extract them.

docs.title: Why graph search is better than text search for code
docs.text:
When you ask "what code is relevant to fixing a bug in authenticate()?", a graph can follow the connections:

  • authenticate() calls hash_password() → probably relevant
  • authenticate() is in AuthService which inherits from BaseService → might need to check
  • process_login() calls authenticate() → will be affected by your change
  • user_model.py is imported by auth_service.py → the User class is probably involved

Text search would only find code that contains words like "authenticate", "login", "password". The graph finds code that is structurally connected regardless of what words it uses.

docs.title: The specific algorithm: Personalized PageRank
docs.text:
To rank which connected nodes are most relevant, this project uses Personalized PageRank (PPR). Full explanation in Component 6, but the intuition is:

Imagine dropping paint at a starting node and watching it spread through the edges. Nearby, well-connected nodes get a lot of paint (high relevance score). Distant, weakly-connected nodes get little paint (low relevance score). PPR computes exactly this — a relevance score for every node in the graph relative to your starting point.

docs.title: 4. System Overview: The Full Pipeline
docs.text:
The system has four stages. Each stage takes input from the previous one:

STAGE 1: PARSE
  Input:  A Python repository (source code files)
  Action: Parse each file into a syntax tree using tree-sitter
  Output: A syntax tree per file (structured representation of the code)

    │
    ▼

STAGE 2: EXTRACT & RESOLVE
  Input:  Syntax trees from Stage 1
  Action: Extract entities (functions, classes, imports) from each tree,
          then resolve cross-file connections (which import points to which file,
          which function call refers to which function definition)
  Output: A list of nodes (entities) and edges (relationships)

    │
    ▼

STAGE 3: BUILD GRAPH & RUN PPR
  Input:  Nodes and edges from Stage 2
  Action: Load into Neo4j graph database, create in-memory projection,
          run Personalized PageRank from seed nodes
  Output: A ranked list of code entities with relevance scores

    │
    ▼

STAGE 4: SERVE
  Input:  The graph database (persistent) + the PPR algorithm (on-demand)
  Action: Expose as an MCP server that AI agents can call
  Output: When an agent asks "what code is relevant to this task?",
          return the top-k most relevant code snippets with their source

Each stage is explained in detail in the following sections.

docs.title: What tree-sitter is
docs.text:
tree-sitter is a parser library. A parser reads source code (which is just text) and converts it into a structured tree that represents what the code means syntactically. This structured tree is called an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).

Analogy: Parsing is like diagramming a sentence in English class. The sentence "The dog chased the cat" is just a string of characters. A diagram shows its structure: subject ("The dog"), verb ("chased"), object ("the cat"). Similarly, an AST shows code structure: this is a function definition, that's a function call, this is an if-statement.

docs.title: What tree-sitter produces
docs.text:
Given this Python code:

def authenticate(username, password):
    user = find_user(username)
    if user and check_password(user, password):
        return create_token(user)
    return None

tree-sitter produces a tree like:

function_definition
  name: "authenticate"
  parameters: ["username", "password"]
  body:
    assignment
      left: "user"
      right: call(name: "find_user", args: ["username"])
    if_statement
      condition: ...
      body:
        return_statement
          value: call(name: "create_token", args: ["user"])
    return_statement
      value: None

This tree tells us: there's a function called authenticate, it calls find_user, check_password, and create_token.

docs.title: Why tree-sitter and not something else
docs.text:
• Fast: Parses ~5-10ms per 1,000 lines of code. A 100,000-line codebase takes under 2 seconds.
  • Supports 100+ languages: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Rust, and more.
  • Industry standard: Used by GitHub, Cursor, and many other tools.
  • Handles broken code: Doesn't crash on syntax errors — marks the broken parts and keeps going.

docs.title: What tree-sitter cannot do
docs.text:
This is critical to understand:

  • Cannot resolve imports: Sees from utils import helper as text, but doesn't know which file utils refers to.
  • Cannot resolve types: Sees obj.method() but doesn't know which class obj is an instance of.
  • Cannot look across files: Only parses one file at a time. Connecting information across files is our responsibility.

Tree-sitter gives us the raw material (what entities exist in each file). The next components connect them across files.

docs.title: How tree-sitter is used in this project
docs.text:
For each .py file in the repository:

import tree_sitter_python as tspython
from tree_sitter import Language, Parser

# Create a Python parser
parser = Parser(Language(tspython.language()))

# Read the source code
with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
    source = f.read()

# Parse into a syntax tree
tree = parser.parse(source)

# Now tree.root_node gives access to the entire AST

tree-sitter also provides a query language that lets you search for specific patterns in the tree. For example, to find all function definitions:

(function_definition name: (identifier) @function.name)

This says: "find every function_definition node and capture its name child." The @function.name part is like naming a capture group in a regex — it lets you extract the matched piece.

docs.title: What entity extraction is
docs.text: After tree-sitter gives us a syntax tree for each file, we walk through it and pull out the entities we care about. An entity is any code element that will become a node in our graph.

docs.title: What we extract
docs.text:
For each Python file, we extract:

Functions (standalone, not inside a class): 

def validate_email(email):  # ← this becomes a Function node
    ...
Captured properties: name, file_path, line_number, end_line, signature (the parameter list), docstring (if present)

Classes: 

class AuthService(BaseService):  # ← this becomes a Class node
    ...                           #    with an INHERITS_FROM edge to BaseService
Captured properties: name, file_path, line_number, end_line, bases (parent classes)

Methods (functions inside a class): 

class AuthService:
    def authenticate(self, username, password):  # ← this becomes a Method node
        ...
Captured properties: same as Functions, plus class_name (which class it belongs to)

Import statements: 

from src.utils.helpers import validate_email  # ← records: this file imports validate_email from src.utils.helpers
import json                                    # ← records: this file imports json (standard library, will be skipped)
Captured: module_path ("src.utils.helpers"), imported_names (["validate_email"]), is_relative (True/False)

Function calls (inside function/method bodies): 

def authenticate(self, username, password):
    user = find_user(username)          # ← records: authenticate calls find_user
    if check_password(user, password):  # ← records: authenticate calls check_password
        return create_token(user)       # ← records: authenticate calls create_token
Captured: caller_name, callee_name, line_number

docs.title: What we skip (and why)
docs.text:
• Standard library imports (import json, import os): These are part of Python, not the project. Including them would add noise without value — every file imports os, and that tells us nothing about the project's structure.
  • Third-party library calls (requests.get(), pandas.DataFrame()): Same reason. We only care about connections within the project's own code.
  • Variables and constants: Not included as nodes for simplicity. Functions and classes capture the meaningful structural relationships. Variables could be added later but would dramatically increase graph size with questionable benefit.
  • Decorators, type annotations, comments: Not extracted as separate entities, though decorators on functions/classes are noted as properties.

docs.title: How entity extraction works in practice
docs.text:
This is a two-step process:

Step 1: Walk the syntax tree and collect raw entities from each file.

For each file, the extractor produces a data structure like:

# Result of extracting from auth_service.py
{
    "file": "src/services/auth_service.py",
    "classes": [
        {"name": "AuthService", "line": 10, "end_line": 85, "bases": ["BaseService"]},
    ],
    "functions": [],  # no standalone functions in this file
    "methods": [
        {"name": "authenticate", "class": "AuthService", "line": 12, "end_line": 30,
         "signature": "(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool"},
        {"name": "refresh_token", "class": "AuthService", "line": 32, "end_line": 50,
         "signature": "(self, token: str) -> str"},
    ],
    "imports": [
        {"module": "src.models.user", "names": ["User"], "is_relative": False},
        {"module": "src.utils.crypto", "names": ["hash_password"], "is_relative": False},
    ],
    "calls": [
        {"caller": "AuthService.authenticate", "callee": "hash_password", "line": 22},
        {"caller": "AuthService.authenticate", "callee": "User.find_by_username", "line": 18},
    ],
}

Step 2: After processing ALL files, resolve cross-file relationships.

The raw extraction says authenticate calls hash_password, but we need to know: which file is hash_password defined in? This is where import resolution and call resolution come in (next sections).

docs.title: Qualified names
docs.text:
To uniquely identify entities across the entire codebase, we use qualified names — the full path to an entity:

  • A function validate_email in src/utils/helpers.py → src.utils.helpers.validate_email
  • A method authenticate on class AuthService in src/services/auth_service.py → src.services.auth_service.AuthService.authenticate
  • A class User in src/models/user.py → src.models.user.User

This prevents confusion when two different files have functions with the same name (e.g., multiple files might have a validate() function).

docs.title: What the problem is
docs.text:
When tree-sitter parses from src.utils.helpers import validate_email, it gives us the string "src.utils.helpers" and the name "validate_email". But tree-sitter does not tell us which actual file on disk "src.utils.helpers" refers to. It only looks at one file at a time and doesn't know about the filesystem.

Import resolution is the process of turning the string "src.utils.helpers" into the actual file path /project/src/utils/helpers.py.

Without this step, we can't create IMPORTS edges in our graph because we don't know which files are connected.

docs.title: How Python imports work (simplified)
docs.text:
When Python sees from src.utils.helpers import validate_email, it:

  1. Takes the dotted path src.utils.helpers
  2. Converts dots to directory separators: src/utils/helpers
  3. Looks for either:
  4. A file: src/utils/helpers.py
  5. A package: src/utils/helpers/__init__.py
  6. Inside that file, looks for something named validate_email

Relative imports start with dots: from ..models import User. Each dot means "go up one directory." So in src/services/auth_service.py, ..models means "go up from services, then into models" → src/models.

docs.title: The categories of difficulty
docs.text:
Easy cases (~80% of real imports in well-structured projects):

from src.utils.helpers import validate_email
# Resolution: find src/utils/helpers.py in the project → done

This is just converting dots to slashes and checking if the file exists. Straightforward string manipulation + filesystem check.

Medium cases (~15%):

from ..models import User
# Resolution: need to know the current file's location,
# count the dots, go up directories, find models.py or models/__init__.py

Relative imports require knowing the current file's package context. If you're in src/services/auth.py, .. means src/, so ..models = src/models.

Another medium case is re-exports through __init__.py: 

# src/models/__init__.py contains:
from .user import User
from .post import Post

# So "from src.models import User" actually resolves to src/models/user.py

To fully resolve this, you'd need to read __init__.py and follow the chain. For the initial implementation, resolving to the __init__.py file itself is acceptable — the connection between src/models/ and the importing file is still captured.

Impossible cases (~5%, skip these):

module = importlib.import_module(config.module_name)   # runtime string — impossible to resolve statically
exec("from " + module_name + " import something")      # generated code — impossible

These depend on values only known at runtime. No static analysis tool can resolve them. We log them as unresolved and move on.

docs.title: The implementation approach
docs.text:
Build a custom resolver (~150-200 lines of Python) that handles the easy and medium cases:

def resolve_import(import_path: str, current_file: str, project_root: str) -> str | None:
    """
