    Turn a dotted import path into an actual file path.

    'src.utils.helpers' → '/project/src/utils/helpers.py'
    Returns None if the import can't be resolved (logged as unresolved).
    """
    if import_path.startswith('.'):
        # Relative import: count leading dots, resolve relative to current file
        dots = len(import_path) - len(import_path.lstrip('.'))
        remaining = import_path.lstrip('.')
        # Go up 'dots' directories from current file's directory
        base_dir = current_file_directory
        for _ in range(dots):
            base_dir = parent(base_dir)
        relative_path = remaining.replace('.', '/')
    else:
        # Absolute import: resolve from project root
        relative_path = import_path.replace('.', '/')
        base_dir = project_root

    # Try as a module file (src/utils/helpers.py)
    candidate = base_dir / (relative_path + '.py')
    if exists(candidate):
        return candidate

    # Try as a package (src/utils/helpers/__init__.py)
    candidate = base_dir / relative_path / '__init__.py'
    if exists(candidate):
        return candidate

    return None  # Unresolved — log it and continue

Why build a custom resolver instead of using an existing tool like jedi:

The custom resolver is educational (good for thesis) and sufficient for the well-structured repos in SWE-bench (Flask, Django, scikit-learn). If the custom resolver proves too limited (resolving less than ~75% of imports), jedi can be added as a fallback. jedi is a Python library (pip install jedi) that can resolve imports with high accuracy because it understands Python's full import system.

What we report: The import resolution rate (e.g., "resolved 87% of 610 imports in Flask"). This is a useful metric to include in the thesis — it shows honesty about limitations.

docs.title: What the problem is
docs.text: Entity extraction tells us: "function authenticate calls something named hash_password". But which hash_password? There might be multiple functions with that name across the codebase. We need to figure out which specific function definition is being called.

docs.title: How call resolution works
docs.text:
Call resolution connects call sites (places where a function is called) to function definitions (where the function is defined). This uses the information from import resolution:

# In auth_service.py:
from src.utils.crypto import hash_password    # ← import resolution told us this points to src/utils/crypto.py

def authenticate(self, username, password):
    hashed = hash_password(password)           # ← call resolution: "hash_password" was imported from
                                               #   src/utils/crypto.py, so this call resolves to
                                               #   src.utils.crypto.hash_password

The resolution logic:

  1. When we see a call to some_function() inside a file, check:
  2. Was some_function imported from another file? → Resolve to that file's definition.
  3. Is some_function defined in the same file? → Resolve to the local definition.
  4. Is some_function a method call on self? (like self.validate()) → Resolve to the method on the same class.

  5. Can't determine? → Log as unresolved.

  6. For method calls on objects (like user.save()), the challenge is knowing what type user is. Without type inference, we can use heuristics:

  7. If user was assigned from User.find_by_id(...), we can guess it's a User instance.
  8. If type hints are present (user: User = ...), we can use those.
  9. If neither, log as unresolved.

docs.title: What gets created from call resolution
docs.text:
Each resolved call becomes a CALLS edge in the graph:

authenticate() ──CALLS──▶ hash_password()
authenticate() ──CALLS──▶ User.find_by_username()
process_login() ──CALLS──▶ authenticate()

Unresolved calls are counted and reported. For Python projects with good structure and type hints, expect 60-80% resolution. This is acceptable — even partial call graphs are valuable for PPR because the algorithm works with incomplete graphs (it just won't find connections through the missing edges).

docs.title: 9. Component 5: Building the Graph in Neo4j
Why Neo4j
docs.text:
Neo4j is a graph database — a database specifically designed to store and query graph-structured data (nodes and relationships). It's the natural fit for this project because:

  • Our data is a graph (code entities with relationships).
  • We need graph algorithms (PPR) — Neo4j has a built-in Graph Data Science (GDS) library with PPR already implemented.
  • It supports fast graph queries via its query language (Cypher).
  • Free: Neo4j Desktop provides a free Enterprise license for development.
  • The CodexGraph paper (NAACL 2025) used Neo4j for the same kind of code graph, validating this choice.

docs.title: The graph schema
docs.text:
Nodes (4 types):

Node type What it represents Example Key properties File A .py source file auth_service.py file_path, name, line_count Class A class definition AuthService name, file_path, line_number, end_line Function A standalone function (not in a class) validate_email() name, file_path, line_number, end_line, signature Method A function inside a class AuthService.authenticate() name, file_path, line_number, signature, class_name

Edges (4 types):

Edge type Connects Meaning Example CONTAINS File → Class, Class → Method "This file defines this class" or "This class has this method" auth_service.py CONTAINS AuthService IMPORTS File → File "This file imports from that file" auth_service.py IMPORTS user_model.py CALLS Function/Method → Function/Method "This function calls that function" authenticate() CALLS hash_password() INHERITS_FROM Class → Class "This class extends that class" AuthService INHERITS_FROM BaseService

Why these 4 edge types and not more:

This schema follows LocAgent (ACL 2025), which uses 4 entity types and 4 relation types and achieved the best results among the papers we reviewed. The key insight from that paper: you need enough relationship types to capture the important structural connections, but adding too many types adds noise without improving results. CONTAINS, IMPORTS, CALLS, and INHERITS_FROM capture the vast majority of meaningful structural relationships in a Python codebase.

docs.title: Loading data into Neo4j
docs.text:
After entity extraction and resolution, we have lists of nodes and edges. These get loaded into Neo4j using batched operations (loading many records at once, not one at a time):

# Loading function nodes in a batch (fast — thousands per second)
tx.run("""
    UNWIND $nodes AS node
    MERGE (n:Function {qualified_name: node.qualified_name})
    SET n.name = node.name,
        n.file_path = node.file_path,
        n.line_number = node.line_number,
        n.end_line = node.end_line,
        n.signature = node.signature
""", nodes=function_list)

MERGE means "create this node if it doesn't exist, or match it if it does." This makes the operation safe to run multiple times (idempotent).

Performance: Loading 10,000-50,000 nodes with batched operations takes 2-15 seconds. For the thesis-sized repositories (Flask: ~5,000 LOC, Django: ~300,000 LOC), this is fast enough.

docs.title: The GDS graph projection
docs.text:
Before running algorithms, Neo4j's Graph Data Science (GDS) library needs an in-memory projection — a copy of the graph loaded into RAM for fast computation. This is created with:

CALL gds.graph.project(
  'codeGraph',                                    -- name for this projection
  ['Function', 'Method', 'Class', 'File'],        -- which node types to include
  {
    CALLS:         {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    IMPORTS:       {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    INHERITS_FROM: {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    CONTAINS:      {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'}
  }
)

Why UNDIRECTED: Explained in Component 6 — Directionality.

Memory usage: A 50,000-node graph with 200,000 edges needs approximately 5.2 MB in-memory. Trivial by modern laptop standards.

docs.title: 10. Component 6: Personalized PageRank (PPR)
docs.text: This is the core algorithm of the project. This section explains it from scratch — no prior knowledge assumed.

docs.title: Starting with regular PageRank
docs.text:
PageRank was invented by Larry Page and Sergey Brin (the founders of Google) in 1998 to rank web pages. The idea:

Imagine a person randomly browsing the web. They start on a random page, then randomly click one of the links on that page, then randomly click a link on the next page, and so on. The pages they visit most often are the most "important" ones.

Why? Because important pages have many incoming links from other important pages. If lots of pages link to Page X, the random surfer will end up at Page X frequently.

There's one catch: what if the surfer reaches a page with no outgoing links, or gets stuck in a loop? To handle this, with some probability (typically 15%), the surfer "teleports" — jumps to a completely random page instead of following a link. This ensures they eventually visit every page.

The result of PageRank is a score for every page representing how often the random surfer visits it. Higher score = more important.

docs.title: What makes Personalized PageRank different
docs.text:
In regular PageRank, when the surfer teleports, they jump to any random page with equal probability. This produces a global importance ranking.

In Personalized PageRank (PPR), when the surfer teleports, they jump back to specific pages you choose — called seed nodes. This biases the walk toward a particular region of the graph.

Analogy: Regular PageRank asks "what are the most important pages on the entire web?" PPR asks "what are the most important pages relative to this specific page?"

For code: Regular PageRank would tell you the most important functions in the entire codebase (useful but not task-specific). PPR from authenticate() tells you the most important functions relative to authenticate() — the ones most structurally connected to it.

docs.title: How PPR works step by step
docs.text:
Given a graph and a seed node (e.g., authenticate()):

  1. Start at the seed. Place all "probability mass" (think of it as 1.0 units of paint) on the seed node.

  2. Walk step: From each node that has paint, spread the paint along outgoing edges. If a node has 3 outgoing edges, each edge gets 1/3 of the paint. But only spread (1 - α) fraction of the paint — keep α fraction at the seed.

  3. α (alpha) is called the "restart probability" or "teleportation probability." Typically 0.15 (15%). It controls how far the paint spreads from the seed. Higher α = paint stays closer to seed. Lower α = paint spreads further.

  4. Repeat steps 1-2 many times (typically 20 iterations). The paint distribution converges — it stops changing significantly.

  5. Read the scores. The amount of paint at each node is its PPR score = its relevance to the seed.

Concrete example:

Seed: authenticate()

After PPR:
  authenticate()      → 0.150  (high — it's the seed itself)
  hash_password()     → 0.082  (high — directly called by seed)
  check_token()       → 0.067  (high — directly called by seed)
  User.find_by_name() → 0.045  (medium — called by seed)
  BaseService.init()  → 0.023  (medium — inherited by seed's class)
  process_login()     → 0.018  (medium — calls the seed)
  log_error()         → 0.012  (lower — but still connected, see "utility function problem" below)
  send_email()        → 0.001  (very low — barely connected)

The top-ranked results are the code entities most structurally relevant to authenticate().

docs.title: The key parameters
docs.text:
Parameter What it controls Default Effect of increasing α (alpha) or restart probability How close results stay to the seed 0.15 Higher → results cluster more tightly around seed, fewer distant results maxIterations How many times the algorithm repeats 20 Higher → more precise scores (diminishing returns after ~20) tolerance When to stop early if scores stop changing 1e-7 Lower → more precise but slower (irrelevant at thesis scale) top-k How many results to return 10-20 Higher → more context but more noise and more tokens used

In Neo4j GDS, the dampingFactor parameter is (1 - α). So dampingFactor = 0.85 means α = 0.15 — a 15% chance of teleporting back to the seed at each step.

docs.title: Why PPR is the right algorithm for this problem
docs.text:
Compared to simpler approaches:

Approach How it works Limitation 1-hop neighbors Return everything directly connected to the seed Misses indirect but important connections (A→B→C — C is missed) k-hop ego-graph (what RepoGraph uses) Return everything within k edges of the seed k=1 is too narrow; k=2 explodes in size (too many results); no ranking within results Shortest path Find entities closest to seed by edge count Doesn't account for multiple paths or connection strength PPR (this project) Probabilistic relevance that considers all paths, multiple connections, and connection strength Slightly more complex to implement — but Neo4j GDS handles it

PPR's advantages: - Multi-hop: Finds indirectly connected but important code. - Ranked: Every node gets a score, so you can take exactly the top-k you need. - Multiple paths matter: If node X is connected to the seed through 5 different paths, it scores higher than node Y connected through only 1 path. This reflects real code coupling. - Mathematically principled: Guaranteed to converge to a unique solution. Not a heuristic — it's provably correct. - Fast: 1-10 milliseconds on 50,000-node graphs. Faster than the network roundtrip to the database.

docs.title: Directionality: which direction should PPR walk?
docs.text:
Edges in the code graph have a direction. authenticate() CALLS hash_password() is an edge from authenticate to hash_password.

When PPR walks from the seed, which direction does it follow?

Direction What it means What PPR finds Example Natural (follow edge direction) Walk from caller to callee Things the seed depends on (downstream) "authenticate calls hash_password" → finds hash_password Reverse (follow edges backward) Walk from callee to caller Things that depend on the seed (upstream) "process_login calls authenticate" → finds process_login Undirected (follow both ways) Walk in both directions Everything structurally connected Finds both hash_password AND process_login

Decision: UNDIRECTED as the default. Reason: for context selection, you want to see both what a function uses (so you understand it) and what uses it (so you know what might break). An AI agent fixing authenticate() needs to see hash_password() (to understand the implementation) AND process_login() (to check if the fix affects callers).

This is configured in the GDS graph projection by setting orientation: 'UNDIRECTED' — Neo4j treats every edge as bidirectional.

In the evaluation phase, we will also test directed and reversed PPR as ablation experiments to measure which direction works best. This comparison is interesting thesis material.

docs.title: Running PPR in Neo4j
docs.text:
-- Find the seed node
MATCH (seed:Function {name: 'authenticate', file_path: 'src/services/auth_service.py'})

-- Run PPR from that seed
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codeGraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [seed],
  relationshipWeightProperty: 'weight'
})
YIELD nodeId, score

-- Return top 20 results
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS name,
       gds.util.asNode(nodeId).file_path AS file,
       score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 20

PPR can also accept multiple seed nodes with different weights:

MATCH (a:Function {name: 'authenticate'}), (b:Function {name: 'refresh_token'})
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codeGraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [a, b]
})

This distributes paint equally across both seeds. GDS also supports weighted seeds if you want to give more importance to one seed over another.

docs.title: The core question
docs.text:
PPR starts from seed nodes and spreads outward. The quality of your seeds directly determines the quality of your results. Good seeds → relevant context. Bad seeds → useless context.

The seed selection problem: how does the system decide where to start PPR?

docs.title: Why "current file" is a bad default seed
docs.text:
A naïve approach would be: "the developer is editing auth_service.py, so use all functions in that file as seeds." This fails because:

  • The developer might have been searching for an hour and landed on a random file that isn't central to their task.
  • The developer might be reading code to understand it, not editing the part they need to change.
  • The AI agent might be exploring multiple files as part of a multi-step task.

"Where the cursor is" does not reliably indicate "what the developer cares about."

docs.title: The actual approach: the AI agent provides the task context
docs.text:
The MCP server doesn't guess the seed — the AI agent calling the tool tells it what the task is. When a developer says "fix the authentication timeout bug" to Claude Code, the agent already understands the task. It passes that understanding to our tool:

@mcp.tool()
def get_relevant_context(
    task_description: str,                  # "fix the authentication timeout bug"
    mentioned_entities: list[str] = None,   # ["authenticate", "TokenExpiredError"]
    current_file: str = None                # optional low-weight hint
) -> str:

The tool then extracts seeds from this information.

docs.title: The seed extraction pipeline
docs.text:
Seeds are selected through a multi-step process, combining multiple signals with different confidence levels:

Step 1: Extract explicitly mentioned entities
  Input:  task_description = "fix the authentication timeout in AuthService"
          mentioned_entities = ["authenticate", "AuthService", "TokenExpiredError"]
  Action: Match these names against nodes in the graph
  Result: AuthService node (exact match), authenticate method (exact match)
  Weight: HIGH (these were explicitly named)

Step 2: BM25 keyword search as supplement
  Input:  task_description text searched against all function signatures and docstrings
  Action: Standard keyword matching (BM25) ranks functions by textual relevance to the task
  Result: Top 3-5 functions that mention "timeout", "token", "auth", etc.
  Weight: MEDIUM (text relevance, not as reliable as explicit mentions)

Step 3: Current file as weak hint (if provided)
  Input:  current_file = "src/services/auth_service.py"
  Action: Add functions from this file as seeds
  Weight: LOW (might be relevant, might be where the developer happened to be browsing)

Step 4: Build the personalization vector
  Action: Combine all seeds with their weights, normalize so they sum to 1.0
  Result: {AuthService.authenticate: 0.35, AuthService: 0.25,
           timeout_handler: 0.15, token_validator: 0.10,
           AuthService.refresh_token: 0.08, AuthService.logout: 0.07}
  This becomes the personalization vector for PPR.

BM25 explained: BM25 is a text search algorithm. Given a query ("authentication timeout") and a set of documents (all function signatures and docstrings in the codebase), it ranks documents by how well they match the query terms. It considers: does the document contain the query words? How many times? How long is the document compared to average? BM25 is the standard baseline in information retrieval — simple, fast, and surprisingly effective.

docs.title: Why this multi-signal approach matters
docs.text:
Different scenarios benefit from different signals:

Scenario Best signal Why Developer says "fix bug in authenticate()" Explicit entity extraction Clear function name mentioned Developer says "the login flow is broken" BM25 keyword search No specific function named, but keywords help Developer says "continue working on the auth module" Current file + BM25 Vague task, file hint helps narrow scope SWE-bench evaluation (bug report text) Entity extraction + BM25 Bug reports often mention specific classes/functions

By combining all signals with weights, the system degrades gracefully — even when one signal is weak, the others compensate.

docs.title: 12. Component 8: Post-Processing the Results
docs.text: PPR returns a ranked list of nodes with scores. Before sending these to the AI agent, two post-processing steps improve quality.

docs.title: Problem 1: The Utility Function Problem
docs.text:
What happens: A function like log_error() is called by 200 other functions across the codebase. Because it's connected to everything, PPR from any seed will rank it highly. But log_error() is not useful context for understanding a specific bug — it's generic infrastructure code.

Analogy: In Google Search, if "Wikipedia" appeared as the #1 result for every query, that would be technically correct (Wikipedia is linked from everywhere) but not helpful. In text search, this is solved by downweighting common words ("the", "is", "and"). The same idea applies to graphs.

Solution: IDF-inspired edge weight adjustment.

IDF stands for Inverse Document Frequency — a concept from text search. The idea: terms that appear in many documents are less informative than terms that appear in few documents. "The" appears everywhere → low IDF → low weight. "Authenticate" appears in few files → high IDF → high weight.

Applied to our graph: functions called by many other functions are less informative than functions called by few. So we adjust edge weights:

adjusted_weight(A → B) = base_weight(edge_type) / log2(in_degree(B) + 2)

Where in_degree(B) is the number of incoming edges to node B (how many functions call B).

Function in_degree Weight adjustment Effect log_error() 200 base / log2(202) ≈ base / 7.7 Heavily downweighted hash_password() 3 base / log2(5) ≈ base / 2.3 Slightly downweighted calculate_token_expiry() 1 base / log2(3) ≈ base / 1.6 Barely affected

This means PPR will naturally reduce the score of utility functions while preserving the scores of specific, task-relevant functions.

docs.title: Problem 2: Token budgeting
docs.text:
PPR returns nodes (function/class names with scores). The AI agent needs to see actual source code, not just names. But we can't send unlimited code — there's a token budget.

The process:

  1. Take the top-k results from PPR (e.g., top 20)
  2. For each result, read the actual source code from the file (using the stored file_path, line_number, end_line)
  3. Accumulate results until reaching the token budget (default: 4,000-8,000 tokens)
  4. Format and return
def format_context(ppr_results, token_budget=6000):
    context_parts = []
    tokens_used = 0

    for node, score in ppr_results:
        # Read the actual source code for this entity
        source_code = read_lines(node.file_path, node.line_number, node.end_line)
        source_tokens = count_tokens(source_code)

        if tokens_used + source_tokens > token_budget:
            break  # Stop — we've filled the budget

        context_parts.append({
            "entity": node.name,
            "file": node.file_path,
            "lines": f"{node.line_number}-{node.end_line}",
            "relevance_score": round(score, 4),
            "source_code": source_code
        })
        tokens_used += source_tokens

    return context_parts

Why 4,000-8,000 tokens: The AI agent has its own context window to manage (100K-200K tokens). Our context is one of many inputs. Providing 4,000-8,000 tokens of highly relevant code is more useful than 50,000 tokens of loosely relevant code — the agent can focus on what matters.

docs.title: What MCP is
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 that defines how AI applications communicate with external tools and data sources.

Analogy: Before USB, every device had its own unique connector. USB standardized this — any USB device plugs into any USB port. MCP does the same for AI tools: any MCP-compliant tool plugs into any MCP-aware AI application.

Without MCP, you'd need to build a separate integration for Claude Desktop, another for Cursor, another for Copilot. With MCP, you build one server and it works with all of them.

docs.title: How MCP works
docs.text:
MCP follows a client-server model:

  • The server (our code) provides tools — functions the AI can call.
  • The host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.) connects to the server and lets the AI use those tools.
  • Communication happens via JSON-RPC (a simple request/response protocol) over either STDIO (for local tools) or HTTP (for remote tools).

Each tool has: - A name: What the tool is called (e.g., get_relevant_context) - A description: What it does (the AI reads this to decide when to use it) - A parameter schema: What inputs it accepts (defined as JSON Schema) - A return value: What it sends back

docs.title: The tools this MCP server exposes
docs.text:
Tool 1: get_relevant_context (the primary tool)

@mcp.tool()
def get_relevant_context(
    task_description: str,
    mentioned_entities: list[str] = None,
    current_file: str = None,
    top_k: int = 15,
    token_budget: int = 6000
) -> str:
    """
    Find the most structurally relevant code for a given task.

    Uses Personalized PageRank on the code dependency graph to find
    functions, classes, and methods that are structurally connected
    to the entities involved in the task.

    Args:
        task_description: What the developer is trying to do
        mentioned_entities: Specific function/class names from the conversation
        current_file: The file currently open (optional, low-weight hint)
        top_k: Maximum number of results to return
        token_budget: Maximum tokens of source code to include

    Returns:
        JSON array of relevant code snippets with file paths, line numbers,
        relevance scores, and source code.
    """
    # 1. Extract seed nodes from the inputs
    seeds = extract_seeds(task_description, mentioned_entities, current_file)

    # 2. Run PPR from the seeds
    ppr_results = run_ppr(seeds, top_k=top_k)

    # 3. Post-process (filter utility functions, read source code, apply token budget)
    context = format_context(ppr_results, token_budget=token_budget)

    return json.dumps(context)

Tool 2: query_dependencies (supporting tool)

@mcp.tool()
def query_dependencies(
    entity_name: str,
    direction: str = "both",
    depth: int = 1
) -> str:
    """
    Get direct dependencies for a specific code entity.

    Unlike get_relevant_context (which uses PPR for ranked relevance),
    this tool returns the raw dependency information — what does this
    function call, what calls it, what does it import.

    Args:
        entity_name: Qualified name of the entity (e.g., "AuthService.authenticate")
        direction: "upstream" (what calls this), "downstream" (what this calls), or "both"
        depth: How many hops to traverse (1 = direct only, 2 = include indirect)
    """

Tool 3: get_graph_stats (diagnostic tool)

@mcp.tool()
def get_graph_stats() -> str:
    """
    Return statistics about the code graph.

    Useful for the AI agent to understand the codebase structure:
    total nodes, edges, most connected files, etc.
    """

docs.title: How the AI agent uses these tools
docs.text:
When a developer asks Claude Code to "fix the authentication timeout bug":

  1. Claude Code reads the task description
  2. Claude Code decides it needs code context and calls our MCP tool: 
    {
  "tool": "get_relevant_context",
  "arguments": {
    "task_description": "fix the authentication timeout bug",
    "mentioned_entities": ["authenticate", "timeout", "AuthService"]
  }
}
  3. Our MCP server receives this, runs the seed extraction + PPR pipeline, returns relevant code
  4. Claude Code includes that code in its context and works on the fix

The AI agent decides when and how to call the tool. We just provide the tool and let the agent use it.

docs.title: What we're measuring
docs.text:
The fundamental question: Does PPR retrieve the right code?

We answer this by using real bug reports where we know which files were actually changed to fix the bug. If PPR's top results include those files, PPR "got it right."

docs.title: The benchmark: SWE-bench Lite
docs.text:
SWE-bench Lite is a dataset of 300 real bug reports from popular Python repositories (Django, Flask, scikit-learn, matplotlib, etc.). For each bug, the dataset provides:

  • The repository state before the fix (a specific git commit)
  • The bug description (the GitHub issue text)
  • The gold patch — the actual code changes the developer made to fix the bug (which files were modified, which functions were changed)

We don't fix the bugs. We don't run an LLM. We just ask: given this bug description, does PPR point to the files that were actually changed?

docs.title: The evaluation process
docs.text:
For each of the 300 bug reports:
  1. Check out the repository at the "before fix" commit
  2. Build the code graph (tree-sitter → Neo4j)
  3. Extract seed nodes from the bug description text
  4. Run PPR from the seeds, get top-k results
  5. Check: do the top-k results include the files from the gold patch?
  6. Record hit/miss and rank of first correct result

docs.title: The metrics
docs.text:
Recall@k — "Out of all the files that needed changing, what percentage appeared in my top k results?"

Example: A bug required changing 3 files. PPR's top 10 results included 2 of them. → Recall@10 = 2/3 = 66.7%

We report Recall@5 and Recall@10.

MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) — "On average, where did the first correct file appear in my ranking?"

If the first correct file is ranked #1 → score = 1/1 = 1.0 If the first correct file is ranked #3 → score = 1/3 = 0.33 If the first correct file is ranked #7 → score = 1/7 = 0.14

MRR is the average of these scores across all 300 bug reports. Higher MRR means the correct files appear earlier in the ranking.

docs.title: What we compare against (baselines)
docs.text: Baseline How it works Why include it Random Pick k random files from the repo Floor — any reasonable method must beat this BM25 Keyword search: match bug description text against code file contents Standard text-retrieval baseline that every paper compares against 1-hop ego-graph Find entities mentioned in the bug, return their direct graph neighbors (no PPR) Tests whether PPR's multi-hop propagation adds value over simple graph lookup

docs.title: Ablation studies
docs.text:
An ablation study is an experiment where you remove or change one component to measure its impact. It answers: "how much does each part of the system contribute?"

Planned ablations:

Experiment What changes What it reveals Remove CALLS edges Graph has only IMPORTS, INHERITS, CONTAINS How important are call relationships? Remove IMPORTS edges Graph has only CALLS, INHERITS, CONTAINS How important are import relationships? Remove INHERITS edges Graph has only CALLS, IMPORTS, CONTAINS How important is inheritance? Change α (restart probability) α ∈ How far should PPR spread from the seed? Change direction Directed vs reversed vs undirected Which edge direction works best for context? With vs without IDF weighting Compare utility-function handling on vs off Does downweighting common functions help? PPR vs 1-hop Compare PPR against simple neighbor lookup Does the algorithm's sophistication matter? Change top-k k ∈ How many results are needed for good recall?

Each ablation is a separate run of the evaluation with one variable changed. This produces a table of results that shows what matters and what doesn't — valuable thesis content regardless of the outcome.

docs.title: 15. Research Papers This Project Builds On
docs.text:
This project combines ideas from three recent papers. The table shows what each paper does and what this project takes from it:

RepoGraph (ICLR 2025) LocAgent (ACL 2025) CodexGraph (NAACL 2025) What it does Builds a code graph, retrieves k-hop ego-graphs as context Builds a code graph, provides agent tools for search + traversal Builds a code graph in Neo4j, uses LLM to write Cypher queries Graph storage In-memory (custom) In-memory (custom) Neo4j Graph schema 2 edge types (contain, invoke) 4 edge types (contain, import, invoke, inherit) 5 edge types (contains, inherits, has_method, has_field, uses) Retrieval method k-hop ego-graph (grab everything within k edges) BFS + BM25 search LLM generates Cypher queries Key limitation No ranking within ego-graph; k=2 explodes in size Relies on agent making good search queries Performance drops catastrophically if LLM writes bad Cypher What this project takes from it Tree-sitter parsing approach; SWE-bench evaluation; plug-in architecture concept Graph schema (4 entity + 4 relation types); seed extraction via keyword search Neo4j as graph storage; graph construction pipeline How this project improves on it PPR gives ranked results instead of unranked ego-graphs MCP server instead of custom agent tools; PPR instead of BFS PPR is automatic (no LLM in retrieval); no fragile query translation

This project's novel combination: 1. From RepoGraph: The idea of a graph-based retrieval module that plugs into existing agents 2. From LocAgent: The comprehensive graph schema with 4 relationship types + keyword search for seed extraction 3. From CodexGraph: Neo4j as the graph database 4. New: PPR as the retrieval algorithm (none of these papers use PPR), exposed via MCP (none use MCP)

This is a valid Bachelor's thesis contribution: taking established techniques, combining them in a new way, and evaluating the result systematically. No breakthrough required.

docs.title: 16. Known Limitations
docs.text:
Being upfront about limitations demonstrates maturity and is expected in academic work.

1. Static analysis only. The graph is built by reading source code, not by running it. This means dynamic behaviors (runtime type changes, monkey-patching, eval()) are invisible. For well-structured Python projects, static analysis captures the majority of relationships. For highly dynamic codebases, the graph will be incomplete.

2. Python only (in this thesis). The architecture is designed to be language-extensible (tree-sitter supports 100+ languages, and the graph schema is language-agnostic). But the implementation only covers Python. Adding TypeScript, Java, etc. is future work.

3. Import resolution is imperfect. The custom resolver handles ~80% of imports in well-structured projects. Complex import patterns (__init__.py re-exports, namespace packages, conditional imports) may be missed. This means some IMPORTS edges will be absent from the graph.

4. Call resolution is imperfect. Without type inference, method calls on objects (obj.method()) can only be resolved when the type of obj is explicitly known (via type hints or direct assignment). Duck-typed calls will be missed.

5. Utility functions may still be noisy. The IDF-inspired weighting reduces but may not eliminate the problem of highly-connected infrastructure functions appearing in results. The threshold and formula may need tuning per-codebase.

6. MCP adoption is still growing. As of February 2026, MCP is supported by Claude Desktop, has been adopted by OpenAI, and is supported in Cursor through extensions. But not every AI coding tool supports MCP natively yet. The system also provides a REST API fallback.

7. No real-time incremental updates. The graph is built once for a repository snapshot. If the code changes, the graph needs to be rebuilt (takes 5-30 seconds for thesis-sized repos). Real-time incremental updates that reflect each keystroke are out of scope.

8. PPR may not outperform simpler methods. It's possible that BM25 or 1-hop ego-graphs perform comparably to PPR for some tasks. This would be a valid negative result — the evaluation will reveal it, and the ablation studies will show why.

docs.title: 17. Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) A tree-shaped representation of source code's structure. Each node represents a code construct (function, class, expression). Created by a parser. BM25 A text search algorithm that ranks documents by keyword relevance. Standard baseline in information retrieval research. Cypher Neo4j's query language (like SQL for graphs). Used to create, read, update, and delete nodes and relationships. Damping factor In PageRank, the probability of following a link (vs. teleporting). Typically 0.85. In PPR terms: damping = 1 - α. Edge A connection between two nodes in a graph. In this project: CALLS, IMPORTS, INHERITS_FROM, CONTAINS. Embedding A list of numbers (vector) that represents the "meaning" of a piece of text or code. Used by Cursor/Copilot for similarity search. Entity A code element that becomes a node in the graph: a function, class, method, or file. GDS (Graph Data Science) Neo4j's plugin library that provides graph algorithms (PageRank, PPR, community detection, etc.) Gold patch In SWE-bench, the actual code changes a developer made to fix a bug. Used as the "correct answer" for evaluation. Graph projection An in-memory copy of part of the Neo4j graph, optimized for running algorithms. Created before running PPR. IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) A weighting technique that gives lower weight to terms/nodes that appear frequently (they're less informative). Import resolution The process of determining which actual file a Python import statement refers to. MCP (Model Context Protocol) An open standard by Anthropic for connecting AI applications to external tools. Like USB-C for AI tools. MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) An evaluation metric: the average of (1 / rank of first correct result) across all queries. Node An entity in a graph. In this project: a File, Class, Function, or Method. PPR (Personalized PageRank) A graph algorithm that ranks nodes by relevance to specific seed nodes, using a random walk with restart. Qualified name The full, unique identifier for a code entity (e.g., src.services.auth.AuthService.authenticate). Recall@k An evaluation metric: what fraction of all correct answers appeared in the top k results. Seed node The starting point for PPR. The node(s) you're computing relevance relative to. SWE-bench A benchmark of real GitHub bug reports with known fixes, used to evaluate code retrieval and fixing tools. Token A unit of text for LLMs. Roughly 3/4 of a word. A "token budget" limits how much text you send. tree-sitter An open-source parser library that converts source code into syntax trees. Fast, supports 100+ languages.

docs.title: Edge Weight Configuration Reference
docs.text:
The complete edge weight system combines a base weight per relationship type with an IDF adjustment based on the target node's connectivity:

final_weight(A → B) = base_weight(edge_type) / log2(in_degree(B) + 2)

Base weights by relationship type:

Edge type Base weight Rationale INHERITS_FROM 1.0 Strongest coupling — changing a base class affects all subclasses CALLS 0.8 Direct dependency — if the callee changes, the caller may break IMPORTS 0.5 File-level connection — weaker than function-level call CONTAINS 0.4 Structural containment — relevant but not a dependency

IDF adjustment examples:

Target node in_degree log2(in_degree + 2) Multiplier (1/log) calculate_expiry() (called by 1 function) 1 1.58 0.63 (barely affected) validate_input() (called by 8 functions) 8 3.32 0.30 (moderately reduced) log_error() (called by 200 functions) 200 7.66 0.13 (heavily reduced)

These weights are applied when creating the GDS graph projection and are used by PPR during the random walk.

docs.title: Performance Expectations
docs.text:
Operation Expected time (10k-50k node graph) tree-sitter parsing (100k LOC) 0.5-2 seconds Entity extraction + resolution 1-5 seconds Neo4j graph loading (batched) 2-15 seconds GDS projection creation < 1 second PPR execution 1-10 milliseconds Source code reading + formatting 50-200 milliseconds Total: full pipeline (parse → result) 5-30 seconds Total: query only (graph already built) < 250 milliseconds

The graph only needs to be built once per repository state. Subsequent queries (PPR + formatting) take under 250ms — well within interactive use.

docs.title: Welcome to CodeGraphContext
docs.text: CodeGraphContext (CGC) is a Code Intelligence Engine. It indexes your codebase into a graph database to provide deep semantic understanding for both developers (via CLI) and AI agents (via MCP).

docs.title: :material-console: For Developers (CLI)
docs.text: Use powerful terminal commands to query call chains, find dependencies, and visual architectural graphs. Explore CLI Reference

docs.title: :material-robot: For AI Agents (MCP)
docs.text: Connect your codebase to Cursor, Claude, or VS Code. Let AI assist you with full context awareness. Setup AI Assistant & Explore Natural Language Queries

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration
docs.text: Customize databases, file limits, and more. View Configuration Guide

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture
docs.text: CGC sits between your code and your tools. It's not just a script; it's a persistent system.

docs.title: 🚀 Why CodeGraphContext?
docs.text:
• Cut Debugging Time: Stop manually grepping. Find the exact root cause in seconds, not hours.
  • Onboard Faster: Help new developers understand complex logic without interrupting senior engineers.
  • Reduce Technical Debt: Identify dead code, circular dependencies, and "god objects" instantly.

docs.title: 🗺️ How to Read These Docs
docs.text:
We have organized the documentation to match your journey:

  1. Getting Started: The linear path to installation.
  2. Usage: Real-world ROI scenarios and user stories.
  3. Guides: Task-based tutorials (Setup, Visualization, CI/CD).
  4. Core Concepts: Understand the "magic" (Architecture, Graph Model).
  5. CLI Reference: Complete terminal command dictionary.
  6. MCP Reference: AI agent tools and queries.
  7. Project Info: Roadmap, contributing, and configuration.

docs.title: Ready to Start?
docs.text: Install CodeGraphContext

docs.title: Learn the Concepts
docs.text: How Indexing Works

docs.title: Project Links
docs.text:
• GitHub Repository: CodeGraphContext/CodeGraphContext
  • Website: codegraphcontext.vercel.app
  • Maintainer: Shashank

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text:
CodeGraphContext (CGC) is a context-aware code intelligence engine that bridges the gap between your source code and your AI tools.

It operates primarily as a background service (MCP Server) backed by a graph database, with a CLI for management.

docs.title: High-Level Diagram
docs.text:
graph TD
    Client[AI Client / CLI]
    Server[MCP Server]
    Graph[Graph Builder]
    DB[(Graph Database)]
    FS[File System]

    Client -- "1. Query (MCP/CLI)" --> Server
    Server -- "2. Read Graph" --> DB
    Server -- "3. Trigger Index" --> Graph
    Graph -- "4. Scan Code" --> FS
    Graph -- "5. Store Nodes" --> DB

docs.title: 1. The Core (Backend)
docs.text:
The "brain" of the operation lives in src/codegraphcontext. It is a pure Python application.

Component Responsibility Server (server.py) Acts as the Model Context Protocol (MCP) host. It translates JSON requests from Claude/Cursor into database queries. Graph Builder The "indexer". It parses your code using Tree-sitter, extracts dependencies, and builds the knowledge graph. Database Layer An abstraction layer that talks to either FalkorDB (embedded) or Neo4j (production). Background Jobs Handles long-running tasks like "Index the entire repo" or "Watch for file changes" without blocking the AI. Watcher A file system monitor that triggers incremental re-indexing when you save a file.

docs.title: 2. No "Frontend" Application
docs.text:
Important: CodeGraphContext does not have a traditional web application frontend. The website/ folder you see in the repository is strictly for documentation (this site).

Instead of a custom UI, we rely on:

  1. AI Chat Interfaces: Your existing IDE chat (Cursor, VS Code) is the UI.
  2. Neo4j Browser: For advanced users wanting a raw visual exploration of the graph (available at localhost:7474 if using Neo4j).
  3. Visualizer Tool: The CLI can generate standalone HTML files (cgc visualize) using Vis.js for quick inspections.

docs.title: 3. Data Flow
docs.text:
1. Indexing:
    • cgc index . -> Scans files -> Parses AST -> Creates Nodes (Functions, Classes) and Edges (CALLS, INHERITS) -> Saves to DB.
  2. Querying (Natural Language):
    • User asks "Who calls function X?" -> MCP Server receives request -> Runs Cypher Query (MATCH (n)-[:CALLS]->(m)...) -> Returns context to LLM.

docs.title: 4. Key Technologies
docs.text:
• Language: Python 3.10+
  • Parsing: Tree-sitter (for robust, multi-language support)
  • Protocol: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
  • Database: FalkorDB (via Redis) or Neo4j
  • CLI: Typer / Click

docs.title: Contributing to CodeGraphContext
docs.text: We welcome contributions! Please follow these steps:

docs.title: General Guidelines
docs.text:
• Ensure your code adheres to the existing style and conventions of the project.
  • Write clear, concise, and well-documented code.
  • All new features or bug fixes should be accompanied by appropriate tests.
  • Keep your pull requests focused on a single feature or bug fix.

docs.title: Setting up Your Development Environment
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository.
  2. Set up your development environment: pip install -e ".[dev]"
  3. Create a new branch for your feature or bugfix (e.g., git checkout -b feature/my-new-feature).

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
To enable debug mode for detailed logging, locate the debug_mode variable in src/codegraphcontext/tools/graph_builder.py and set its value to 1.

# src/codegraphcontext/tools/graph_builder.py
debug_mode = 1

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Tests are located in the tests/ directory and are run using pytest.

  1. Navigate to the root of the CodeGraphContext directory.
  2. Run all tests using the command: pytest
  3. To run specific tests, you can provide the path to the test file, for example: pytest tests/test_tools.py
  4. Skipping Re-indexing: To speed up test runs, especially during development, you can set the CGC_SKIP_REINDEX environment variable to true. This will prevent the test suite from re-indexing the sample project if it's already indexed. 
    CGC_SKIP_REINDEX=true pytest

docs.title: Submitting Changes
docs.text:
1. Write your code and add corresponding tests in the tests/ directory.
  2. Ensure all tests pass and your code lints without errors.
  3. Commit your changes with a descriptive commit message.
  4. Submit a pull request to the main branch.

docs.title: Contributing New Language Support to CodeGraphContext
docs.text: This document outlines the steps and best practices for adding support for a new programming language to CodeGraphContext. By following this guide, contributors can efficiently integrate new languages and leverage the Neo4j graph for verification.

docs.title: 1. Understanding the Architecture
docs.text:
CodeGraphContext uses a modular architecture for multi-language support:

  • Generic TreeSitterParser (in graph_builder.py): This acts as a wrapper, dispatching parsing tasks to language-specific implementations.
  • Language-Specific Parser Modules (in src/codegraphcontext/tools/languages/): Each language (e.g., Python, JavaScript) has its own module (e.g., python.py, javascript.py) containing:
    • Tree-sitter queries (<LANG>_QUERIES).
    • A <Lang>TreeSitterParser class that encapsulates language-specific parsing logic.
    • A pre_scan_<lang> function for initial symbol mapping.
  • GraphBuilder (in graph_builder.py): Manages the overall graph building process, including file discovery, pre-scanning, and dispatching to the correct language parser.

docs.title: 2. Steps to Add a New Language (e.g., TypeScript - .ts)
Step 2.1: Create the Language Module File
docs.text:
1. Create a new file: src/codegraphcontext/tools/languages/typescript.py.
  2. Add the necessary imports: from pathlib import Path, from typing import Any, Dict, Optional, Tuple, import logging, import ast (if needed for AST manipulation).
  3. Define TS_QUERIES (Tree-sitter queries for TypeScript).
  4. Create a TypescriptTreeSitterParser class.
  5. Create a pre_scan_typescript function.

docs.title: Step 2.2: Define Tree-sitter Queries (TS_QUERIES)
docs.text:
This is the most critical and often iterative step. You'll need to define queries for:

  • functions: Function declarations, arrow functions, methods.
  • classes: Class declarations, class expressions.
  • imports: ES6 imports (import ... from ...), CommonJS require().
  • calls: Function calls, method calls.
  • variables: Variable declarations (let, const, var).
  • docstrings: (Optional) How documentation comments are identified.
  • lambda_assignments: (Optional, Python-specific) If the language has similar constructs.

Tips for Query Writing: * Consult Tree-sitter Grammars: Find the node-types.json or grammar definition for your language (e.g., tree-sitter-typescript). * Use tree-sitter parse: Use the tree-sitter parse command-line tool to inspect the AST of sample code snippets. This is invaluable for identifying correct node types and field names. * Start Simple: Begin with basic queries and gradually add complexity. * Test Iteratively: After each query, test it with sample code.

docs.title: Step 2.3: Implement <Lang>TreeSitterParser Class
docs.text:
This class (e.g., TypescriptTreeSitterParser) will encapsulate the language-specific logic.

  1. __init__(self, generic_parser_wrapper):
    • Store generic_parser_wrapper, language_name, language, parser from the generic wrapper.
    • Load TS_QUERIES using self.language.query(query_str).
  2. Helper Methods:
    • _get_node_text(self, node): Extracts text from a tree-sitter node.
    • _get_parent_context(self, node, types=...): (Language-specific node types for context).
    • _calculate_complexity(self, node): (Language-specific complexity nodes).
    • _get_docstring(self, body_node): (Language-specific docstring extraction).
  3. parse(self, path: Path, is_dependency: bool = False) -> Dict:
    • Reads the file, parses it with self.parser.
    • Calls its own _find_* methods (_find_functions, _find_classes, etc.).
    • Returns a standardized dictionary format (as seen in python.py and javascript.py).
  4. _find_* Methods: Implement these for each query type, extracting data from the AST and populating the standardized dictionary.

docs.title: Step 2.4: Implement pre_scan_<lang> Function
docs.text:
This function (e.g., pre_scan_typescript) will quickly scan files to build an initial imports_map.

  1. It takes files: list[Path] and parser_wrapper (an instance of TreeSitterParser).
  2. Uses a simplified query (e.g., for class_declaration and function_declaration) to quickly find definitions.
  3. Returns a dictionary mapping symbol names to file paths.

docs.title: Step 2.5: Integrate into graph_builder.py
docs.text:
1. GraphBuilder.__init__:
    • Add '.ts': TreeSitterParser('typescript') to self.parsers.
  2. TreeSitterParser.__init__:
    • Add an elif self.language_name == 'typescript': block to initialize self.language_specific_parser with TypescriptTreeSitterParser(self).
  3. GraphBuilder._pre_scan_for_imports:
    • Add an elif '.ts' in files_by_lang: block to import pre_scan_typescript and call it.

docs.title: 3. Verification and Debugging using Neo4j
docs.text: After implementing support for a new language, it's crucial to verify that the graph is being built correctly.

docs.title: Step 3.1: Prepare a Sample Project
docs.text: Create a small sample project for your new language (e.g., tests/sample_project_typescript/) with: * Function declarations. * Class declarations (including inheritance). * Various import types (if applicable). * Function calls. * Variable declarations.

docs.title: Step 3.2: Index the Sample Project
docs.text:
1. Delete existing data (if any): ```bash # Replace with your sample project path print(default_api.delete_repository(repo_path='/path/to/your/sample_project'))
  2. Index the project: ```bash # Replace with your sample project path print(default_api.add_code_to_graph(path='/path/to/your/sample_project'))
  3. Monitor job status: ```bash # Use the job_id returned by add_code_to_graph print(default_api.check_job_status(job_id=''))

docs.title: Step 3.3: Query the Neo4j Graph
docs.text:
Use Cypher queries to inspect the generated graph.

  • Check for Files and Language Tags: 

    MATCH (f:File)
WHERE f.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
RETURN f.name, f.path, f.lang
    Expected: All files from your sample project should be listed with the correct lang tag.

  • Check for Functions: 

    MATCH (f:File)-[:CONTAINS]->(fn:Function)
WHERE f.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
  AND fn.lang = '<your_language_name>'
RETURN f.name AS FileName, fn.name AS FunctionName, fn.line_number AS Line
    Expected: All functions from your sample project should be listed.

  • Check for Classes: 

    MATCH (f:File)-[:CONTAINS]->(c:Class)
WHERE f.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
  AND c.lang = '<your_language_name>'
RETURN f.name AS FileName, c.name AS ClassName, c.line_number AS Line
    Expected: All classes from your sample project should be listed.

  • Check for Imports (Module-level): 

    MATCH (f:File)-[:IMPORTS]->(m:Module)
WHERE f.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
  AND f.lang = '<your_language_name>'
RETURN f.name AS FileName, m.name AS ImportedModule, m.full_import_name AS FullImportName
    Expected: All module-level imports should be listed.

  • Check for Function Calls: 

    MATCH (caller:Function)-[:CALLS]->(callee:Function)
WHERE caller.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
  AND caller.lang = '<your_language_name>'
RETURN caller.name AS Caller, callee.name AS Callee, caller.path AS CallerFile, callee.path AS CalleeFile
    Expected: All function calls should be correctly linked.

  • Check for Class Inheritance: 

    MATCH (child:Class)-[:INHERITS]->(parent:Class)
WHERE child.path STARTS WITH '/path/to/your/sample_project'
  AND child.lang = '<your_language_name>'
RETURN child.name AS ChildClass, parent.name AS ParentClass, child.path AS ChildFile, parent.path AS ParentFile
    Expected: All inheritance relationships should be correctly linked.

docs.title: Step 3.4: Debugging Common Issues
docs.text:
• NameError: Invalid node type ...: Your tree-sitter query is using a node type that doesn't exist in the language's grammar. Use tree-sitter parse to inspect the AST.
  • Missing Relationships (e.g., CALLS, IMPORTS):
    • Check _find_* methods: Ensure your _find_* methods are correctly extracting the necessary data.
    • Check imports_map: Verify that the pre_scan_<lang> function is correctly populating the imports_map.
    • Check local_imports map: Ensure the local_imports map (built in _create_function_calls and _create_inheritance_links) is correctly resolving symbols.
  • Incorrect lang tags: Ensure self.language_name is correctly passed and stored.

By following these steps, contributors can effectively add and verify new language support.

docs.title: MCP Tool Cookbook
docs.text: This cookbook provides examples of how to use the mcp tool to query and understand your Python codebase. The "Tool" indicates which mcp tool to use, and the "JSON Arguments" are what the LLM would provide to that tool.

docs.title: 1. Find a specific function by name
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Where is the function foo defined?"
  • Tool: find_code
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query": "foo"
}

docs.title: 2. Find all calls to a specific function
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all calls to the helper function."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_callers",
  "target": "helper"
}

docs.title: 3. Find what a function calls
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "What functions are called inside the foo function?"
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_callees",
  "target": "foo",
  "context": "/teamspace/studios/this_studio/demo/CodeGraphContext/tests/sample_project/module_a.py"
}

docs.title: 4. Find all imports of a specific module
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Where is the math module imported?"
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_importers",
  "target": "math"
}

docs.title: 5. Find all methods of a class
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "What are the methods of the A class?"
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "class_hierarchy",
  "target": "A"
}
  • Note: The response for class_hierarchy includes a list of methods.

docs.title: 6. Find all classes that inherit from a specific class
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all classes that inherit from Base."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "class_hierarchy",
  "target": "Base"
}
  • Note: The response for class_hierarchy includes a list of child classes.

docs.title: 7. Find all functions with a specific decorator
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions with the log_decorator."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_functions_by_decorator",
  "target": "log_decorator"
}

docs.title: 8. Find all dataclasses
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all dataclasses."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class) WHERE 'dataclass' IN c.decorators RETURN c.name, c.path"
}

docs.title: 9. Find the 5 most complex functions
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find the 5 most complex functions."
  • Tool: find_most_complex_functions
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "limit": 5
}

docs.title: 10. Calculate cyclomatic complexity of a function
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "What is the cyclomatic complexity of try_except_finally?"
  • Tool: calculate_cyclomatic_complexity
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "function_name": "try_except_finally"
}

docs.title: 11. Find unused code
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find unused code, but ignore API endpoints decorated with @app.route."
  • Tool: find_dead_code
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "exclude_decorated_with": ["@app.route"]
}

docs.title: 12. Find the call chain between two functions
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "What is the call chain from wrapper to helper?"
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "call_chain",
  "target": "wrapper->helper"
}

docs.title: 13. Find all direct and indirect callers of a function
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all functions that eventually call the helper function."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_all_callers",
  "target": "helper"
}

docs.title: 14. Find functions by argument name
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions that take self as an argument."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_functions_by_argument",
  "target": "self"
}

docs.title: 15. List all python package imports from a directory
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "List all python package imports from my project directory."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:File)-[:IMPORTS]->(m:Module) WHERE f.path ENDS WITH '.py' RETURN DISTINCT m.name"
}

docs.title: 16. List all indexed projects
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "List all projects I have indexed."
  • Tool: list_indexed_repositories
  • JSON Arguments: 
      {}

docs.title: 17. Check the status of an indexing job
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "What is the status of job 4cb9a60e-c1b1-43a7-9c94-c840771506bc?"
  • Tool: check_job_status
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "job_id": "4cb9a60e-c1b1-43a7-9c94-c840771506bc"
}

docs.title: 18. List all background jobs
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all background jobs."
  • Tool: list_jobs
  • JSON Arguments: 
      {}

docs.title: Advanced Cypher Queries
docs.text: These examples use the execute_cypher_query tool for more specific and complex questions.

docs.title: 19. Find all function definitions
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all function definitions in the codebase."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (n:Function) RETURN n.name, n.path, n.line_number LIMIT 50"
}

docs.title: 20. Find all classes
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all the classes."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (n:Class) RETURN n.name, n.path, n.line_number LIMIT 50"
}

docs.title: 21. Find all functions in a file
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions in module_a.py."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.path ENDS WITH 'module_a.py' RETURN f.name"
}

docs.title: 22. Find all classes in a file
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all classes in advanced_classes.py."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class) WHERE c.path ENDS WITH 'advanced_classes.py' RETURN c.name"
}

docs.title: 23. List all top-level functions and classes in a file
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "List all top-level functions and classes in module_a.py."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:File)-[:CONTAINS]->(n) WHERE f.name = 'module_a.py' AND (n:Function OR n:Class) AND n.context IS NULL RETURN n.name"
}

docs.title: 24. Find functions in one module that call a function in another
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find functions in module_a.py that call helper in module_b.py."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (caller:Function)-[:CALLS]->(callee:Function {name: 'helper'}) WHERE caller.path ENDS WITH 'module_a.py' AND callee.path ENDS WITH 'module_b.py' RETURN caller.name"
}

docs.title: 25. Find circular file imports
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Are there any circular dependencies between files?"
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f1:File)-[:IMPORTS]->(m2:Module), (f2:File)-[:IMPORTS]->(m1:Module) WHERE f1.name = m1.name + '.py' AND f2.name = m2.name + '.py' RETURN f1.name, f2.name"
}

docs.title: 26. Find all functions with more than 5 arguments
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions with a large number of arguments."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE size(f.args) > 5 RETURN f.name, f.path, size(f.args) as arg_count"
}

docs.title: 27. Find all functions in a file that have a docstring
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions in module_a.py that have a docstring."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.path ENDS WITH 'module_a.py' AND f.docstring IS NOT NULL AND f.docstring <> '' RETURN f.name"
}

docs.title: 28. Find all classes that have a specific method
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all classes that have a greet method."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class)-[:CONTAINS]->(m:Function {name: 'greet'}) RETURN c.name, c.path"
}

docs.title: 29. Find the depth of inheritance for all classes
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "How deep are the inheritance chains for all classes?"
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class) OPTIONAL MATCH path = (c)-[:INHERITS*]->(parent:Class) RETURN c.name, c.path, length(path) AS depth ORDER BY depth DESC"
}

docs.title: 30. Find all functions that have a docstring
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all functions that are documented."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.docstring IS NOT NULL AND f.docstring <> '' RETURN f.name, f.path LIMIT 50"
}

docs.title: 31. Find all decorated methods in a class
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all decorated methods in the Child class."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class {name: 'Child'})-[:CONTAINS]->(m:Function) WHERE m.decorators IS NOT NULL AND size(m.decorators) > 0 RETURN m.name"
}

docs.title: 32. Find the number of functions in each file
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "How many functions are in each file?"
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) RETURN f.path, count(f) AS function_count ORDER BY function_count DESC"
}

docs.title: 33. Find all methods that override a parent method
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all methods that are overridden from a parent class."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Class)-[:INHERITS]->(p:Class), (c)-[:CONTAINS]->(m:Function), (p)-[:CONTAINS]->(m_parent:Function) WHERE m.name = m_parent.name RETURN m.name as method, c.name as child_class, p.name as parent_class"
}

docs.title: 34. Find all functions that call super()
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all methods that call their parent's method via super()."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function)-[r:CALLS]->() WHERE r.full_call_name STARTS WITH 'super(' RETURN f.name, f.path"
}

docs.title: 35. Find all calls to a function with a specific argument
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all calls to helper with the argument x."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH ()-[r:CALLS]->(f:Function {name: 'helper'}) WHERE 'x' IN r.args RETURN r.full_call_name, r.line_number, r.path"
}

docs.title: 36. Find all functions that are not called by any other function
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all dead code (functions that are never called)."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE NOT (()-[:CALLS]->(f)) AND f.is_dependency = false RETURN f.name, f.path"
}

docs.title: 37. Find all functions that are called with a specific argument
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all calls to print with the argument 'hello'."
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (c:Call) WHERE c.name = 'print' AND 'hello' IN c.args RETURN c.file, c.lineno"
}

docs.title: 38. Find all direct and indirect callees of a function
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Show me all functions that are eventually called by the foo function."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "find_all_callees",
  "target": "foo",
  "context": "/teamspace/studios/this_studio/demo/CodeGraphContext/tests/sample_project/module_a.py"
}

docs.title: 39. Find all functions that are overridden
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions that are overridden."
  • Tool: analyze_code_relationships
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "query_type": "overrides",
  "target": "foo"
}

docs.title: 40. Find all modules imported by module_a
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all modules imported by module_a."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:File {name: 'module_a.py'})-[:IMPORTS]->(m:Module) RETURN m.name AS imported_module_name"
}

docs.title: 41. Find large functions that should be refactored
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find functions with more than 20 lines of code that might need refactoring."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function)\n    WHERE f.end_line - f.line_number > 20\n    RETURN f"
}

docs.title: 42. Find recursive functions
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find all functions that call themselves (recursive functions)."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH p=(f:Function)-[:CALLS]->(f2:Function)\n    WHERE f.name = f2.name AND f.path = f2.path\n    RETURN p"
}

docs.title: 43. Find most connected functions (hub functions)
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find the functions that are most central to the codebase (called by many and call many others)."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "MATCH (f:Function)
    OPTIONAL MATCH (f)-[:CALLS]->(callee:Function)
    OPTIONAL MATCH (caller:Function)-[:CALLS]->(f)
    WITH f, count(DISTINCT callee) AS calls_out, count(DISTINCT caller) AS calls_in
    ORDER BY (calls_out + calls_in) DESC
    LIMIT 5
    MATCH p=(f)-[*0..2]-()
    RETURN p"
}

docs.title: 44. Find potential security vulnerabilities (hardcoded secrets)
docs.text:
• Natural Language: "Find potential hardcoded passwords, API keys, or secrets in the codebase."
  • Tool: execute_cypher_query
  • JSON Arguments: 
    {
  "cypher_query": "WITH [\"password\",  \"api_key\", \"apikey\",    \"secret_token\", \"token\", \"auth\", \"access_key\", \"private_key\", \"client_secret\", \"sessionid\", \"jwt\"] AS keywords\n    MATCH (f:Function)\n    WHERE ANY(word IN keywords WHERE toLower(f.source_code) CONTAINS word)\n    RETURN f"
}

docs.title: Ongoing Concerns and Future Work
docs.text: This page outlines some of the current limitations of CodeGraphContext and areas for future development.

docs.title: Semantic Search
docs.text: The tool is smart enough to find and analyze a function through millions of code files, but the tool is not yet smart enough to understand that a user searching for “calculate_sum” is also intending to look at the “calculate_addition” function. This level of semantic similarity needs to be researched, developed, tested and eventually implemented by our tool.

docs.title: Integration Guide - CodeGraphContext
docs.text: This document explains how CodeGraphContext integrates seamlessly into typical developer workflows, rather than requiring "step-outs" or context switching.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Philosophy: Integration vs Step-Out
  2. IDE Integration (Primary Workflow)
  3. CI/CD Integration
  4. Git Workflow Integration
  5. Code Review Integration
  6. Documentation Integration
  7. Team Collaboration Integration
  8. Comparison: CGC vs Alternatives

docs.title: ❌ Step-Out Workflow (What We Avoid)
docs.text:
Traditional code analysis tools require context switching:

Developer Workflow (Broken):
1. Writing code in IDE
2. ❌ STOP - Open terminal
3. ❌ Run analysis tool
4. ❌ Read output in terminal
5. ❌ Switch back to IDE
6. ❌ Remember what you were doing
7. Continue coding (context lost)

Problems: - Context switching kills productivity - Interrupts flow state - Requires remembering to use the tool - Separate from where you work

docs.title: ✅ Integrated Workflow (What CGC Provides)
docs.text:
CGC integrates into your existing tools:

Developer Workflow (Seamless):
1. Writing code in IDE
2. ✅ Ask AI assistant (already in IDE)
3. ✅ AI uses CGC automatically (invisible)
4. ✅ Get answer in same context
5. Continue coding (flow maintained)

Benefits: - No context switching - Natural language interface - Automatic, not manual - Integrated where you already work

docs.title: Setup (One-Time, 5 minutes)
docs.text:
# 1. Install CGC
pip install codegraphcontext

# 2. Index your project
cd ~/projects/my-project
cgc index .

# 3. Setup MCP
cgc mcp setup
# Select: Cursor

# 4. Start MCP server (auto-start recommended)
cgc mcp start &

# 5. Restart Cursor

docs.title: Daily Workflow (Zero Extra Steps)
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Understanding Code

You're reading unfamiliar code:

// You see this function call
processPayment(order, user);

// You wonder: "What does this do?"

Traditional Approach:
1. ❌ Search for function definition
2. ❌ Open file
3. ❌ Read code
4. ❌ Find what IT calls
5. ❌ Repeat...

CGC Integrated Approach:
1. ✅ Highlight "processPayment"
2. ✅ Ask AI: "What does this function do?"
3. ✅ AI uses CGC, shows:
   - Function definition
   - What it calls
   - Who calls it
   - Full execution flow
4. ✅ Continue reading (no context switch)

Scenario 2: Before Refactoring

You want to rename a function:

Traditional Approach:
1. ❌ Open terminal
2. ❌ Run: grep -r "oldFunction" .
3. ❌ Read 100+ lines of output
4. ❌ Manually filter false positives
5. ❌ Switch back to IDE
6. ❌ Start refactoring (hope you didn't miss anything)

CGC Integrated Approach:
1. ✅ Right-click function name
2. ✅ Ask AI: "What will break if I rename this?"
3. ✅ AI uses CGC, shows:
   - All 23 callers
   - Files affected
   - Test coverage
4. ✅ IDE refactor tool with confidence
5. ✅ No terminal, no context switch

Scenario 3: Code Review

You're reviewing a PR:

Traditional Approach:
1. ❌ Read PR diff
2. ❌ Open terminal
3. ❌ Run analysis commands
4. ❌ Switch back to GitHub
5. ❌ Write review
6. ❌ Repeat for each file

CGC Integrated Approach:
1. ✅ Open PR in Cursor
2. ✅ Ask AI: "Analyze the impact of this PR"
3. ✅ AI uses CGC, provides:
   - Functions affected
   - Test coverage
   - Risk assessment
4. ✅ Write review in same window
5. ✅ No context switch

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
Automatic Code Quality Checks

# .github/workflows/code-quality.yml
name: Code Quality

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Setup Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'

      - name: Install CodeGraphContext
        run: pip install codegraphcontext

      - name: Index codebase
        run: cgc index .

      - name: Find dead code
        run: |
          cgc analyze dead-code --exclude-decorated @api.route > dead-code.txt
          if [ -s dead-code.txt ]; then
            echo "⚠️ Dead code found:"
            cat dead-code.txt
            echo "::warning::Dead code detected. Consider cleanup."
          fi

      - name: Check complexity
        run: |
          cgc analyze complexity --limit 10 --threshold 15 > complex.txt
          if [ -s complex.txt ]; then
            echo "⚠️ Complex functions found:"
            cat complex.txt
            echo "::warning::High complexity detected. Consider refactoring."
          fi

      - name: Find circular dependencies
        run: |
          cgc query "
            MATCH (m1:Module)-[:IMPORTS]->(m2:Module)-[:IMPORTS]->(m1)
            RETURN m1.name, m2.name
          " > circular.txt
          if [ -s circular.txt ]; then
            echo "❌ Circular dependencies found:"
            cat circular.txt
            echo "::error::Circular dependencies detected!"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Comment on PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v6
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const deadCode = fs.readFileSync('dead-code.txt', 'utf8');
            const complex = fs.readFileSync('complex.txt', 'utf8');

            let comment = '## Code Quality Report\n\n';

            if (deadCode) {
              comment += '### ⚠️ Dead Code\n```\n' + deadCode + '\n```\n\n';
            }

            if (complex) {
              comment += '### ⚠️ Complex Functions\n```\n' + complex + '\n```\n\n';
            }

            if (!deadCode && !complex) {
              comment += '✅ No issues found!\n';
            }

            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: comment
            });

Result: Every PR automatically gets code quality feedback

docs.title: GitLab CI
docs.text:
# .gitlab-ci.yml
code_quality:
  stage: test
  image: python:3.10
  script:
    - pip install codegraphcontext
    - cgc index .
    - cgc analyze dead-code > dead-code.txt || true
