docs.title: 🌐 Hosting Options Comparison Guide
docs.text: This guide compares different hosting options for CodeGraphContext to help you choose the best option for your needs.

docs.title: 📊 Quick Comparison Table
docs.text: Provider Type Monthly Cost Setup Difficulty Best For Pros Cons DigitalOcean Droplet VM $12-24 Easy Small teams, production Simple, affordable, predictable pricing Manual scaling AWS EC2 VM $15-50 Medium Enterprise, AWS users Flexible, integrates with AWS Complex pricing, requires AWS knowledge Google Cloud VM VM $15-45 Medium GCP users Good free tier, reliable Complex pricing Azure VM VM $20-50 Medium Microsoft shops Azure integration More expensive Google Cloud Run Serverless $5-20 Easy Variable workloads Pay per use, auto-scaling Cold starts, stateless AWS Fargate Container $15-40 Medium AWS users, containers Serverless containers AWS lock-in Azure Container Instances Container $30-60 Easy Azure users Simple deployment More expensive Railway.app PaaS $0-20 Very Easy Hobby projects Free tier, simple Limited resources Render.com PaaS $0-25 Very Easy Small projects Free tier, easy setup Limited free tier Fly.io PaaS $0-30 Easy Global deployment Edge deployment, free tier Learning curve Oracle Cloud VM $0 (Free tier) Medium Budget-conscious Always free tier ARM architecture Self-hosted On-premise Hardware cost Hard Full control needed Complete control Maintenance burden Kubernetes (GKE/EKS/AKS) Orchestration $50-200+ Hard Large scale, microservices Scalable, resilient Complex, expensive

docs.title: DigitalOcean Droplets ⭐ RECOMMENDED
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $12/month (2GB RAM) - $24/month (4GB RAM)
⚙️ Setup: Easy
📈 Scaling: Manual

Pros: - Simple, predictable pricing - Excellent documentation - Easy-to-use control panel - Good performance - Managed databases available - Free bandwidth allowance

Cons: - Manual scaling required - Limited to specific regions - No serverless options

Best For: - Small to medium teams - Production deployments - Budget-conscious projects - Developers new to cloud

Setup Steps: 

# 1. Create droplet (Ubuntu 22.04, 2-4GB RAM)
# 2. SSH into droplet
ssh root@your-droplet-ip

# 3. Install Docker
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh

# 4. Clone and deploy
git clone https://github.com/CodeGraphContext/CodeGraphContext.git
cd CodeGraphContext
./docker-quickstart.sh

docs.title: AWS EC2
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $15-50/month (t3.medium or larger)
⚙️ Setup: Medium
📈 Scaling: Auto-scaling available

Pros: - Integrates with AWS ecosystem - Auto-scaling groups - Spot instances for cost savings - Global presence - Advanced networking

Cons: - Complex pricing - Steep learning curve - Can get expensive - Requires IAM knowledge

Best For: - Existing AWS users - Enterprise deployments - Need for AWS integrations - Advanced networking requirements

docs.title: Google Cloud Compute Engine
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $15-45/month (e2-medium or larger)
⚙️ Setup: Medium
📈 Scaling: Auto-scaling available

Pros: - $300 free credit for new users - Good performance - Preemptible VMs for savings - Global network - Easy integration with GCP services

Cons: - Complex pricing - Learning curve - Requires GCP knowledge

Best For: - GCP users - Need for Google services integration - Global deployment

docs.title: Azure Virtual Machines
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $20-50/month (B2s or larger)
⚙️ Setup: Medium
📈 Scaling: Auto-scaling available

Pros: - Azure ecosystem integration - Good for .NET applications - Enterprise support - Hybrid cloud options

Cons: - Generally more expensive - Complex portal - Steeper learning curve

Best For: - Microsoft shops - Enterprise with Azure commitment - .NET integration needs

docs.title: Google Cloud Run ⭐ BEST SERVERLESS OPTION
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $5-20/month (pay per use)
⚙️ Setup: Easy
📈 Scaling: Automatic (0 to N)

Pros: - Pay only for actual usage - Automatic scaling to zero - No infrastructure management - Fast deployments - Free tier (2M requests/month)

Cons: - Cold starts (1-3 seconds) - Stateless (need external DB) - Request timeout limits - Not ideal for long-running tasks

Best For: - Variable workloads - API endpoints - Cost optimization - Minimal maintenance

Setup: 

gcloud builds submit --tag gcr.io/PROJECT_ID/codegraphcontext
gcloud run deploy codegraphcontext \
  --image gcr.io/PROJECT_ID/codegraphcontext \
  --platform managed \
  --memory 2Gi \
  --allow-unauthenticated

docs.title: AWS Fargate
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $15-40/month
⚙️ Setup: Medium
📈 Scaling: Automatic

Pros: - Serverless containers - No EC2 management - Integrates with ECS/EKS - Good for microservices

Cons: - AWS lock-in - More expensive than EC2 - Complex setup - Limited customization

Best For: - AWS container users - Microservices architecture - No server management

docs.title: Azure Container Instances
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $30-60/month
⚙️ Setup: Easy
📈 Scaling: Manual/Automatic

Pros: - Simple deployment - Fast startup - Azure integration - Per-second billing

Cons: - More expensive - Limited features vs AKS - Regional availability

Best For: - Azure users - Simple container deployments - Batch processing

docs.title: Railway.app ⭐ BEST FOR HOBBY PROJECTS
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $0-20/month (Free tier available)
⚙️ Setup: Very Easy
📈 Scaling: Automatic

Pros: - Generous free tier - Extremely simple deployment - GitHub integration - Automatic HTTPS - Built-in databases

Cons: - Limited resources on free tier - Less control - Smaller community

Best For: - Hobby projects - Prototypes - Learning/testing - Small personal projects

Setup: 

# 1. Connect GitHub repo
# 2. Railway auto-detects Dockerfile
# 3. Deploy with one click

docs.title: Render.com
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $0-25/month (Free tier available)
⚙️ Setup: Very Easy
📈 Scaling: Automatic

Pros: - Free tier for web services - Simple deployment - Automatic SSL - Good documentation - Managed databases

Cons: - Free tier spins down after inactivity - Limited free tier resources - Less flexible than VMs

Best For: - Small projects - Side projects - Testing - Simple deployments

docs.title: Fly.io
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $0-30/month (Free tier: 3 VMs)
⚙️ Setup: Easy
📈 Scaling: Global edge deployment

Pros: - Free tier with 3 shared VMs - Global edge deployment - Low latency worldwide - Good for distributed apps - Persistent volumes

Cons: - Learning curve for flyctl CLI - Smaller ecosystem - Limited documentation

Best For: - Global applications - Low latency requirements - Edge computing - Distributed systems

docs.title: Oracle Cloud Always Free ⭐ BEST FREE OPTION
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $0 (Forever free tier)
⚙️ Setup: Medium
📈 Scaling: Manual

Pros: - Truly free forever - 4 ARM cores, 24GB RAM (generous!) - 200GB storage - No credit card required after trial - Production-ready

Cons: - ARM architecture (may need adjustments) - Complex interface - Account approval can be slow - Limited support

Best For: - Budget-conscious developers - Long-term free hosting - Learning cloud platforms - Personal projects

Free Tier Includes: - 2 AMD VMs (1/8 OCPU, 1GB RAM each) - 4 ARM VMs (1 OCPU, 6GB RAM each) - 200GB block storage - 10TB outbound data transfer/month

docs.title: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $50-200+/month
⚙️ Setup: Hard
📈 Scaling: Highly scalable

Pros: - Production-grade orchestration - Auto-scaling - Self-healing - Rolling updates - Multi-zone deployment

Cons: - Complex setup - Expensive - Requires K8s expertise - Overkill for small projects

Best For: - Large-scale deployments - Microservices - High availability needs - Teams with K8s experience

docs.title: Amazon EKS
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $75-250+/month ($0.10/hour for control plane)
⚙️ Setup: Hard
📈 Scaling: Highly scalable

Best For: - AWS ecosystem users - Enterprise Kubernetes - Complex microservices

docs.title: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
docs.text:
💰 Cost: $50-200+/month (free control plane)
⚙️ Setup: Hard
📈 Scaling: Highly scalable

Best For: - Azure users - Enterprise deployments - .NET microservices

docs.text:
1. Railway.app (free tier)
  2. Oracle Cloud (always free)
  3. Render.com (free tier)

docs.title: Small Team/Startup
docs.text:
1. DigitalOcean ($12-24/month)
  2. Google Cloud Run ($5-20/month)
  3. Fly.io ($0-30/month)

docs.title: Medium Business
docs.text:
1. DigitalOcean ($24-48/month)
  2. AWS EC2 with auto-scaling
  3. Google Cloud VM with managed services

docs.title: Enterprise
docs.text:
1. Kubernetes (GKE/EKS/AKS)
  2. AWS with full ecosystem
  3. Azure for Microsoft shops

docs.title: High Traffic/Scale
docs.text:
1. Kubernetes with auto-scaling
  2. AWS with load balancers
  3. Multi-region deployment

docs.title: 📈 Cost Optimization Tips
docs.text:
1. Start Small: Begin with minimal resources, scale up as needed
  2. Use Spot/Preemptible Instances: Save 60-80% on cloud VMs
  3. Reserved Instances: Commit for 1-3 years for 30-70% savings
  4. Right-size Resources: Monitor and adjust CPU/RAM allocation
  5. Use Free Tiers: Take advantage of always-free and trial credits
  6. Optimize Images: Smaller Docker images = faster deploys, less storage
  7. Clean Up: Delete unused resources, snapshots, and volumes
  8. Use CDN: Offload static content to reduce server load
  9. Implement Caching: Reduce database queries and processing
  10. Monitor Costs: Set up billing alerts and budgets

docs.title: 🔄 Migration Path
docs.text:
Recommended progression:

  1. Start: Local Docker (free)
  2. Test: Railway.app or Render.com (free tier)
  3. Production: DigitalOcean Droplet ($12-24/month)
  4. Scale: Upgrade droplet or move to Kubernetes
  5. Enterprise: Multi-region Kubernetes with managed services

docs.title: 🎉 Final Recommendation
docs.text:
For most users starting out: → DigitalOcean Droplet ($12/month, 2GB RAM)

Why? - Simple and predictable pricing - Easy to set up and manage - Good performance - Excellent documentation - Can easily upgrade as you grow - Great balance of cost, features, and ease of use

Quick Start: 

# 1. Create DigitalOcean account
# 2. Create Ubuntu 22.04 droplet (2GB RAM)
# 3. SSH in and run:
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh
git clone https://github.com/CodeGraphContext/CodeGraphContext.git
cd CodeGraphContext
./docker-quickstart.sh

You'll be up and running in under 10 minutes! 🚀

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: We have designed the installation to be as automatic as possible.

docs.title: Step 1: Install the Package
docs.text:
Open your terminal and run:

pip install codegraphcontext

Tip: We recommend installing this in a virtual environment (venv) or globally via pipx.

docs.title: Step 2: Database Setup
docs.text:
CGC requires a graph database backend. Choose ONE path below.

=== "Option A: FalkorDB (Recommended for Local)"

**Platforms:** Linux, macOS, WSL.

!!! warning "Windows Users"
    On Windows? Please Check the **[Windows Setup Guide](../windows_setup.md)** for WSL and FalkorDB/Neo4j compatibility.

FalkorDB is an embedded, lightweight graph database.
*   **Pros:** Requires zero configuration. Runs automatically. Persistent storage is managed for you.
*   **Cons:** No built-in Interactive Browser (unlike Neo4j). Use `cgc visualize` for basic graphs.

*If you are on Linux/macOS with Python 3.12+, this is the default.*

=== "Option B: Neo4j (Production / Visual)"

**Platforms:** Windows, All OSs, Docker users.

Neo4j is the industry-standard graph database.
*   **Pros:** Powerful web-based Graph Browser (`localhost:7474`). Handles massive codebases better.
*   **Cons:** Heavier resource usage. Requires Docker or a separate service.

1.  **Run the setup wizard:**
    ```bash
    cgc neo4j setup
    ```
2.  **Follow the prompts:**

    ??? info "Detailed Setup Steps"
        *   **Select Installation Type**:
            *   `Docker`: Pulls the official image and starts a container on port 7687. (Requires Docker Desktop/Engine).
            *   `Native`: Help you find your local `neo4j-admin` installation.
        *   **Configure Password**:
            *   You will be asked to set a password for the `neo4j` user.
            *   Default suggestion: `codegraphcontext`.
        *   **Start Service**:
            *   The wizard attempts to run `docker run` or `systemctl start neo4j`.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Let's make sure everything is talking to each other. Run the "Doctor" command:

cgc doctor

What to look for:

  • ✅ Database check passed
  • ✅ Core dependencies satisfied

docs.title: Step 4: Configure AI Assistant (For MCP Users)
docs.text:
If you plan to use CodeGraphContext with Cursor, Claude, VS Code, or Kiro, you must configure the MCP server.

  1. Run the MCP wizard: 

    cgc mcp setup

  2. What it does:

    • 🔍 Asks you to choose your IDE from a supported list (Cursor, VS Code, Claude, Windsurf, Cline, Kiro, etc.).
    • ⚙️ Updates the configuration file (e.g., mcp.json) to invoke cgc.
    • 🔐 Saves local database credentials so the AI can connect.

    Note: We support any MCP-compliant client. If your tool isn't listed, simply copy the JSON output from this command and paste it into your tool's config manually.

  3. Refresh your AI Tool:

    • Click the "Refresh" icon in the MCP/Continue panel.
    • Verify that CodeGraphContext appears in your enabled tools list.

docs.title: Prerequisites & Context
docs.text: Before installing CodeGraphContext (CGC), it helps to understand the pieces involved. CGC is a Client-Server system, even if you run it all on your laptop.

docs.title: The Three Components
docs.text:
1. The Engine (This Tool)
    • A Python package responsible for parsing code and talking to the database.
  2. The Database
    • Where the graph lives. CGC needs a place to store "Function A calls Function B".
  3. The Client
    • CLI: Your terminal.
    • MCP: Your AI Editor (Cursor, VS Code, Claude).

docs.title: 💻 System Requirements
docs.text:
• OS: Linux, macOS, or Windows (WSL recommended).
  • Python: 3.10 or higher.
  • Memory: At least 4GB RAM (Graph DBs love RAM).

docs.title: 🗄️ Database Options (Context)
docs.text:
You do not need to install a database yet. The installer will help you. But you should know your choice:

Option Best For... Complexity FalkorDB Lite Quick Start / Linux / macOS. Runs inside Python. No extra setup. ⭐ Neo4j Windows / Production / Large Team. Persistent storage. Requires Docker or Desktop App. ⭐⭐⭐

docs.title: 🤖 AI Assistant (Optional)
docs.text:
If you plan to use CGC with an AI, you need an MCP-compliant client. We officially support:

  • Cursor IDE
  • VS Code
  • Claude Desktop App
  • ...and any other tool relevant to Agentic Coding that supports the Model Context Protocol.

docs.title: Quickstart (5 Minutes)
docs.text: Let's index a project and run your first analysis.

docs.title: 1. Index Code
docs.text:
Pick a small folder to start with (e.g., this project's own directory).

Tip: Ignore Files First

You can create a .cgcignore file (like .gitignore) to skip folders like node_modules or venv. 📄 Configure .cgcignore

cgc index .
You will see the tool parsing files and creating nodes in the database.

docs.title: 2. Lists Repos
docs.text:
Confirm it was added to the graph.

cgc list

docs.title: 3. Run Your First Analysis
docs.text:
Let's ask the graph a question. "Find all callers of a function". Replace my_function with a real function name from your code.

cgc analyze callers my_function

docs.title: 4. (Optional) Visualize
docs.text:
If you have a browser available, seeing is believing.

cgc visualize
This command generates a link. Click it to open the Graph Browser.

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
You have two paths forward:

  1. I want to code with AI:
    • 👉 Setup AI Assistant (MCP)
    • 👉 Explore Natural Language Queries
    • ⚙️ Customize Settings
  2. I want to use the Terminal:
    • 👉 Explore CLI Commands
    • ⚙️ Customize Settings

docs.title: Using On-Demand Bundles
docs.text: Don't index everything yourself. Use pre-built graphs for popular libraries.

docs.title: What is a Bundle?
docs.text: A .cgc bundle is a snapshot of a graph. It allows you to "import" the knowledge of flask, pandas, or react without parsing it yourself.

docs.title: 1. Search the Registry
docs.text: cgc registry search react

docs.title: 2. Load a Bundle
docs.text:
cgc load react
(This downloads ~5MB instead of parsing 50MB of source code).

docs.title: 3. Query it
docs.text: Now your AI knows about React's internals. "How does useEffect work internally in React?" -> The AI can traverse the imported graph nodes.

docs.title: Requesting a Bundle
docs.text:
If a library isn't there, request it: 

cgc registry request https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi
Our build servers will index it and make it available within minutes.

docs.title: CI/CD & Advanced Usage
docs.text: Integrate CodeGraphContext into your automation pipelines.

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline Integration
docs.text:
You can use CGC to block PRs that introduce "Dead Code" or excessive complexity.

Example GitHub Action:

name: Code Quality
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Install CGC
        run: pip install codegraphcontext
      - name: Index Code
        run: cgc index .
      - name: Check Complexity
        # Fail if any function is complexity > 20
        run: cgc analyze complexity --threshold 20 --fail-on-found

docs.title: Large Scale Indexing
docs.text: For repos with > 100,000 LOC: 1. Use Neo4j: FalkorDB may run out of RAM. 2. Increase Memory: NEO4J_dbms_memory_heap_max_size=4G. 3. Exclude Tests: Add tests/ to .cgcignore.

docs.title: Using with AI (MCP Guide)
docs.text: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) allows AI coding assistants to talk directly to tools like CodeGraphContext.

docs.title: 1. Run the MCP Setup Wizard
docs.text:
We provide an interactive tool to configure your editors automatically.

cgc mcp setup

What happens here:

  1. The tool looks for configuration files (e.g., ~/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/globalStorage/mcp.json).
  2. It injects the CodeGraphContext server details.
  3. It ensures the server knows how to find your database.

docs.title: 2. Supported Clients
docs.text: Client Setup Method Notes Cursor Automatic Requires "MCP" feature enabled in settings. Claude Desktop Automatic Works with the Claude 3.5 Sonnet model. VS Code Semi-Automatic Requires the "Continue" extension or similar MCP client.

docs.title: 3. How to Use It (Once Connected)
docs.text:
Open your AI Chat and talk naturally. The AI now has a "tool" it can call.

Example Prompts:

  • "Please index the current directory." -> AI calls add_code_to_graph
  • "Who calls the process_payment function?" -> AI calls analyze_callers
  • "Find all dead code in utils.py." -> AI calls find_dead_code

docs.title: 4. Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• "Component not found": This usually means the MCP server didn't start. Check the logs in your AI editor.
  • "Database error": Ensure your Neo4j container is running (docker ps) or that your Python environment is active.

docs.title: Setup Deep Dive: Neo4j Wizard
docs.text: This guide explains exactly what the cgc neo4j setup wizard does behind the scenes.

docs.title: 🪄 cgc neo4j setup
docs.text:
Purpose: Configures the Storage backend.

What it does (Docker Mode):

  1. Checks if docker is available.
  2. Runs docker pull neo4j:latest.
  3. Runs a container mapping ports 7474 (HTTP) and 7687 (Bolt).
  4. Sets a default password (codegraphcontext).

What it does (Native Mode):

  1. Checks for apt (Debian/Ubuntu).
  2. Adds the Neo4j repository keys.
  3. Runs apt install neo4j.

Failure Modes:

  • Port Conflict: If port 7687 is already used, the container will exit.
  • No Docker: Steps will fail immediately.

docs.title: Visualizing the Graph
docs.text: Sometimes a table of text isn't enough. You need to see the map.

docs.title: 1. Browser link (cgc visualize)
docs.text:
The easiest way involves the cgc visualize command (alias cgc v).

cgc visualize

This will print a URL (e.g., http://localhost:7474/browser?cmd=edit&arg=MATCH...). Clicking this links opens the Neo4j Browser with a pre-filled query to show the immediate neighborhood of your code.

docs.title: 2. Using Neo4j Bloom (Advanced)
docs.text: If you are using Neo4j Desktop, you can use Bloom. * Bloom allows "Google Maps" style zooming. * Type logical phrases like "Show me callers of X".

docs.title: 3. Interactive Web View (Coming Soon)
docs.text: We are building a lightweight React-based visualizer that runs directly from cgc analyze --viz. * View Roadmap

docs.title: .cgcignore Guide
docs.text: The .cgcignore file tells CodeGraphContext which files or folders to skip during indexing. It functions exactly like .gitignore.

docs.title: Why use it?
docs.text:
• Performance: Skipping large directories (like node_modules or vendor) makes indexing significantly faster.
  • Relevance: Excluding tests, build artifacts, or generated code keeps your knowledge graph focused on source code.
  • Security: Ensure sensitive configuration files aren't indexed (though everything remains local).

docs.title: File Specification
docs.text:
• Filename: .cgcignore
  • Location: Root of your project (where you run cgc index).
  • Syntax: Standard glob patterns (same as git).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Create a file named .cgcignore in your project root with the following content:

# Dependency directories
node_modules/
venv/
.venv/
__pycache__/

# Build artifacts
dist/
build/
target/
*.egg-info/

# Tests (Optional - if you only want source code)
tests/
spec/
**/*_test.py
**/*.test.js

# Sensitive files
.env
config.js
secrets.json

# Documentation
docs/
*.md

docs.title: CLI: Analysis & Querying
docs.text: These commands allow you to extract insights from your indexed code.

docs.title: analyze callers
docs.text:
Finds every function that calls a specific function. Essential for "Impact Analysis" before refactoring.

Usage: 

cgc analyze callers <func_name>

docs.title: analyze calls
docs.text:
The reverse of callers. Shows what a specific function calls.

Usage: 

cgc analyze calls <func_name>

docs.title: analyze chain
docs.text:
Connects the dots. Finds the path of execution between two functions.

Usage: 

cgc analyze chain <start_func> <end_func> --depth 5

docs.title: analyze deps
docs.text:
Shows dependencies and imports for a specific module.

Usage: 

cgc analyze deps <module>

docs.title: analyze tree
docs.text:
Visualizes the Class Inheritance hierarchy for a given class.

Usage: 

cgc analyze tree <class_name>

docs.title: analyze complexity
docs.text:
Finds functions that are difficult to maintain (Cyclomatic Complexity).

Usage: 

cgc analyze complexity --threshold 10

docs.title: analyze dead-code
docs.text:
Finds potentially unused functions (0 callers).

Usage: 

cgc analyze dead-code --exclude "@route"

docs.title: analyze overrides
docs.text:
Shows methods that override parent class methods.

Usage: 

cgc analyze overrides <class_name>

docs.title: analyze variable
docs.text:
Analyzes where a variable is defined and used.

Usage: 

cgc analyze variable <var_name>

docs.title: find pattern
docs.text:
Fuzzy search for code elements. Use this when you don't know the exact name.

Usage: 

cgc find pattern <text>

docs.title: find name
docs.text:
Finds code elements (Class, Function) by their exact name.

Usage: 

cgc find name <name>

docs.title: find type
docs.text:
List all nodes of a specific type.

Usage: 

cgc find type <type>

Supported Types:

  • class: Find all classes.
  • function: Find all functions/methods.
  • module: Find all indexed files/modules.

Example: 

# Find all classes in the codebase
cgc find type class

docs.title: find content
docs.text:
Full-text search across your source code and docstrings.

Usage: 

cgc find content "search term"

docs.title: find decorator
docs.text:
Finds all functions that are decorated with a specific decorator.

Usage: 

cgc find decorator @app.route

docs.title: find argument
docs.text:
Finds all functions that define a specific argument name.

Usage: 

cgc find argument user_id

docs.title: CLI: Indexing & Management
docs.text: These commands are the foundation of CodeGraphContext. They allow you to add, remove, and monitor the code repositories in your graph.

docs.title: cgc index
docs.text:
Adds a code repository to the graph database. This is the first step for any project.

Excluding Files (.cgcignore)

Want to skip specific files or folders? CodeGraphContext supports a .cgcignore file. 📄 Read the .cgcignore Guide

Usage: 

cgc index [path] [options]

Common Options:

  • path: The folder to index (default: current directory).
  • --force: Re-index from scratch, even if it looks unchanged.

Example: 

# Index the current folder
$ cgc index .

# Index a specific project
$ cgc index /home/user/projects/backend-api

docs.title: cgc list
docs.text:
Shows all repositories currently stored in your graph database.

Usage: 

cgc list

Example Output: 

Indexed Repositories:
1. /home/user/projects/backend-api (Nodes: 1205)
2. /home/user/projects/frontend-ui (Nodes: 850)

docs.title: cgc watch
docs.text:
Starts a real-time monitor. If you edit a file, the graph updates instantly.

Foreground Process

This command runs in the foreground. Open a new terminal tab to keep it running.

Usage: 

cgc watch [path]

Example: 

$ cgc watch .
[INFO] Watching /home/user/projects/backend-api for changes...
[INFO] Detected change in users/models.py. Re-indexing...

docs.title: cgc delete
docs.text:
Removes a repository from the database. This does not delete your actual files, only the graph index.

Usage: 

cgc delete [path] [options]

Common Options:

  • --all: Dangerous. Wipes the entire database.

docs.title: cgc bundle Commands
docs.text: Tools for managing portable graph snapshots (.cgc files).

docs.title: cgc bundle export
docs.text:
Save your graph to a file. Useful for sharing context with team members or loading into a production read-only instance. 

cgc bundle export my-graph.cgc --repo /path/to/repo

docs.title: cgc bundle load
docs.text:
Download and install a popular library bundle from our registry. (Alias: cgc load)

cgc load flask

docs.title: cgc registry
docs.text:
Search for available pre-indexed bundles in the cloud registry. Usage: cgc registry [query]

# List top bundles
cgc registry

# Search for a specific package
cgc registry pandas

docs.title: Comprehensive CLI Reference
docs.text: This page lists every single command available in CodeGraphContext.

docs.title: Indexing & Management
docs.text: Command Description Full Details cgc index Adds a directory to the code graph. details cgc list Lists all indexed repositories. details cgc delete Removes a repository from the graph. details cgc watch Monitors a directory for real-time updates. details cgc clean Removes orphaned nodes from the DB. - cgc stats Show node count statistics. -

docs.title: Code Analysis
docs.text: Command Description Full Details cgc analyze callers Show what functions call X. details cgc analyze calls Show what functions X calls (callees). details cgc analyze chain Show path between function A and B. details cgc analyze deps Show imports/dependencies for a module. details cgc analyze tree Show class inheritance hierarchy. details cgc analyze complexity Find complex functions (Cyclomatic). details cgc analyze dead-code Find unused functions. details cgc analyze overrides Find method overrides in subclasses. details cgc analyze variable Find variable usage across files. details

docs.title: Discovery & Search
docs.text: Command Description Full Details cgc find name Find element by exact name. details cgc find pattern Fuzzy search (substring). details cgc find type List all Class/Function nodes. details cgc find variable Find variables by name. details cgc find content Full-text search in source code. details cgc find decorator Find functions with @decorator. details cgc find argument Find functions with specific arg. details

docs.title: System & Configuration
docs.text: Command Description Full Details cgc doctor Run system health check. details cgc mcp setup Configure AI clients. details cgc neo4j setup Configure Neo4j database. details cgc config View or modify settings. details cgc bundle export Save graph to .cgc file. details cgc bundle load Load graph from file/registry. details cgc registry Browse cloud bundles. details

docs.title: CLI: System & Configuration
docs.text: Commands to manage the CodeGraphContext engine itself.

docs.title: cgc doctor
docs.text:
Self-diagnostic tool. Runs a health check on your installation.

Checks performed:

  • Database connectivity (Neo4j / FalkorDB).
  • Python version compatibility.
  • Required dependencies.

Usage: 

cgc doctor

docs.title: cgc mcp setup
docs.text:
The interactive wizard for configuring AI clients.

What it does:

  1. Detects installed AI Clients (Cursor, VS Code, Claude).
  2. Creates the necessary config files (e.g., mcp.json).
  3. Generates a .env file with database credentials.

Usage: 

cgc mcp setup

docs.title: cgc neo4j setup
docs.text:
The interactive wizard for configuring the graph database backend.

What it does:

  • Docker: Pulls and runs the official Neo4j image.
  • Local: Helps locate a local installation.
  • Remote: Configures credentials for AuraDB.

Usage: 

cgc neo4j setup

docs.title: cgc config Commands
docs.text:
Directly modify settings without editing text files.

  • cgc config show: Print current configuration.
  • cgc config set <key> <value>: Update a setting.
    • Example: cgc config set DEFAULT_DATABASE neo4j
  • cgc config db <backend>: Switch backends (shortcut).
    • Example: cgc config db falkordb

docs.title: Configuration & Settings
docs.text: CodeGraphContext is highly configurable through environment files and the CLI.

docs.title: cgc config Command
docs.text: View and modify settings directly from the terminal.

docs.title: 1. View Settings
docs.text:
Shows the current effective configuration (merged from defaults and .env).

cgc config show

docs.title: 2. Set a Value
docs.text:
Update a setting permanently. This writes to ~/.codegraphcontext/.env.

Syntax: cgc config set <KEY> <VALUE>

# Switch to Neo4j backend
cgc config set DEFAULT_BACKEND neo4j

# Increase max file size to index (MB)
cgc config set MAX_FILE_SIZE_MB 20

# Enable automatic watching after index
cgc config set ENABLE_AUTO_WATCH true

docs.title: 3. Quick Switch Database
docs.text:
A shortcut to toggle between falkordb and neo4j.

cgc config db neo4j

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: Here are the available settings you can configure.

docs.title: Core Settings
docs.text: Key Default Description DEFAULT_BACKEND falkordb The database engine to use (neo4j or falkordb). ENABLE_AUTO_WATCH false If true, cgc index will automatically start watching for changes. PARALLEL_WORKERS 4 Number of parallel threads to use during indexing. CACHE_ENABLED true Caches file hashes to speed up re-indexing.

docs.title: Indexing Scope
docs.text: Key Default Description MAX_FILE_SIZE_MB 5 Files larger than this (in MB) are skipped. IGNORE_TESTS false If true, skips folders named tests or spec. IGNORE_HIDDEN true Skips hidden files (.git, .vscode). INDEX_VARIABLES true Creates nodes for variables. Set to false for a smaller graph.

docs.title: Database Connection (Neo4j)
docs.text: Key Description NEO4J_URI Connection URI (e.g., bolt://localhost:7687). NEO4J_USERNAME Database user (default: neo4j). NEO4J_PASSWORD Database password.

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
CodeGraphContext uses the following hierarchy:

  1. Project Level: .cgcignore in your project root (files to exclude).
  2. User Level: ~/.codegraphcontext/.env (global settings).
  3. Defaults: Built-in application defaults.

To reset everything to defaults: 

cgc config reset

docs.title: MCP Reference & Natural Language Queries
docs.text:
This page lists all available MCP Tools that your AI assistant (Cursor, Claude, VS Code) can use.

When you ask a question in natural language, the AI selects one of these tools behind the scenes.

File Exclusion

You can control what gets indexed using .cgcignore. 📄 Read the Guide

docs.title: Core Analysis Tools
docs.text:
These are the most commonly used tools for understanding code.

Tool Name Description Natural Language Example find_code Search for code by name or fuzzy text. "Where is the User class defined?" analyze_code_relationships The swiss-army knife for call graphs and dependencies. "Find all callers of process_payment." calculate_cyclomatic_complexity Measure function complexity. "What is the complexity of main?" find_most_complex_functions List the hardest-to-maintain functions. "Show me the 5 most complex functions." find_dead_code Identify unused functions. "Find dead code, but ignore @route."

docs.title: System & Management
docs.text:
Tools for managing the graph and background jobs.

Tool Name Description Natural Language Example monitor_directory Start monitoring a folder (Alias: watch_directory) "Watch the src folder." list_watched_paths See what is being monitored. "What directories are being watched?" unwatch_directory Stop monitoring a folder. "Stop watching src." list_indexed_repositories Show what projects are currently indexed. "What repos are indexed?" get_repository_stats Show counts of files, classes, LOC. "Show stats for the backend repo." delete_repository Remove a repo from the graph. "Remove the frontend repo." add_code_to_graph Manually add a specific path. "Add the lib folder." add_package_to_graph Index an external library/package. "Add the requests library."

docs.title: Job Control
docs.text: Tool Name Description Natural Language Example list_jobs View all background tasks. "Show me active jobs." check_job_status Check if a specific job is done. "Is job xyz finished?"

docs.title: Bundles & Registry
docs.text: Tool Name Description Natural Language Example search_registry_bundles Find shared graphs in the cloud. "Search for a flask bundle." load_bundle Install a graph bundle. "Load the flask bundle."

docs.title: Advanced Querying
docs.text:
For complex questions that standard tools can't answer.

Tool Name Description Natural Language Example execute_cypher_query Run a raw read-only database query. "Find all recursive functions." visualize_graph_query Generate a Neo4j Browser link for a query. "Visualize the class hierarchy of BaseModel."

docs.title: Example Queries (Cookbook)
docs.text:
For a deep dive into exactly how to phrase questions and what JSON arguments look like, check out the Cookbook.

📖 View the MCP Cookbook

docs.title: 1. "cgc: command not found"
docs.text: Cause: The folder where pip installs scripts is not in your system PATH. Fix: * Linux/Mac: Add export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" to your .bashrc or .zshrc. * Windows: Reinstall Python and check "Add to PATH".

docs.title: 2. "Connection Refused" (Neo4j)
docs.text:
Cause: The Neo4j container is not running. Fix: 

docker start cgc-neo4j

docs.title: 3. "Import Error: FalkorDB"
docs.text: Cause: You are trying to use FalkorDB on Windows, or heavily outdated Python. Fix: Switch to Neo4j, or upgrade to Python 3.12+ (WSL).

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text: If these didn't solve it, please open an issue on GitHub with the output of cgc doctor.

docs.title: CodeGraph Documentation
docs.text: CodeGraph is a graph-based context selection engine for AI coding agents. It parses Python repositories into dependency graphs, ranks code relevance using Personalized PageRank (PPR), and exposes results via an MCP server that any AI agent can plug into.

docs.title: How to Read These Docs
docs.text:
This wiki is organized by how you'll use the project:

If you want to... Go to Set up the project for the first time Getting Started → Understand how the system works Architecture → Deep dive into a concept (PPR, parsing, etc.) Concepts → Look up config options or MCP tools Reference → Connect to Claude Code or Cursor MCP Setup Guide → Run the evaluation benchmark Evaluation Guide → See what's planned for the future Future Work → Look up a term Glossary →

docs.title: The Pipeline in One Diagram
docs.text:
Python Codebase
      │
      ▼
 tree-sitter                ← parses source files into ASTs
      │
      ▼
Entity Extraction           ← pulls out functions, classes, methods, imports, calls
      │
      ▼
Import & Call Resolution    ← connects entities across files
      │
      ▼
Neo4j Graph                 ← stores nodes + edges with weights
      │
      ▼
Seed Selection              ← identifies starting nodes from task description
      │
      ▼
Personalized PageRank       ← ranks every node by structural relevance to seeds
      │
      ▼
Post-Processing             ← IDF reweighting + token budgeting
      │
      ▼
MCP Server                  ← returns top-k code snippets to the AI agent

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
• Prerequisites — Neo4j, Python 3.12+, GDS plugin
  • Installation — pip install + config
  • Quickstart — first run walkthrough

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
• Graph Model — nodes, edges, schema
  • Parsing & Extraction — tree-sitter, ASTs, entity extraction
  • Import & Call Resolution — cross-file connection building
  • Personalized PageRank — the core ranking algorithm
  • Seed Selection — multi-signal seed extraction
  • Post-Processing — IDF weighting + token budgeting

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
• Configuration — config.yaml full reference
  • MCP Tools — tool schemas, parameters, examples
  • Cypher Queries — useful Neo4j queries
  • Troubleshooting — common issues and fixes

docs.title: Guides
docs.text:
• MCP Setup — connect to Claude Code, Cursor, Claude Desktop
  • Building the Graph — indexing a repository
  • Evaluation — running the SWE-bench benchmark

docs.title: Project Links
docs.text:
• Source code: src/ — core implementation
  • Tests: tests/ — pytest suite (238 tests)
  • Scripts: scripts/ — CLI tools and demos
  • Config: config.yaml — global configuration
  • Decisions: DECISIONS.md — architectural decision log

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: CodeGraph is a pipeline system. Data flows linearly through stages, each producing output consumed by the next. Understanding the architecture means understanding what each stage does, what it produces, and where the code lives.

docs.title: High-Level Diagram
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Python Repository                   │
│            (source files on disk)                   │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │  STAGE 1: PARSE  │
              │  tree-sitter     │   src/parser/python_parser.py
              │  AST per file    │   src/parser/extractors.py
              └────────┬────────┘
                       │  FileEntities per file
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │ STAGE 2: BUILD   │
              │  GRAPH           │   src/graph/graph_builder.py
              │  Neo4j + GDS     │   src/graph/connection.py
              └────────┬────────┘
                       │  Graph ready in Neo4j
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │ STAGE 3: QUERY   │
              │  Seed Selection  │   src/retrieval/seed_selection.py
              │  PPR Algorithm   │   src/graph/ppr.py
              │  Post-Processing │   src/retrieval/post_processing.py
              └────────┬────────┘
                       │  Ranked code snippets
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │  STAGE 4: SERVE  │
              │  MCP Server      │   src/mcp_server.py
              └─────────────────┘
                       │
              ┌────────▼────────┐
              │   AI Agent       │
              │ (Claude, Cursor) │
              └─────────────────┘

docs.title: Stage 1 — Parsing (src/parser/)
docs.text:
File Responsibility python_parser.py Entry point: parse_file(), parse_directory(), create_parser() extractors.py Walks AST nodes, extracts functions/classes/methods/imports/calls models.py Data models: FileEntities, FunctionEntity, ClassEntity, MethodEntity, ImportEntity, CallEntity node_utils.py tree-sitter node traversal helpers

Input: A path to a .py file or directory Output: list[FileEntities] — one per file, containing all extracted entities

docs.title: Stage 2 — Graph Building (src/graph/)
docs.text:
File Responsibility graph_builder.py build_graph() — loads all nodes and edges into Neo4j; clear_database() connection.py create_driver(), verify_connectivity(), load_full_config() queries.py Read-only graph queries: node lookup, neighbor queries, stats ppr.py GDS projection management and PPR execution utils.py Shared graph utilities

Input: list[FileEntities] + Neo4j connection Output: Populated Neo4j database with node and edge weights

docs.title: Stage 3 — Retrieval (src/retrieval/)
docs.text:
File Responsibility seed_selection.py extract_seeds() — combines entity match, BM25, and current-file signals into a personalization vector post_processing.py apply_idf_weights(), format_context(), count_tokens() pipeline.py run_retrieval_pipeline() — orchestrates seed extraction → PPR → post-processing

Input: Task description string + optional entity hints + optional current file Output: List of ContextResult objects (entity, source code, relevance score)

docs.title: Stage 4 — MCP Server (src/mcp_server.py)
docs.text:
Single file exposing three tools over the Model Context Protocol:

Tool Purpose get_relevant_context Full pipeline: seeds → PPR → formatted code snippets query_dependencies Raw graph query — what does entity X call/import? get_graph_stats Codebase statistics for the AI agent

Transport: stdio (local) for Claude Code / Cursor integration Entry point: python -m src.mcp_server

docs.title: Data Flow: A Concrete Example
docs.text:
A developer asks Claude Code: "Fix the authentication timeout bug."

1. Claude calls: get_relevant_context(
       task_description="fix the authentication timeout bug",
       mentioned_entities=["authenticate", "timeout", "AuthService"]
   )

2. Seed selection:
   - Entity match:  finds AuthService.authenticate in graph  → weight 0.6
   - BM25 search:   finds timeout_handler, token_validator    → weight 0.3
   - Current file:  no file provided                          → skipped

3. Personalization vector:
   { AuthService.authenticate: 0.50, timeout_handler: 0.25, token_validator: 0.25 }

4. PPR runs from these seeds, returns top 20 nodes with scores

5. Post-processing:
   - IDF reweighting reduces generic utility functions (log_error, etc.)
   - Token budgeting reads source code, fills up to 6,000 tokens

6. Result returned to Claude:
   [
     { entity: "AuthService.authenticate", score: 0.15, source_code: "..." },
     { entity: "TokenManager.validate_token", score: 0.09, source_code: "..." },
     ...
   ]

7. Claude uses this context to write the fix

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
Decision Choice Why Graph database Neo4j + GDS Built-in PPR, Cypher queries, free for development Parser tree-sitter Fast, error-tolerant, industry-standard Algorithm Personalized PageRank Multi-hop, ranked, mathematically principled Edge direction UNDIRECTED AI agents need both upstream (callers) and downstream (callees) context MCP transport stdio Zero-config for local Claude Code / Cursor setup Node identity file_path::qualified_name Prevents name collisions across files

See DECISIONS.md for the full decision log.

docs.title: Module Boundaries
docs.text:
Each module has a single job and can be tested independently:

parser/     → has no knowledge of Neo4j
graph/      → has no knowledge of retrieval
retrieval/  → has no knowledge of MCP
mcp_server  → thin orchestration layer, no business logic

Dependencies flow in one direction: mcp_server → retrieval → graph + parser.

docs.title: Future Work & Limitations
docs.text: This page is an honest assessment of what CodeGraph currently cannot do well, and what would meaningfully improve it.

docs.title: 1. Static Analysis Only
docs.text:
CodeGraph analyzes code structure at rest — it cannot see dynamic behavior. This means:

  • Monkey-patching is invisible (obj.method = other_function creates a runtime edge that doesn't appear in the graph)
  • eval() and exec() calls are completely unresolvable
  • Dynamic imports (importlib.import_module(config.name)) cannot be followed
  • Duck typing means many method calls remain unresolved without type inference

Impact: ~5–20% of connections in typical Python codebases are unreachable via static analysis. PPR still works — it just won't traverse those missing edges.

docs.title: 2. Python Only
docs.text:
The parser and entity extraction are Python-specific. The architecture is designed to be extensible to other languages (tree-sitter supports 100+), but the current implementation only handles .py files.

What would be needed for another language: A new language-specific extractor module (replacing extractors.py), handling that language's import syntax and entity types.

docs.title: 3. Imperfect Import Resolution
docs.text:
The custom import resolver handles ~80–90% of imports in well-structured Python projects. It fails on:

  • Complex __init__.py re-export chains
  • Namespace packages (PEP 420)
  • Conditional imports (if TYPE_CHECKING: ...)
  • sys.path manipulation at runtime

Potential fix: Use jedi (a Python type inference library) as a fallback for unresolved imports. This would push resolution close to 100% but adds complexity.

docs.title: 4. No Incremental Updates
docs.text:
When code changes, the entire graph must be rebuilt from scratch. A 5–30 second rebuild is fast for evaluation but would be disruptive in a live coding assistant.

What incremental updates would require: - File change detection (file hashing, filesystem watcher) - Partial graph deletion (remove nodes/edges for changed files) - Partial rebuild (re-parse and re-resolve only changed files) - Projection invalidation and recreation

docs.title: 5. Utility Function Noise
docs.text:
IDF reweighting reduces utility function noise but doesn't eliminate it. Functions like validate() or process() that are moderately connected (not enough to be strongly downweighted) may still appear in results when they're not relevant.

Potential fix: Domain-specific function blocklist, or learned weights from evaluation feedback.

docs.title: 6. PPR May Not Always Win
docs.text:
PPR is the thesis's primary contribution, but it's not guaranteed to outperform simpler baselines on all tasks. For tasks that are very localized (one function, obvious name), BM25 alone may be sufficient. For very sparse graphs (under-resolved imports/calls), 1-hop ego-graphs may perform comparably.

Evaluation ablations will quantify this.

docs.title: 7. Imperfect Call Resolution
docs.text: Without type inference, obj.method() calls where obj is a local variable of unknown type cannot be resolved. This affects method call edges significantly — particularly in code that uses dependency injection or factory patterns.

docs.title: Near-Term (Thesis Scope)
docs.text:
Evaluation completion (Stage 6) Run SWE-bench Lite evaluation across all 300 instances and ablation variants. This is the primary remaining milestone.

CLI entry point A simple command-line interface to build the graph without writing Python code: 

codegraph index /path/to/repo
codegraph query "fix the authentication timeout"
codegraph stats

Better BM25 corpus Currently BM25 searches function signatures + docstrings. Including function bodies (stripped of syntax) would improve recall for tasks where the relevant functions don't have descriptive names.

docs.title: Medium-Term (Post-Thesis)
docs.text:
Incremental graph updates File-change detection + partial graph rebuilding. This would enable real-time CodeGraph in a live coding assistant without the 5–30 second rebuild delay.

Multi-language support Add JavaScript/TypeScript support (largest potential user base after Python). The graph schema and PPR algorithm are language-agnostic; only the parser and extractor would need new implementations.

Jedi fallback for import resolution Using jedi as a fallback for the ~15% of imports the custom resolver misses would significantly improve graph completeness for complex repositories.

Semantic similarity as an additional seed signal Currently seeds are based on exact name matching and BM25 keywords. A semantic embedding model could find entities that are semantically similar to the task description even when terminology doesn't overlap — e.g., finding calculate_sum() when the task mentions "add numbers."

docs.title: Long-Term (Research Directions)
docs.text:
Type-inference-aware call resolution Integrating a lightweight type inference pass (using pyright or mypy's type inference engine) would resolve method calls on typed objects, dramatically improving graph completeness.

Graph attention for PPR personalization Instead of hand-tuned seed weights (entity_match: 0.6, BM25: 0.3, current_file: 0.1), a learned model could predict optimal personalization vectors from task descriptions and codebase characteristics.

Cross-repository transfer Building a universal code knowledge graph that spans multiple repositories — useful for understanding library usage patterns across projects.

Real-time graph streaming As the developer types, update the graph incrementally (at the AST diff level) so that the retrieval system always reflects the current code state.

docs.title: Comparison to Related Systems
docs.text:
Feature CodeGraph CodeGraphContext RepoGraph LocAgent Algorithm PPR Simple graph traversal k-hop ego-graph BFS + reranking Database Neo4j FalkorDB / Neo4j In-memory No persistent graph Languages Python Python, JS, Go, Java... Python Python MCP support Yes Yes No No Incremental updates No Yes (watcher) No No Evaluation SWE-bench Not published SWE-bench SWE-bench

CodeGraph's differentiator is the PPR algorithm with IDF edge reweighting — a more principled approach to relevance ranking than k-hop ego-graphs or simple BFS.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Key terms used throughout the CodeGraph documentation and codebase.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) A tree representation of source code's syntactic structure. Each node represents a language construct (function definition, variable assignment, function call, etc.). tree-sitter produces ASTs from Python source files.

Alpha (α) The restart probability in Personalized PageRank. With probability α, the random walker teleports back to the seed nodes instead of following an edge. Default: 0.15. Controls how tightly results cluster around seeds.

Ablation study An experiment that removes or changes one component of a system to measure that component's contribution. Example: running PPR without CALLS edges to measure how much call relationships contribute to retrieval quality.

docs.title: B
docs.text:
BM25 (Best Match 25) A text ranking algorithm from information retrieval. Given a query and a set of documents, BM25 ranks documents by how well they match the query — considering term frequency, document length, and inverse document frequency. Used in CodeGraph for keyword-based seed extraction.

Base weight The starting edge weight before IDF adjustment. Base weights by edge type: INHERITS_FROM=1.0, CALLS=0.8, IMPORTS=0.5, CONTAINS=0.4.

docs.title: C
docs.text:
Call resolution The process of connecting a function call site (e.g., hash_password(pw) inside authenticate()) to the actual function definition (e.g., hash_password in src/utils/crypto.py). One of the two resolution problems — the other is import resolution.

Cypher Neo4j's query language for graph databases. Analogous to SQL but for graph queries. Pattern-matching syntax: MATCH (a)-[:CALLS]->(b) finds all pairs where a calls b.

ContextResult The output type of the retrieval pipeline. Contains: entity_name, qualified_name, file_path, line_number, source_code, relevance_score.

CONTAINS edge A graph edge representing structural containment: File → Class (the file defines this class), File → Function, or Class → Method.

docs.title: D
docs.text:
Damping factor In PPR/PageRank: damping_factor = 1 − α. The probability of continuing the random walk at each step. Neo4j GDS uses damping factor instead of alpha. Default: 0.85.

Dependency graph A graph where nodes are code entities and edges represent dependencies between them (calls, imports, inheritance). CodeGraph builds a dependency graph for Python repositories.

docs.title: E
docs.text:
Edge A connection between two nodes in the graph. In CodeGraph: CONTAINS, IMPORTS, CALLS, and INHERITS_FROM. Edges have a weight property used by PPR.

Entity A named code element extracted from source files. The four entity types in CodeGraph: File, Class, Function, Method.

Entity extraction The process of walking an AST and pulling out named entities and their properties. Implemented in src/parser/extractors.py.

docs.title: F
docs.text:
FileEntities The data model produced by parsing a single Python file. Contains lists of FunctionEntity, ClassEntity, MethodEntity, ImportEntity, and CallEntity objects.

Fan-in The number of incoming edges to a node. High fan-in = many things depend on this entity. Used in IDF reweighting: high fan-in nodes get their incoming edges downweighted.

docs.title: G
docs.text:
GDS (Graph Data Science) Neo4j's plugin library providing graph algorithms including Personalized PageRank. Requires a separate installation. The GDS Python client (graphdatascience) wraps the plugin's REST API.

GDS Projection An in-memory copy of a subset of the Neo4j graph, optimized for algorithm execution. Created once per graph; PPR can run many times against the same projection. Named "codegraph" in this project.

docs.title: I
docs.text:
IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) A text retrieval concept: terms appearing in many documents are less informative. Applied to graphs: edges to highly-connected nodes (high in-degree) are downweighted. Used to reduce utility function noise.

Import resolution The process of turning a Python import string (e.g., "src.utils.crypto") into an actual file path on disk (src/utils/crypto.py). One of the two resolution problems.

In-degree The number of incoming edges to a node. A function with in-degree 200 is called by 200 other functions — it's likely a utility function and gets IDF-downweighted.

docs.title: M
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) An open standard (Anthropic, November 2024) for communication between AI applications and external tools/data sources. CodeGraph implements an MCP server exposing three tools: get_relevant_context, query_dependencies, get_graph_stats.

Method A function defined inside a class body. Stored separately from standalone Function entities. Qualified name format: file_path::ClassName.method_name.

MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) An evaluation metric: the average of 1/rank of the first correct result across all queries. MRR = 1.0 means the correct answer always appears first. MRR = 0.5 means it appears at rank 2 on average.

docs.title: N
docs.text:
Neo4j A graph database that stores data as nodes and edges. CodeGraph uses Neo4j as its persistent store for the code dependency graph. Neo4j Desktop provides a free development license.

Node A code entity in the graph: File, Class, Function, or Method. Nodes have properties (name, file_path, line_number, etc.) and a unique qualified_name.

docs.title: P
docs.text:
PageRank An algorithm (Google, 1998) that ranks nodes in a graph by simulating a random walker who follows edges and occasionally teleports to random nodes. The visit frequency converges to an importance score for each node.

Personalization vector The weighted distribution of probability mass across seed nodes used to initialize PPR. Weights sum to 1.0. Nodes with higher weight get more starting probability mass and pull the walk more strongly.

Personalized PageRank (PPR) A variant of PageRank where teleportation goes to specific seed nodes (not random nodes). Produces node rankings that reflect structural relevance to the seeds, not global importance.

Post-processing The two steps applied to PPR results before returning them to the AI agent: (1) IDF edge reweighting to reduce utility function noise, and (2) token budgeting to fit results within a token limit.

PPRConfig A configuration object for PPR parameters: damping_factor, max_iterations, tolerance, top_k.

PPRResult A single result from PPR execution: node qualified name + relevance score.

project_root The root directory of the codebase being indexed, specified in config.yaml. All file paths are relative to this directory.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: Qualified name A globally unique identifier for a code entity, combining the file path and entity name: - Function: src/utils/helpers.py::validate_email - Method: src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate - Class: src/models/user.py::User

docs.title: R
docs.text:
Recall@k An evaluation metric: the fraction of relevant results (gold files) that appear in the top-k retrieved results. Recall@10 = 0.70 means 70% of the files that needed changing appeared in the top 10 results.

Resolution rate The percentage of imports (or calls) that the resolver successfully maps to a graph entity. Reported during graph building. Typical rates: 80–90% for imports, 60–80% for calls.

docs.title: S
docs.text:
Seed nodes The starting nodes for PPR. Seeds are extracted from the task description, explicitly mentioned entities, and optionally the current file. Seeds with high weight pull the PPR walk toward their region of the graph.

Seed selection The multi-signal process of identifying which graph nodes to use as PPR seeds. Three signals: entity name matching (weight 0.6), BM25 keyword search (weight 0.3), current file hint (weight 0.1).

SWE-bench Lite A dataset of 300 real Python bug reports with associated repository states and gold patches (the code changes that fixed each bug). Used to evaluate CodeGraph's retrieval quality.

docs.title: T
docs.text:
Token budget The maximum number of tokens of source code to include in the MCP response. Default: 6,000 tokens. PPR results are added in score order until the budget is consumed.

tree-sitter A parser library that converts source code into an Abstract Syntax Tree. Used in CodeGraph to parse Python files. Supports 100+ languages, handles broken code gracefully.

docs.title: U
docs.text: UNDIRECTED projection In GDS, projecting edges as undirected means PPR can flow in both directions along each edge. Used in CodeGraph so that the random walk can find both upstream (callers) and downstream (callees) neighbors of the seed.

docs.title: W
docs.text: Weight (edge) A numeric property on graph edges representing coupling strength. Used by PPR during the random walk — higher weight means more probability mass flows along that edge. Base weights by type: INHERITS_FROM=1.0, CALLS=0.8, IMPORTS=0.5, CONTAINS=0.4, adjusted by IDF.

docs.title: Graph Model
docs.text: CodeGraph represents a Python codebase as a property graph stored in Neo4j. Every code entity becomes a node; every relationship between entities becomes a directed, weighted edge.

docs.title: Node Types
docs.text:
The graph uses 4 node types, chosen to capture the meaningful structural units in Python code:

Label Represents Example File A .py source file src/services/auth_service.py Class A class definition AuthService Function A standalone (top-level) function validate_email() Method A function defined inside a class AuthService.authenticate()

docs.title: Node Properties
docs.text:
File: 

file_path   — absolute path on disk
name        — filename only (e.g., "auth_service.py")
line_count  — total lines in the file

Class: 

name            — class name (e.g., "AuthService")
qualified_name  — file_path + "::" + name
file_path       — which file this class is in
line_number     — where the class definition starts
end_line        — where it ends

Function / Method: 

name            — function name (e.g., "authenticate")
qualified_name  — file_path + "::" + class_name + "." + name  (for methods)
                  file_path + "::" + name                      (for functions)
file_path       — which file this function is in
line_number     — start line
end_line        — end line
signature       — full parameter signature (e.g., "(self, username: str) -> bool")
class_name      — parent class (methods only)

docs.title: Node Identity
docs.text:
Every node is uniquely identified by its qualified_name:

"src/models/user.py::User"                       ← a Class
"src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate"  ← a Method
"src/utils/helpers.py::validate_email"           ← a Function
"src/services/auth_service.py"                   ← a File

This prevents ambiguity when different files define entities with the same name.

docs.title: Edge Types
docs.text:
The graph uses 4 edge types, each representing a different kind of dependency:

Type From → To Meaning Example CONTAINS File → Class, File → Function, Class → Method Structural containment auth_service.py CONTAINS AuthService IMPORTS File → File File-level import dependency auth_service.py IMPORTS user_model.py CALLS Function/Method → Function/Method Direct function call authenticate() CALLS hash_password() INHERITS_FROM Class → Class Class inheritance AuthService INHERITS_FROM BaseService

docs.title: Edge Weights
docs.text:
Every edge carries a numeric weight property used by PPR during the random walk:

Edge Type Base Weight Rationale INHERITS_FROM 1.0 Strongest coupling — base class changes affect all subclasses CALLS 0.8 Direct dependency — callee changes may break caller IMPORTS 0.5 File-level connection — weaker than function-level CONTAINS 0.4 Structural — relevant but not a functional dependency

These base weights are then adjusted by in-degree (IDF reweighting) before the GDS projection is created. See Post-Processing →.

docs.title: A Worked Example
docs.text:
Given this code:

# src/services/auth_service.py
from src.models.user import User
from src.utils.crypto import hash_password

class AuthService(BaseService):
    def authenticate(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool:
        user = User.find_by_username(username)
        return hash_password(password) == user.password_hash

The graph contains:

Nodes: 

(File {name: "auth_service.py"})
(File {name: "user.py"})
(File {name: "crypto.py"})
(Class {name: "AuthService"})
(Class {name: "BaseService"})
(Class {name: "User"})
(Method {name: "authenticate", class_name: "AuthService"})
(Function {name: "hash_password"})
(Method {name: "find_by_username", class_name: "User"})

Edges: 

auth_service.py  -[CONTAINS]->    AuthService
auth_service.py  -[IMPORTS]->     user.py
auth_service.py  -[IMPORTS]->     crypto.py
AuthService      -[CONTAINS]->    authenticate
AuthService      -[INHERITS_FROM]-> BaseService
authenticate     -[CALLS]->       find_by_username
authenticate     -[CALLS]->       hash_password

docs.title: Querying the Graph
docs.text:
Find all methods of a class: 

MATCH (c:Class {name: "AuthService"})-[:CONTAINS]->(m:Method)
RETURN m.name, m.line_number

Find what a function calls: 

MATCH (f:Method {name: "authenticate"})-[:CALLS]->(callee)
RETURN callee.name, callee.file_path

Find all callers of a function: 

MATCH (caller)-[:CALLS]->(f {name: "hash_password"})
RETURN caller.name, caller.file_path

Find all classes that inherit from a base: 

MATCH (child:Class)-[:INHERITS_FROM]->(parent:Class {name: "BaseService"})
RETURN child.name, child.file_path

See Cypher Queries → for a full reference.

docs.title: Schema Design Rationale
docs.text:
This 4+4 schema follows LocAgent (ACL 2025), which found it captures the vast majority of meaningful structural relationships in Python codebases while keeping the graph manageable. Notably:

  • Variables and constants are not modeled as nodes (too numerous, add noise)
  • Decorators are stored as properties, not edges
  • Docstrings are stored on nodes for BM25 text search in seed selection
  • Standard library and third-party imports are not modeled as IMPORTS edges (only project-internal connections matter)

docs.title: Import & Call Resolution
docs.text:
tree-sitter gives us entities within each file. Resolution connects them across files — turning raw strings like "src.utils.helpers" into actual edges in the graph.

This is the trickiest part of the pipeline and the primary source of incomplete graphs.

docs.title: Problem 1: Import Resolution
docs.text:
tree-sitter parses from src.utils.crypto import hash_password and gives us the string "src.utils.crypto". It does not tell us which file on disk this refers to.

Import resolution turns "src.utils.crypto" → src/utils/crypto.py

Without this, we can't create IMPORTS edges because we don't know which files are connected.

docs.title: Problem 2: Call Resolution
docs.text:
Entity extraction tells us: "authenticate() calls something named hash_password." But which hash_password? There may be multiple functions with that name across the codebase.

Call resolution connects a call site (e.g., hash_password(password) in auth_service.py) to the actual function definition (e.g., hash_password in src/utils/crypto.py).

docs.title: Python's Import System (Simplified)
docs.text:
When Python sees from src.utils.crypto import hash_password:

  1. Take src.utils.crypto
  2. Replace dots with slashes: src/utils/crypto
  3. Look for either a module file (src/utils/crypto.py) or a package (src/utils/crypto/__init__.py)
  4. hash_password is then an attribute of that module

Relative imports start with dots: from ..models import User. Each dot means "go up one directory." In src/services/auth_service.py, ..models resolves to src/models.

docs.title: Difficulty Tiers
docs.text:
Category Frequency Example Easy ~80% from src.utils.helpers import validate_email — direct path mapping Medium ~15% Relative imports (from ..models import User), re-exports via __init__.py Impossible ~5% importlib.import_module(config.module_name) — runtime dynamic imports

The impossible cases are logged as unresolved and skipped — returning partial results is better than crashing.

docs.title: The Resolver Algorithm
docs.text:
def _resolve_import_to_file_path(module_path: str, current_file: str, project_root: str) -> str | None:
    if module_path.startswith('.'):
        # Relative import — count leading dots, go up directories
        dots = len(module_path) - len(module_path.lstrip('.'))
        remaining = module_path.lstrip('.')
        base_dir = Path(current_file).parent
        for _ in range(dots - 1):
            base_dir = base_dir.parent
        relative_path = remaining.replace('.', '/')
    else:
        # Absolute import — resolve from project root
        relative_path = module_path.replace('.', '/')
        base_dir = Path(project_root)

    # Try as a module file: src/utils/crypto.py
    candidate = base_dir / (relative_path + '.py')
    if candidate.exists():
        return str(candidate)

    # Try as a package: src/utils/crypto/__init__.py
    candidate = base_dir / relative_path / '__init__.py'
    if candidate.exists():
        return str(candidate)

    # Unresolved — log and continue
    return None

Path boundary check: When matching against known file paths, the resolver checks fp == suffix or fp.endswith("/" + suffix) to avoid false matches on partial path segments.

docs.title: Call Resolution: How It Works
docs.text: Call resolution uses import resolution results as a key input.

docs.title: Resolution Priority
docs.text:
When a call site callee_name appears inside a function in some_file.py:

  1. Import match — Was callee_name imported from another file? If so, resolve to that file's definition.
  2. Same-file match — Is callee_name defined in the same file?
  3. Unique global match — Does only one entity with that name exist in the entire codebase?
  4. Ambiguous / unresolved — Multiple candidates, none clearly correct. Log and skip.

docs.title: Implementation: Entity Lookup
docs.text:
def _build_entity_lookup(all_entities: list[FileEntities]) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    """Returns dict[name → list of qualified_names] — all candidates per name."""
    lookup = {}
    for file_entities in all_entities:
        for func in file_entities.functions + file_entities.methods:
            lookup.setdefault(func.name, []).append(func.qualified_name)
    return lookup

def _build_import_map(file_entities: FileEntities, resolved_imports: dict) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Maps imported names → resolved file path for this file."""
    import_map = {}
    for imp in file_entities.imports:
        if imp.module_path in resolved_imports:
            for name in imp.imported_names:
                import_map[name] = resolved_imports[imp.module_path]
    return import_map

docs.title: Method Call Heuristics
docs.text:
For self.method() calls, the class is known from context, so these resolve cleanly. For obj.method() calls on local variables, the resolver uses:

  • Type hints if present (user: User = ...)
  • Assignment inference (user = User.find_by_id(...) → assume User instance)
  • Otherwise: logged as unresolved

docs.title: Resolution Rates and Expectations
docs.text:
For well-structured Python projects (like those in SWE-bench):

Resolution Type Expected Rate Import resolution ~80–90% Call resolution ~60–80%

These rates are logged during graph building and reported as an honesty metric. PPR works with incomplete graphs — missing edges just mean some connections aren't captured, but the overall ranking is still meaningful.

docs.title: Why Not Use jedi?
docs.text:
jedi is a Python type inference library that can resolve imports and types much more accurately (near 100%). We use a custom resolver instead for two reasons:

  1. Educational value — understanding the resolution algorithm is important for the thesis
  2. Sufficient accuracy — SWE-bench repositories are well-structured; ~80% resolution produces good PPR results

If resolution falls below 70% for a particular repository, jedi can be added as a fallback.

docs.title: Source Files
docs.text:
File Responsibility src/graph/graph_builder.py _resolve_import_to_file_path(), _build_entity_lookup(), _build_import_map(), _resolve_callee(), _resolve_base_class()

The resolution helpers are pure functions with no Neo4j dependencies — they're tested independently in tests/test_graph_resolution.py (31 tests, no Neo4j required).

docs.title: Parsing and Entity Extraction
docs.text: This page covers how CodeGraph reads Python source files and pulls out the entities that become nodes in the graph.

docs.title: The Two-Step Process
docs.text:
Step 1: Parsing — Convert raw source text into a structured Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) using tree-sitter.

Step 2: Extraction — Walk the AST and collect functions, classes, methods, imports, and call sites.

These two steps together produce FileEntities — the structured representation of a single source file, which feeds directly into the graph builder.

docs.title: What Is tree-sitter?
docs.text:
tree-sitter is a parser library that turns source code (text) into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) — a structured representation of the code's syntax.

Think of it like parsing a sentence:

"The dog chased the cat"
→ subject("The dog") + verb("chased") + object("the cat")

tree-sitter does the same for code:

def authenticate(username, password):
    user = find_user(username)
    return create_token(user)

Becomes:

function_definition
  name: identifier → "authenticate"
  parameters: ["username", "password"]
  body:
    assignment → user = call(find_user, [username])
    return_statement → call(create_token, [user])

docs.title: Why tree-sitter?
docs.text: Property Value Speed ~5–10ms per 1,000 lines Error tolerance Handles broken/incomplete code, keeps parsing Multi-language 100+ languages with the same API Industry adoption Used by GitHub, Cursor, Neovim Query language Pattern matching syntax (like regex for ASTs)

docs.title: What tree-sitter Cannot Do
docs.text:
• Cannot resolve imports — sees from utils import helper as text, not as a file reference
  • Cannot determine types — sees obj.method() but doesn't know which class obj is
  • Cannot look across files — parses one file at a time

Cross-file resolution is handled separately. See Import & Call Resolution →.

docs.title: How tree-sitter Is Used in This Project
docs.text:
# src/parser/python_parser.py
import tree_sitter_python as tspython
from tree_sitter import Language, Parser

def create_parser() -> Parser:
    return Parser(Language(tspython.language()))

def parse_file(file_path: str, parser: Parser) -> FileEntities:
    with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
        source = f.read()
    tree = parser.parse(source)
    return extract_entities(tree, file_path, source)

tree-sitter also provides a pattern-matching query language:

; Find every function definition and capture its name
(function_definition name: (identifier) @function.name)

; Find every class definition
(class_definition name: (identifier) @class.name)

; Find function calls
(call function: (identifier) @call.name)

This is how the extractor identifies nodes of interest without manually walking the AST tree.

docs.title: What Gets Extracted
docs.text: From each .py file, the extractor (src/parser/extractors.py) pulls out:

docs.title: Functions (top-level)
docs.text:
def validate_email(email: str) -> bool:
    """Check if the email is valid."""
    ...

Captured: name, file_path, line_number, end_line, signature, docstring

docs.title: Classes
docs.text:
class AuthService(BaseService):
    """Handles user authentication."""
    ...

Captured: name, file_path, line_number, end_line, base_classes

docs.title: Methods (functions inside a class)
docs.text:
class AuthService:
    def authenticate(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool:
        ...

Captured: same as function + class_name

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
from src.utils.crypto import hash_password
import os

Captured: module_path, imported_names, is_relative

Note: import os is captured but filtered out later (standard library = ignored).

docs.title: Call Sites
docs.text:
def authenticate(self, username, password):
    user = find_user(username)         # ← call site
    hashed = hash_password(password)  # ← call site

Captured: caller_name, callee_name, line_number

docs.title: What Gets Skipped
docs.text: Skipped Reason Standard library imports (import json, import os) Not part of the project; adds noise Third-party library calls (requests.get(), np.array()) Only internal connections matter Variables and constants Too numerous; not structurally meaningful Decorators Stored as properties on functions, not separate nodes Type annotations Captured as part of the signature string, not resolved Python built-ins (len, print, isinstance, etc.) Filtered via frozenset(dir(builtins))

docs.title: Output: FileEntities
docs.text:
After extraction, each file produces a FileEntities object:

@dataclass
class FileEntities:
    file_path: str
    functions: list[FunctionEntity]
    classes: list[ClassEntity]
    methods: list[MethodEntity]
    imports: list[ImportEntity]
    calls: list[CallEntity]

All files are processed, producing a list[FileEntities] that feeds into the graph builder.

docs.title: Qualified Names
docs.text:
To uniquely identify entities across the entire codebase, every entity gets a qualified name:

Entity Qualified Name Pattern Example File file_path src/services/auth_service.py Class file_path::ClassName src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService Function file_path::function_name src/utils/helpers.py::validate_email Method file_path::ClassName.method_name src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate

This prevents ambiguity when two files define entities with the same name (e.g., both utils.py and helpers.py define parse()).

docs.title: Source File
docs.text:
The main entry points are in src/parser/python_parser.py:

create_parser() -> Parser              # Create the tree-sitter parser
parse_file(path, parser) -> FileEntities    # Parse a single file
parse_directory(path, parser) -> list[FileEntities]  # Parse all .py files

The extractor logic lives in src/parser/extractors.py. Data models are in src/parser/models.py.

docs.title: Personalized PageRank (PPR)
docs.text: PPR is the core algorithm of CodeGraph. It scores every node in the code graph by its structural relevance to a given set of starting nodes (seeds). This page explains it from scratch.

docs.title: Background: Regular PageRank
docs.text:
PageRank was invented by Google's founders in 1998 to rank web pages.

The intuition: Imagine a random web surfer. They start on a random page, click a link, then another, and so on, forever. Pages they visit most often are the most "important" — because important pages have many incoming links from other important pages.

The teleportation trick: With probability α (typically 0.15), the surfer ignores all links and jumps to a completely random page. This prevents getting stuck in loops and ensures all pages can be reached. The result: every page gets a score (visit frequency). Higher score = more globally important.

docs.title: What Makes PPR Different
docs.text:
In regular PageRank, the teleportation target is any random page with equal probability.

In Personalized PageRank, teleportation goes to specific pages you choose — called seed nodes. This biases the entire walk toward a particular region of the graph.

Algorithm Teleports to Answers PageRank Any random node "What are the most important nodes globally?" PPR Specific seed nodes "What are the most important nodes near my seeds?"

For code: PageRank tells you the most important functions in the entire codebase. PPR from authenticate() tells you the most important functions relative to authenticate() — the ones most structurally connected to it through call chains, imports, and inheritance.

docs.title: How PPR Works Step by Step
docs.text:
Given a code graph and seed nodes (e.g., AuthService.authenticate):

  1. Initialize. Place all probability mass on the seeds. If there are multiple seeds, distribute proportionally to their weights.

  2. Walk. From each node with probability mass, spread it along edges. Each neighbor gets mass / out_degree. But only spread (1 − α) of the mass; keep α as a pull-back to seeds.

  3. α (alpha) = restart probability = 0.15 by default. Controls how far the walk spreads. Higher α = results stay closer to seeds.

  4. Repeat for 20 iterations (configurable). Scores converge.

  5. Read results. The probability mass at each node after convergence = its PPR score = its structural relevance to the seeds.

docs.title: Concrete Example
docs.text:
Starting PPR from authenticate():

authenticate()          → 0.150  (high — it's the seed)
hash_password()         → 0.082  (high — directly called by seed)
check_token()           → 0.067  (high — directly called by seed)
User.find_by_username() → 0.045  (medium — called by seed)
BaseService.__init__()  → 0.023  (medium — inherited by seed's class)
process_login()         → 0.018  (medium — calls the seed)
log_error()             → 0.012  (lower — connected but generic)
send_email()            → 0.001  (very low — barely connected)

The algorithm naturally ranks structurally connected code higher and distant code lower — without any hand-crafted rules.

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text:
