Parameter Meaning Default Effect of Increasing damping_factor (= 1 − α) How far the walk spreads 0.85 Results spread further from seeds max_iterations Precision of convergence 20 More precise (diminishing returns after 20) tolerance Early stopping threshold 1e-7 Lower = more precise but slower top_k How many results PPR returns 20 More results, but more post-processing noise

In Neo4j GDS: dampingFactor = 1 − α. So dampingFactor = 0.85 means 15% restart probability.

docs.title: Why PPR Is the Right Algorithm
docs.text: Approach Problem 1-hop neighbors Misses indirect connections (A→B→C; C is invisible) k-hop ego-graph (used by RepoGraph) k=1 too narrow; k=2 explodes exponentially; no ranking within results Shortest path Doesn't account for multiple connection paths or connection strength Random walk without personalization Returns globally important nodes, not task-relevant ones PPR Multi-hop, ranked, personalized, mathematically principled

docs.title: PPR's Specific Advantages
docs.text:
• Multi-hop: Finds indirectly connected but important code (e.g., base class 3 hops away)
  • Ranked: Every node gets a score → take exactly top-k
  • Multiple paths count: Node X reachable via 5 paths scores higher than Y via 1 path
  • Fast: 1–10ms on 50,000-node graphs in Neo4j GDS

docs.title: Edge Directionality
docs.text:
Edges have natural directions (authenticate() CALLS hash_password() is a directed edge). PPR can follow them in different ways:

Direction What PPR Finds When Useful Natural (follow arrows) Things the seed depends on Understanding implementation Reverse (follow backward) Things that depend on the seed Impact analysis Undirected (both ways) Everything structurally connected General context for AI agents

Decision: UNDIRECTED. An AI agent fixing authenticate() needs to see hash_password() (to understand the implementation) AND process_login() (to know what calls authenticate() and might break). Undirected is strictly more informative.

Evaluation includes directed and reversed variants as ablation experiments to measure whether undirected is actually best.

docs.title: Running PPR in Neo4j GDS
docs.text:
The GDS projection is created once per graph; PPR can then run many times with different seeds:

-- Project the graph into memory (one-time setup)
CALL gds.graph.project(
  'codegraph',
  ['Function', 'Method', 'Class', 'File'],
  {
    CALLS:         {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    IMPORTS:       {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    INHERITS_FROM: {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    CONTAINS:      {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'}
  }
)

-- Run PPR from a seed
MATCH (seed:Method {name: 'authenticate'})
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codegraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [seed],
  relationshipWeightProperty: 'weight'
})
YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS name, score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 20

docs.title: Multiple Seeds with Weights
docs.text:
PPR accepts multiple seed nodes. The personalization vector distributes probability mass across them:

# From src/graph/ppr.py
def run_ppr(
    gds: GraphDataScience,
    personalization: PersonalizationVector,  # dict[qualified_name → weight]
    config: PPRConfig
) -> list[PPRResult]:
    ...

Weights are normalized to sum to 1.0. A seed with weight 0.6 gets 3× the starting probability mass of a seed with weight 0.2.

docs.title: Source Files
docs.text:
File Responsibility src/graph/ppr.py PPRConfig, PPRResult, create_gds_client(), project_graph(), run_ppr() config.yaml ppr section: damping_factor, max_iterations, tolerance, top_k

The GDS Python client accesses the underlying driver via gds._driver (an internal API, used deliberately for query execution).

docs.title: Post-Processing
docs.text: PPR returns a ranked list of nodes with scores. Before these reach the AI agent, two post-processing steps improve quality: IDF-based edge reweighting and token budgeting.

docs.title: What Happens Without This Step
docs.text:
A function like log_error() is called by 200 other functions across the codebase. Because it has 200 incoming edges, PPR from any seed node will rank it highly — it's structurally well-connected. But log_error() is not useful context. It's generic infrastructure.

Analogy: If "Wikipedia" appeared as the #1 search result for every Google query, that's technically correct (Wikipedia is linked from everywhere) but useless.

docs.title: Solution: IDF-Inspired Edge Weight Adjustment
docs.text:
IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) is a concept from text search: terms appearing in many documents are less informative. "The" appears everywhere → low weight. "Authenticate" appears rarely → high weight.

We apply the same logic to graph edges: edges to highly-connected nodes are downweighted.

adjusted_weight(A → B) = base_weight(edge_type) / log₂(in_degree(B) + 2)

Where in_degree(B) = number of incoming edges to node B.

docs.title: The Effect
docs.text:
Function In-degree Weight factor Outcome log_error() 200 base / log₂(202) ≈ base / 7.7 Heavily downweighted hash_password() 3 base / log₂(5) ≈ base / 2.3 Slightly downweighted calculate_token_expiry() 1 base / log₂(3) ≈ base / 1.6 Barely affected

Generic utility functions see much less weight flowing into them via PPR, so they score lower and are less likely to appear in the final top-k.

docs.title: When It's Applied
docs.text:
IDF reweighting is applied to edge weights before the GDS projection is created, not after PPR runs. This means PPR already operates on the adjusted weights — the downweighting is baked into the walk itself.

# src/retrieval/post_processing.py
def apply_idf_weights(
    edges: list[Edge],
    in_degree: dict[str, int]
) -> list[Edge]:
    """Adjust edge weights by in-degree of target node."""
    for edge in edges:
        target_degree = in_degree.get(edge.target, 0)
        edge.weight = edge.weight / math.log2(target_degree + 2)
    return edges

docs.title: The Challenge
docs.text: PPR returns node names with scores. The AI agent needs actual source code, not just names. But we can't send unlimited code — context windows have limits, and too much irrelevant code buries the signal.

docs.title: The Process
docs.text:
1. Take the top-k results from PPR (default: 20 nodes)
  2. For each node, read source code using the stored file_path, line_number, end_line
  3. Count tokens in the source code
  4. Add nodes to the result until the token budget is consumed
  5. Format and return
def format_context(
    ppr_results: list[PPRResult],
    token_budget: int = 6000
) -> list[ContextResult]:
    context_parts = []
    tokens_used = 0

    for node, score in ppr_results:
        source_code = read_lines(node.file_path, node.line_number, node.end_line)
        source_tokens = count_tokens(source_code)

        if tokens_used + source_tokens > token_budget:
            break   # Budget consumed — stop adding

        context_parts.append(ContextResult(
            entity_name=node.name,
            qualified_name=node.qualified_name,
            file_path=node.file_path,
            line_number=node.line_number,
            source_code=source_code,
            relevance_score=round(score, 4)
        ))
        tokens_used += source_tokens

    return context_parts

docs.title: Why 4,000–8,000 Tokens?
docs.text:
The AI agent (Claude, GPT-4, etc.) manages its own context window (100K–200K tokens). CodeGraph's contribution is one part of that context. 4,000–8,000 tokens of highly relevant code is more useful than 50,000 tokens of loosely relevant code — the agent can focus on what matters.

The default token budget is 6,000, configurable in config.yaml.

docs.title: Output: ContextResult
docs.text:
Each item in the final output:

@dataclass
class ContextResult:
    entity_name: str        # e.g., "authenticate"
    qualified_name: str     # e.g., "src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate"
    file_path: str          # e.g., "src/services/auth_service.py"
    line_number: int        # start line in the file
    end_line: int           # end line in the file
    source_code: str        # the actual Python source
    relevance_score: float  # PPR score (higher = more relevant)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
# config.yaml
mcp:
  default_token_budget: 6000   # Max tokens in the MCP response
  default_top_k: 15            # How many PPR results to consider before budgeting

docs.title: Source Files
docs.text: File Responsibility src/retrieval/post_processing.py ContextResult, apply_idf_weights(), format_context(), count_tokens() tests/test_post_processing.py Unit tests (no Neo4j required)

docs.title: Seed Node Selection
docs.text: PPR needs starting nodes (seeds) to determine which part of the code graph to explore. The quality of seeds directly determines the quality of results — bad seeds produce irrelevant context.

docs.title: The Problem: Where Do We Start?
docs.text:
Given a task like "fix the authentication timeout bug," we need to identify the 3–6 most relevant graph nodes to use as PPR seeds.

Naïve approach: Use all nodes in the current file as seeds.

Why this fails: - The developer may have been searching for an hour and landed on an unrelated file - The AI agent may be doing multi-step reasoning, not focusing on one file - "Where the cursor is" ≠ "what the task is about"

docs.title: The Solution: Multi-Signal Seed Extraction
docs.text: Seeds come from three signals with different confidence levels, combined into a single personalization vector.

docs.title: Signal 1: Explicit Entity Mentions (Weight: 0.6)
docs.text:
The AI agent passes a mentioned_entities list when calling the MCP tool. These are entities the agent already identified as relevant from the task description.

get_relevant_context(
    task_description="fix the authentication timeout bug",
    mentioned_entities=["authenticate", "AuthService", "TokenExpiredError"]
)

The system looks up each name in the graph. Exact matches get the highest weight (0.6 of the total personalization vector).

Why highest weight: If the AI agent explicitly names a function, it almost certainly belongs in the seed set.

docs.title: Signal 2: BM25 Keyword Search (Weight: 0.3)
docs.text:
BM25 ranks all graph entities by textual similarity to the task description. The top N results become seeds.

What is BM25? A text retrieval algorithm. Given a query ("authentication timeout") and a set of documents (all function signatures + docstrings in the graph), it ranks documents by how well they match — considering word frequency, document length, and inverse document frequency (rare words matter more).

# Corpus: all function signatures + docstrings
corpus = [
    "authenticate(self, username, password) -> bool",
    "validate_token(token: str) -> bool",
    "hash_password(password: str) -> str",
    ...
]

# Query: the task description
query = "fix authentication timeout bug"

# BM25 ranks the corpus
bm25 = BM25Okapi([doc.split() for doc in corpus])
scores = bm25.get_scores(query.split())
top_5 = sorted(zip(scores, corpus))[-5:]

Why medium weight: BM25 doesn't understand code structure, so it sometimes matches on incidental keywords. It's a useful signal but not as reliable as explicit mentions.

docs.title: Signal 3: Current File Hint (Weight: 0.1)
docs.text:
If the MCP caller provides current_file, all functions/methods in that file are added as weak seeds.

get_relevant_context(
    task_description="fix timeout",
    current_file="src/services/auth_service.py"
)

Why lowest weight: The current file is a noisy signal. The developer might be reading unrelated code. But it's not useless — if the task is "continue working on this file," the current-file signal matters.

docs.title: Building the Personalization Vector
docs.text:
All seed signals are combined and normalized to sum to 1.0:

@dataclass
class SeedNode:
    qualified_name: str   # e.g., "src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate"
    weight: float         # raw weight before normalization

@dataclass
class PersonalizationVector:
    seeds: list[SeedNode]

def extract_seeds(
    task_description: str,
    mentioned_entities: list[str] | None,
    current_file: str | None,
    all_entities: list[FileEntities],
    config: dict
) -> PersonalizationVector:
    ...

Example output:

PersonalizationVector(seeds=[
    SeedNode("auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate",  weight=0.50),
    SeedNode("auth_service.py::AuthService",               weight=0.25),
    SeedNode("token_manager.py::validate_token",           weight=0.12),
    SeedNode("crypto.py::hash_password",                   weight=0.08),
    SeedNode("auth_service.py::AuthService.refresh_token", weight=0.05),
])

Weights sum to 1.0. PPR uses this as the initial probability distribution.

docs.title: Why Multi-Signal Matters
docs.text:
Different scenarios benefit from different signals:

Scenario Dominant Signal Why "Fix bug in authenticate()" Entity match Function name is explicit "The login flow is broken" BM25 No specific function; keywords like "login" guide BM25 "Continue working on auth" Current file Vague task; file context helps SWE-bench bug report text Entity match + BM25 Bug reports often name classes; BM25 captures surrounding keywords

By combining signals, the system degrades gracefully — when one signal is weak, the others compensate.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
# config.yaml
seed_selection:
  entity_match_weight: 0.6   # Weight for explicitly mentioned entities
  bm25_weight: 0.3           # Weight for BM25 top-N matches
  current_file_weight: 0.1   # Weight for entities in the current file
  bm25_top_n: 5              # How many BM25 results to include

docs.title: Source Files
docs.text:
File Responsibility src/retrieval/seed_selection.py SeedNode, PersonalizationVector, extract_seeds() tests/test_seed_selection.py Unit tests for all three signals

The seed selection module has no Neo4j dependency — it works entirely on FileEntities and can be tested without a running database.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page covers installing CodeGraph's Python dependencies and configuring it to connect to Neo4j.

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone <repo-url>
cd codegraph

docs.title: 2. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
Install the package in editable mode with all development dependencies:

python -m pip install -e ".[dev]"

The [dev] extras include pytest and other testing tools. The -e flag installs in editable mode so your source changes are reflected immediately.

Verify the install worked: 

python -c "import tree_sitter; import neo4j; import mcp; print('OK')"

docs.title: 3. Configure Neo4j Connection
docs.text:
Open config.yaml in the project root and update the neo4j section:

neo4j:
  uri: "neo4j://localhost:7687"
  username: "neo4j"
  password: "your_password_here"   # ← change this
  database: "neo4j"

Important: Never commit your real password to version control. The config.yaml is gitignored by default.

docs.title: 4. Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Run the connection check:

python -c "
from src.graph.connection import create_driver, verify_connectivity, load_full_config
config = load_full_config('config.yaml')
driver = create_driver(config['neo4j'])
verify_connectivity(driver)
print('Connected to Neo4j successfully')
driver.close()
"

If this prints Connected to Neo4j successfully, you're ready to go.

docs.title: 5. Run the Tests
docs.text:
Confirm everything works end-to-end:

# Run all tests (requires Neo4j running)
python -m pytest tests/ -v

# Run only tests that don't need Neo4j
python -m pytest tests/test_graph_resolution.py tests/test_seed_selection.py tests/test_post_processing.py -v

Tests that require Neo4j are marked with @neo4j_required and skip gracefully if Neo4j isn't running.

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text:
The full config.yaml with all default values:

project_root: "."
exclude_patterns:
  - "__pycache__"
  - ".venv"
  - ".git"
  - "*.pyc"
  - ".pytest_cache"

neo4j:
  uri: "neo4j://localhost:7687"
  username: "neo4j"
  password: "your_password"
  database: "neo4j"

ppr:
  damping_factor: 0.85       # 1 - alpha; higher = walks stay closer to seed
  max_iterations: 20
  tolerance: 1.0e-7
  top_k: 20                  # How many results PPR returns before post-processing

seed_selection:
  entity_match_weight: 0.6   # Weight for explicitly mentioned entities
  bm25_weight: 0.3           # Weight for BM25 keyword matches
  current_file_weight: 0.1   # Weight for entities in the current file
  bm25_top_n: 5              # How many BM25 results to include as seeds

mcp:
  server_name: "codegraph"
  default_token_budget: 6000  # Max tokens in MCP response
  default_top_k: 15           # Default number of results returned

See Configuration Reference → for detailed explanations of each field.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Quickstart → — build your first graph and run a query.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before running CodeGraph, you need these dependencies set up correctly. This page explains what each one is and how to verify it.

docs.title: 1. Python 3.12+
docs.text:
CodeGraph uses modern Python features (type hints, match statements, tomllib).

Check your version: 

python --version
# Expected: Python 3.12.x or higher

Install: Download from python.org or use pyenv.

docs.title: 2. Neo4j Database
docs.text: CodeGraph stores the code graph in Neo4j, a graph database. You need Neo4j running locally before you can build or query a graph.

docs.title: Option A: Neo4j Desktop (Recommended for Development)
docs.text:
1. Download Neo4j Desktop (free)
  2. Create a new project → Add a local database
  3. Set a password (you'll put this in config.yaml)
  4. Click Start

The database runs at bolt://localhost:7687 by default.

docs.title: Option B: Docker
docs.text:
docker run \
  --name neo4j \
  -p 7474:7474 -p 7687:7687 \
  -e NEO4J_AUTH=neo4j/your_password \
  -e NEO4J_PLUGINS='["graph-data-science"]' \
  neo4j:5

This starts Neo4j with the GDS plugin pre-installed.

docs.title: Verify Neo4j is Running
docs.text: Open http://localhost:7474 in your browser. You should see the Neo4j Browser login page.

docs.title: 3. Neo4j Graph Data Science (GDS) Plugin
docs.text: The GDS plugin provides the Personalized PageRank algorithm that CodeGraph relies on. Without it, the retrieval pipeline won't work.

docs.title: Installing GDS
docs.text:
Neo4j Desktop: 1. Open your database in Neo4j Desktop 2. Go to Plugins tab 3. Find Graph Data Science Library → click Install 4. Restart the database

Docker: Pass -e NEO4J_PLUGINS='["graph-data-science"]' as shown above.

docs.title: Verify GDS is Installed
docs.text:
Run this in the Neo4j Browser (localhost:7474):

RETURN gds.version()

You should see a version string like "2.x.x".

docs.title: 4. Python Dependencies
docs.text:
All Python dependencies are defined in pyproject.toml. Key ones:

Package Purpose neo4j>=5.0 Neo4j Python driver graphdatascience>=1.7 GDS Python client (PPR) tree-sitter>=0.23.0 AST parser tree-sitter-python>=0.23.0 Python grammar for tree-sitter rank-bm25>=0.2.2 BM25 for seed selection mcp>=1.0.0 MCP server SDK pyyaml Config file parsing pathspec .gitignore-style file exclusion

Install everything at once in the next step: Installation →

docs.title: Summary Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Python 3.12 or higher
  • [ ] Neo4j running at localhost:7687
  • [ ] GDS plugin installed and gds.version() returns a value
  • [ ] Project dependencies installed (pip install -e ".[dev]")
  • [ ] config.yaml updated with your Neo4j password

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
This walkthrough takes you from a fresh install to running your first query. By the end, you'll have a code graph built and an MCP query returning relevant context.

Prerequisites: Neo4j running, GDS installed, pip install -e ".[dev]" done.

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Your Setup
docs.text:
python -c "
from src.graph.connection import create_driver, verify_connectivity, load_full_config
config = load_full_config('config.yaml')
driver = create_driver(config['neo4j'])
verify_connectivity(driver)
print('Neo4j: OK')
driver.close()
"

Expected output: Neo4j: OK

docs.title: Step 2: Build a Graph
docs.text:
Use the demo script to index a sample repository. The demo uses the tests/fixtures/ directory (a small hand-crafted Python project):

python -m scripts.demo_neo4j

This will: 1. Parse all .py files in the target directory 2. Extract entities (functions, classes, methods, imports, calls) 3. Resolve imports and calls across files 4. Load nodes and edges into Neo4j 5. Print a summary of what was loaded

Expected output: 

Parsing 7 files...
Extracted: 3 classes, 5 functions, 9 methods
Resolved: 87% of imports, 74% of calls
Loading graph into Neo4j...
Graph built: 24 nodes, 27 edges

docs.title: Step 3: Inspect the Graph
docs.text:
Open the Neo4j Browser at http://localhost:7474 and run:

// See all node types
MATCH (n) RETURN labels(n), count(*) ORDER BY count(*) DESC

// See all edge types
MATCH ()-[r]->() RETURN type(r), count(*) ORDER BY count(*) DESC

// Find all functions and what they call
MATCH (f:Function)-[:CALLS]->(g)
RETURN f.name, g.name LIMIT 20

docs.title: Step 4: Run a Retrieval Query
docs.text:
Use the pipeline directly from Python:

from src.retrieval.pipeline import run_retrieval_pipeline
import yaml

with open('config.yaml') as f:
    config = yaml.safe_load(f)

results = run_retrieval_pipeline(
    task_description="validate user authentication token",
    mentioned_entities=["validate_token", "AuthService"],
    config=config
)

for r in results:
    print(f"[{r.relevance_score:.3f}] {r.entity_name}")
    print(f"  File: {r.file_path}:{r.line_number}")
    print()

docs.title: Step 5: Start the MCP Server
docs.text:
To use CodeGraph with Claude Code or Cursor:

python -m src.mcp_server

The server starts on stdio and waits for MCP client connections.

See MCP Setup Guide → for how to connect this to Claude Code or Cursor.

docs.title: Step 6: Run the Tests
docs.text:
python -m pytest tests/ -v

All 238 tests should pass (with Neo4j running). Tests marked @neo4j_required skip automatically if Neo4j is not available.

docs.title: What Just Happened
docs.text:
scripts/demo_neo4j.py
  → src/parser/python_parser.py    (parse files into FileEntities)
  → src/graph/graph_builder.py     (load into Neo4j)

run_retrieval_pipeline()
  → src/retrieval/seed_selection.py   (extract seeds from task description)
  → src/graph/ppr.py                  (run PPR from seeds)
  → src/retrieval/post_processing.py  (IDF + token budgeting)
  → returns ContextResult list

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
"Connection refused" error: Neo4j isn't running. Start it via Neo4j Desktop or Docker.

"GDS not found" error: The GDS plugin isn't installed. See Prerequisites →.

Tests failing: Run python -m pytest tests/test_graph_resolution.py -v first — these don't need Neo4j and isolate parser issues.

Empty results from pipeline: Check that the graph was built (MATCH (n) RETURN count(n) in Neo4j Browser should be > 0).

See Troubleshooting → for more.

docs.title: Building the Graph
docs.text: This guide explains how to index a Python repository into the CodeGraph Neo4j database. The graph must be built before any retrieval queries can be run.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Building the graph has three steps:

  1. Parse — tree-sitter reads all .py files and extracts entities
  2. Resolve — imports and calls are connected across files
  3. Load — nodes and edges are written to Neo4j (idempotently)

The graph is built once per repository state. Subsequent MCP queries run in under 250ms. When the code changes, rebuild the graph.

docs.title: Option 1: Demo Script (Quickstart)
docs.text:
The demo script indexes a small fixture project and prints a summary. Good for testing that everything works:

python -m scripts.demo_neo4j

This indexes tests/fixtures/simple_project/ — a hand-crafted project designed for testing.

docs.title: Option 2: Rebuild Script (Production)
docs.text:
To index a real repository, use the rebuild script:

python -m scripts.rebuild_graph

This reads project_root from config.yaml, clears the existing graph, rebuilds from scratch, and creates the GDS projection.

Before running: Set project_root in config.yaml to the repository you want to index:

project_root: "/path/to/your/python/project"

docs.title: Option 3: Programmatic Build
docs.text:
For fine-grained control or integration into your own scripts:

import yaml
from src.parser.python_parser import create_parser, parse_directory
from src.graph.connection import create_driver, load_full_config
from src.graph.graph_builder import build_graph, clear_database
from src.graph.ppr import create_gds_client, project_graph

# Load config
config = load_full_config('config.yaml')

# Create connections
driver = create_driver(config['neo4j'])
gds = create_gds_client(config['neo4j'])

# Parse the codebase
parser = create_parser()
all_entities = parse_directory(config['project_root'], parser)

print(f"Parsed {len(all_entities)} files")

# Clear old graph and build new one
clear_database(driver)
build_graph(driver, all_entities)

print("Graph built. Creating GDS projection...")

# Create the in-memory projection for PPR
project_graph(gds)

print("Done.")
driver.close()

docs.title: Phase 1: Parsing
docs.text:
Scanning /path/to/project for .py files...
  Skipping: __pycache__/, .venv/, .git/  (exclude_patterns)
  Found 87 Python files

Parsing with tree-sitter...
  Parsed auth_service.py: 3 classes, 0 functions, 12 methods, 5 imports, 18 calls
  Parsed user_model.py:   1 class,   0 functions,  8 methods, 2 imports, 11 calls
  ...
  Parsed 87 files in 0.8s

docs.title: Phase 2: Resolution
docs.text:
Resolving imports...
  Resolved 312 of 361 imports (86.4%)
  Unresolved: ['requests', 'flask', 'datetime', ...]  (standard library / third party — expected)

Resolving calls...
  Resolved 1,891 of 2,734 calls (69.2%)
  Unresolved: 843 calls (ambiguous or external — logged, skipped)

docs.title: Phase 3: Loading into Neo4j
docs.text:
Loading nodes...
  87 File nodes
  234 Class nodes
  412 Function nodes
  514 Method nodes
  Total: 1,247 nodes loaded

Loading edges...
  1,247 CONTAINS edges
  312   IMPORTS edges
  1,891 CALLS edges
  381   INHERITS_FROM edges
  Total: 3,831 edges loaded

Creating GDS projection 'codegraph'...
  Projection created: 1,247 nodes, 7,662 relationships (undirected)
  Memory used: ~4.2 MB

docs.title: Rebuild After Code Changes
docs.text:
The graph is not automatically updated when code changes. You must rebuild manually.

For a full rebuild: 

python -m scripts.rebuild_graph

For incremental updates (not yet implemented — see Future Work →), you would only rebuild changed files.

When to rebuild: - After any significant refactoring - When adding new files to the project - Before running evaluations - When results feel stale or wrong

docs.title: Checking the Build
docs.text:
After building, verify the graph is populated:

# Via Neo4j Browser
MATCH (n) RETURN labels(n), count(*) ORDER BY count(*) DESC

# Via Python
python -c "
from src.graph.connection import create_driver, load_full_config
config = load_full_config('config.yaml')
driver = create_driver(config['neo4j'])
with driver.session() as session:
    result = session.run('MATCH (n) RETURN count(n) AS total')
    print(f'Total nodes: {result.single()[\"total\"]}')
driver.close()
"

docs.title: Configuring What Gets Indexed
docs.text:
Exclude patterns control what gets skipped:

# config.yaml
exclude_patterns:
  - "__pycache__"
  - ".venv"
  - ".git"
  - "*.pyc"
  - ".pytest_cache"
  - "tests"        # add this to skip test files
  - "docs"         # add this to skip documentation

Pattern matching uses pathspec (gitignore-style syntax). You can use wildcards (*.pyc), directory names (__pycache__), and paths.

docs.title: Performance Reference
docs.text:
Repository Files Nodes Edges Parse Time Load Time Small project (1k LOC) ~10 ~200 ~400 < 0.1s < 1s Medium project (10k LOC) ~100 ~2,000 ~8,000 ~1s ~5s Flask (65k LOC) ~200 ~5,000 ~20,000 ~2s ~10s Django (300k LOC) ~900 ~20,000 ~80,000 ~8s ~40s

These are rough estimates. Actual times depend on hardware and graph complexity.

docs.title: Evaluation Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to evaluate CodeGraph's retrieval quality using the SWE-bench benchmark.

docs.title: What We're Evaluating
docs.text:
The fundamental question: Does PPR retrieve the files that actually need to be changed to fix a bug?

We answer this with SWE-bench Lite — a dataset of 300 real bug reports where we already know which files were changed in the fix. If PPR's top-k results include those files, it "got it right."

Important: We are NOT fixing bugs or running an LLM. We are measuring retrieval recall — how well the system identifies where to look.

docs.title: The Benchmark: SWE-bench Lite
docs.text:
SWE-bench Lite provides 300 curated bug instances from popular Python repositories:

Repository Examples Django ORM bugs, middleware issues Flask Request handling, routing scikit-learn ML algorithm bugs matplotlib Plotting/rendering issues requests HTTP client bugs

For each instance: - Repository at a specific commit (before the fix) - Issue text — the GitHub bug report - Gold patch — the actual code changes that fixed the bug (ground truth)

The evaluation asks: given the issue text, does CodeGraph's top-k output include the files/functions from the gold patch?

docs.title: Recall@k
docs.text:
"Of all files/functions that needed changing, what fraction appear in our top-k results?"

Recall@k = |gold ∩ top_k| / |gold|

Example: Bug required changing 3 functions. Top-10 results included 2 of them → Recall@10 = 0.667

We report Recall@5 and Recall@10. Higher k gives higher recall but requires the AI agent to process more context.

docs.title: MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank)
docs.text:
"On average, how early does the first correct result appear?"

MRR = (1/N) × Σ (1 / rank_of_first_correct_result)
First correct result at rank Contribution 1 1.000 3 0.333 5 0.200 10 0.100 Not found 0.000

Example: Across 3 bug reports, first correct at ranks 1, 3, 7 → MRR = (1.0 + 0.33 + 0.14) / 3 = 0.49

Higher MRR means correct results appear earlier — the AI agent gets useful context faster.

docs.title: Baselines
docs.text:
Every evaluation needs baselines to establish what "good" looks like.

Baseline How It Works What It Tests Random Pick k random files from the repo Floor — any method must beat this BM25 Keyword search of issue text against code contents Text-only retrieval (no graph) 1-hop ego-graph Return direct graph neighbors of seeds (no PPR) Whether multi-hop adds value over 1-hop

The goal is for PPR to outperform all three baselines.

docs.title: Ablation Studies
docs.text:
Ablations remove or change one component to measure its contribution.

Experiment Change What It Reveals Remove CALLS edges Graph has only IMPORTS, INHERITS_FROM, CONTAINS Are call relationships important? Remove IMPORTS edges Graph has only CALLS, INHERITS_FROM, CONTAINS Are file-level imports important? Remove INHERITS_FROM edges Graph has only CALLS, IMPORTS, CONTAINS Does inheritance matter? Alpha sweep α ∈ What's the optimal PPR spread? Direction sweep Natural vs Reversed vs Undirected PPR Which edge direction is best? IDF on/off With vs without IDF edge reweighting Does downweighting utility functions help? PPR vs 1-hop PPR vs simple neighbor lookup Is multi-hop necessary? Top-k sweep k ∈ How many results are needed for good recall?

Each ablation is a separate run with exactly one variable changed. The ablations are thesis content regardless of outcome — finding that a component doesn't help is a valid research finding.

docs.title: Running the Evaluation
docs.text: The evaluation pipeline (evaluations/) is work in progress. The manual process:

docs.title: Step 1: Get SWE-bench Lite
docs.text:
# SWE-bench Lite is available from HuggingFace
pip install datasets
python -c "
from datasets import load_dataset
dataset = load_dataset('princeton-nlp/SWE-bench_Lite', split='test')
print(f'Loaded {len(dataset)} instances')
"

docs.title: Step 2: For Each Instance
docs.text:
for instance in dataset:
    repo = instance['repo']          # e.g., "django/django"
    issue_text = instance['problem_statement']   # bug description
    gold_files = extract_gold_files(instance['patch'])  # files changed in fix

    # 1. Check out repo at the "before fix" commit
    checkout_repo(repo, instance['base_commit'])

    # 2. Build the code graph
    build_graph(repo_path, config)

    # 3. Extract seeds from the issue text
    seeds = extract_seeds(
        task_description=issue_text,
        mentioned_entities=extract_mentioned_names(issue_text),
        config=config['seed_selection']
    )

    # 4. Run PPR
    results = run_ppr(seeds, top_k=10, config=config['ppr'])

    # 5. Compute metrics
    retrieved_files = {r.file_path for r in results}
    recall_at_10 = len(gold_files & retrieved_files) / len(gold_files)
    mrr = compute_mrr(results, gold_files)

    record(instance['instance_id'], recall_at_10, mrr)

docs.title: Step 3: Aggregate Results
docs.text:
avg_recall_5  = mean(results[r]['recall_5'] for r in results)
avg_recall_10 = mean(results[r]['recall_10'] for r in results)
avg_mrr       = mean(results[r]['mrr'] for r in results)

print(f"Recall@5:  {avg_recall_5:.3f}")
print(f"Recall@10: {avg_recall_10:.3f}")
print(f"MRR:       {avg_mrr:.3f}")

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text:
Based on related work:

Method Recall@10 (approximate) Random ~0.05–0.10 BM25 ~0.25–0.40 1-hop ego-graph ~0.30–0.45 PPR (our method) ~0.40–0.60 (thesis goal)

These are rough estimates — actual results depend on the specific seed extraction quality and graph completeness.

docs.title: Evaluation Directory
docs.text:
The evaluations/ directory contains: - Scripts for running individual instances - Results from completed runs - Comparison tables across ablation variants

This directory is not under version control (gitignored) because evaluation results can be large.

docs.title: Related Reading
docs.text:
• Personalized PageRank — the algorithm being evaluated
  • Seed Selection — how seeds are derived from issue text
  • Future Work — planned evaluation improvements
  • docs/09-evaluation.md — detailed academic treatment of the evaluation methodology
  • docs/10-research-papers.md — prior work this evaluation is compared against

docs.title: MCP Setup Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to connect CodeGraph's MCP server to AI coding assistants so they can call CodeGraph tools directly from your IDE.

docs.title: How MCP Works (Brief Overview)
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) works like a plugin system for AI tools. CodeGraph runs as a local MCP server; your AI host (Claude Code, Cursor, Claude Desktop) connects to it and gains access to its three tools.

AI Host (Claude Code)  ←──MCP stdio──→  CodeGraph MCP Server  ←──→  Neo4j

The AI decides when to call the tools. You don't need to do anything — once connected, the AI will automatically use get_relevant_context when it thinks graph-based context would help.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before connecting any AI host:

  1. Neo4j is running with the GDS plugin installed
  2. The code graph has been built for your target repository (python -m scripts.demo_neo4j or scripts/rebuild_graph.py)
  3. python -m src.mcp_server starts without errors

Test the server locally: 

python -m src.mcp_server
# Should start and wait silently (no output = working correctly)
# Press Ctrl+C to stop

docs.title: Connecting to Claude Code (Claude CLI)
docs.text: Claude Code reads MCP server configuration from a JSON file.

docs.title: Step 1: Find or Create the Config File
docs.text:
Claude Code looks for MCP config at: - macOS/Linux: ~/.config/claude/mcp_servers.json - Windows: %APPDATA%\claude\mcp_servers.json

Create the file if it doesn't exist.

docs.title: Step 2: Add CodeGraph as an MCP Server
docs.text:
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "codegraph": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["-m", "src.mcp_server"],
      "cwd": "/absolute/path/to/your/codegraph/directory"
    }
  }
}

Important: cwd must be the absolute path to the CodeGraph project directory (where config.yaml lives). The server reads config.yaml from this directory.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Start a Claude Code session in your target project directory and type:

/tools

You should see codegraph::get_relevant_context, codegraph::query_dependencies, and codegraph::get_graph_stats listed.

docs.title: Step 1: Open MCP Settings
docs.text: In Cursor: Settings → MCP → Add MCP Server

docs.title: Step 2: Configure the Server
docs.text:
Fill in: - Name: codegraph - Command: python -m src.mcp_server - Working Directory: /absolute/path/to/codegraph/

Or edit ~/.cursor/mcp.json directly:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "codegraph": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["-m", "src.mcp_server"],
      "cwd": "/absolute/path/to/codegraph/"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Enable MCP in Cursor
docs.text: Go to Settings → search "MCP" → ensure Enable MCP is toggled on.

docs.title: Step 1: Edit the Config File
docs.text: macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json Windows: %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json

docs.title: Step 2: Add the Server
docs.text:
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "codegraph": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["-m", "src.mcp_server"],
      "cwd": "/absolute/path/to/codegraph/"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Restart Claude Desktop
docs.text: The MCP server is started fresh each time Claude Desktop launches. After restarting, you should see the CodeGraph tools in the tool menu (hammer icon).

docs.title: Using CodeGraph Tools in a Session
docs.text:
Once connected, the AI uses tools automatically. You can also prompt it explicitly:

Explicit prompts that trigger tool use: - "Use CodeGraph to find relevant context for this authentication bug" - "What are the direct callers of the authenticate function?" - "Get the graph stats for this codebase"

Natural prompts the AI will auto-decide to use tools for: - "Fix the token expiry bug in the auth service" - "Refactor the payment processing flow" - "Why is the login failing?"

Example session: 

You: Fix the authentication timeout bug. Users are getting logged out after 5 minutes.

Claude: Let me get relevant context from the code graph first.

[calls get_relevant_context(
    task_description="authentication timeout bug users logged out 5 minutes",
    mentioned_entities=["authenticate", "timeout", "session"]
)]

I found 8 relevant functions. Looking at TokenManager.validate_token (score: 0.12),
I can see the session timeout is hardcoded to 300 seconds...

docs.title: Pointing CodeGraph at a Different Repository
docs.text:
By default, CodeGraph indexes the project_root specified in config.yaml. To analyze a different codebase:

  1. Update config.yaml: 

    project_root: "/path/to/target/repository"

  2. Rebuild the graph: 

    python -m scripts.rebuild_graph

  3. The MCP server automatically uses the new graph for subsequent queries.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Tools don't appear in the AI host: - Check that cwd in the MCP config is the correct absolute path - Verify python -m src.mcp_server works in that directory - Some hosts require restarting after MCP config changes

Tools appear but return errors: - Neo4j must be running when the AI calls the tools - Check that the graph is built: run get_graph_stats explicitly

"Module not found" errors: - The Python environment used by the MCP config must have CodeGraph installed - Use the full Python path if needed: /path/to/venv/bin/python

See Troubleshooting → for more.

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: All CodeGraph configuration lives in config.yaml at the project root. The file is loaded once at startup and passed through the system via dependency injection.

docs.title: Full Configuration File
docs.text:
# config.yaml

# ── Project Settings ──────────────────────────────────────────
project_root: "."                # Root of the codebase to index

exclude_patterns:                # Paths/patterns to skip during parsing
  - "__pycache__"
  - ".venv"
  - ".git"
  - "*.pyc"
  - ".pytest_cache"

# ── Neo4j Connection ──────────────────────────────────────────
neo4j:
  uri: "neo4j://localhost:7687"  # Connection URI
  username: "neo4j"              # Database username
  password: "your_password"      # Database password — keep this secret
  database: "neo4j"              # Database name (use "neo4j" for the default)

# ── Personalized PageRank ─────────────────────────────────────
ppr:
  damping_factor: 0.85     # 1 − alpha; higher = walks spread further from seeds
  max_iterations: 20       # Convergence iterations (20 is sufficient)
  tolerance: 1.0e-7        # Early stopping threshold
  top_k: 20                # How many PPR results to return before post-processing

# ── Seed Selection ────────────────────────────────────────────
seed_selection:
  entity_match_weight: 0.6  # Weight for explicitly mentioned entities
  bm25_weight: 0.3          # Weight for BM25 keyword search results
  current_file_weight: 0.1  # Weight for entities in the current file
  bm25_top_n: 5             # How many BM25 top results to include as seeds

# ── MCP Server ────────────────────────────────────────────────
mcp:
  server_name: "codegraph"    # Server name shown to MCP clients
  default_token_budget: 6000  # Max tokens in the response to the AI agent
  default_top_k: 15           # Default number of results returned by get_relevant_context

docs.title: Project Settings
docs.text: Field Type Default Description project_root string "." Root directory of the codebase to parse. Relative paths are resolved from the working directory. exclude_patterns list[string] See above Files/directories matching these patterns are skipped during parsing. Supports glob syntax.

docs.title: Neo4j Connection
docs.text:
Field Type Default Description uri string "neo4j://localhost:7687" Neo4j connection URI. Use bolt:// for local non-TLS, neo4j+s:// for TLS-encrypted cloud instances. username string "neo4j" Database username. password string — Database password. Never commit to version control. database string "neo4j" Name of the database to use. The default Neo4j installation uses "neo4j".

Connection URIs by environment:

Environment URI Format Local (Neo4j Desktop) neo4j://localhost:7687 Docker neo4j://localhost:7687 Neo4j AuraDB (cloud) neo4j+s://xxxxxxxx.databases.neo4j.io

docs.title: PPR Settings
docs.text:
Field Type Default Description damping_factor float (0–1) 0.85 Probability of continuing the random walk (= 1 − restart probability). Higher values spread further from seeds. 0.85 is the standard PageRank default. max_iterations int 20 Maximum convergence iterations. Scores typically converge by iteration 15–20. tolerance float 1e-7 Early stopping: if max score change between iterations is below this, stop early. top_k int 20 How many nodes PPR returns (by score) before token budgeting. Setting higher means post-processing sees more candidates.

Effect of damping_factor: - 0.95 → walk spreads very far from seeds; results include loosely related code - 0.85 → balanced (recommended) - 0.70 → walk stays close to seeds; results tightly clustered around mentioned entities

docs.title: Seed Selection Settings
docs.text:
Field Type Default Description entity_match_weight float (0–1) 0.6 Weight given to entities explicitly mentioned by the AI agent. bm25_weight float (0–1) 0.3 Weight given to entities found via BM25 text search of the task description. current_file_weight float (0–1) 0.1 Weight given to entities in the current file (if provided). bm25_top_n int 5 How many top BM25 results to include in the seed set.

Constraint: entity_match_weight + bm25_weight + current_file_weight should equal 1.0. The system normalizes the final personalization vector, so the exact sum doesn't matter, but it should be close.

docs.title: MCP Server Settings
docs.text: Field Type Default Description server_name string "codegraph" Name shown to MCP clients in their tool list. default_token_budget int 6000 Maximum tokens in the context returned by get_relevant_context. Can be overridden per-call. default_top_k int 15 Default number of results returned. Can be overridden by the AI agent when calling the tool.

docs.title: Loading Configuration in Code
docs.text:
from src.graph.connection import load_full_config

config = load_full_config('config.yaml')

# Access sections
neo4j_config = config['neo4j']
ppr_config = config['ppr']
seed_config = config['seed_selection']
mcp_config = config['mcp']

The load_full_config() function reads the YAML file and returns a plain dict. Configuration is passed explicitly as function arguments — no global state.

docs.title: Cypher Query Reference
docs.text: Useful Cypher queries for exploring and inspecting the CodeGraph Neo4j database. Run these in the Neo4j Browser (http://localhost:7474) or via the Neo4j Python driver.

docs.title: Graph Overview
docs.text:
// Count all nodes by type
MATCH (n)
RETURN labels(n) AS type, count(*) AS count
ORDER BY count DESC

// Count all edges by type
MATCH ()-[r]->()
RETURN type(r) AS relation, count(*) AS count
ORDER BY count DESC

// Total nodes and edges
MATCH (n) WITH count(n) AS nodes
MATCH ()-[r]->() WITH nodes, count(r) AS edges
RETURN nodes, edges

docs.title: Finding Entities
docs.text:
// Find a function/method by name
MATCH (n {name: "authenticate"})
RETURN labels(n), n.name, n.file_path, n.line_number

// Find all methods of a class
MATCH (c:Class {name: "AuthService"})-[:CONTAINS]->(m:Method)
RETURN m.name, m.line_number, m.signature
ORDER BY m.line_number

// Find all classes in a file
MATCH (f:File {name: "auth_service.py"})-[:CONTAINS]->(c:Class)
RETURN c.name, c.line_number

// Find entities matching a partial name (case insensitive)
MATCH (n)
WHERE toLower(n.name) CONTAINS "auth"
RETURN labels(n), n.name, n.file_path
LIMIT 20

docs.title: Call Graph Navigation
docs.text:
// What does this function call? (downstream dependencies)
MATCH (f {name: "authenticate"})-[:CALLS]->(callee)
RETURN callee.name, callee.file_path, callee.line_number

// What calls this function? (upstream — who depends on it)
MATCH (caller)-[:CALLS]->(f {name: "authenticate"})
RETURN caller.name, caller.file_path

// Full call chain — 2 hops downstream
MATCH path = (start {name: "authenticate"})-[:CALLS*1..2]->(end)
RETURN [n IN nodes(path) | n.name] AS chain

// Find the most-called functions (hotspots)
MATCH ()-[:CALLS]->(f)
RETURN f.name, f.file_path, count(*) AS call_count
ORDER BY call_count DESC
LIMIT 20

// Find functions that call nothing (leaf nodes)
MATCH (f:Function)
WHERE NOT (f)-[:CALLS]->()
RETURN f.name, f.file_path
LIMIT 20

// Detect potential circular dependencies (calls that form cycles)
MATCH path = (a)-[:CALLS*2..5]->(a)
RETURN [n IN nodes(path) | n.name] AS cycle
LIMIT 10

docs.title: Import Graph Navigation
docs.text:
// What does this file import?
MATCH (f:File {name: "auth_service.py"})-[:IMPORTS]->(dep:File)
RETURN dep.name, dep.file_path

// What files import from this file?
MATCH (f:File)-[:IMPORTS]->(target:File {name: "user.py"})
RETURN f.name, f.file_path

// Find the most-imported files (high fan-in = central modules)
MATCH ()-[:IMPORTS]->(f:File)
RETURN f.name, count(*) AS import_count
ORDER BY import_count DESC
LIMIT 10

// Find files that import nothing from the project (entry points or utilities)
MATCH (f:File)
WHERE NOT (f)-[:IMPORTS]->()
RETURN f.name, f.file_path

// Find the deepest import chains
MATCH path = (a:File)-[:IMPORTS*]->(z:File)
WHERE NOT (z)-[:IMPORTS]->()
RETURN length(path) AS depth, [n IN nodes(path) | n.name] AS chain
ORDER BY depth DESC
LIMIT 5

docs.title: Inheritance Graph
docs.text:
// Find all classes that inherit from a base class
MATCH (child:Class)-[:INHERITS_FROM]->(parent:Class {name: "BaseService"})
RETURN child.name, child.file_path

// Find the full inheritance hierarchy (multi-level)
MATCH path = (leaf:Class)-[:INHERITS_FROM*]->(root:Class)
WHERE NOT (root)-[:INHERITS_FROM]->()
RETURN [n IN nodes(path) | n.name] AS hierarchy

// Find classes with multiple inheritance
MATCH (c:Class)-[:INHERITS_FROM]->(p:Class)
WITH c, count(p) AS parent_count
WHERE parent_count > 1
RETURN c.name, c.file_path, parent_count

docs.title: Graph Health & Diagnostics
docs.text:
// Check for nodes with no edges (orphans — parser may have failed)
MATCH (n)
WHERE NOT (n)-[]-() AND NOT ()-[]-(n)
RETURN labels(n), n.name, n.file_path
LIMIT 20

// Find files with very high connectivity (god files)
MATCH (f:File)-[r]-()
RETURN f.name, count(r) AS connections
ORDER BY connections DESC
LIMIT 10

// Check edge weight distribution
MATCH ()-[r]->()
RETURN type(r), min(r.weight), max(r.weight), avg(r.weight)

// Count nodes by file (which files have the most entities?)
MATCH (f:File)-[:CONTAINS]->(n)
RETURN f.name, count(n) AS entity_count
ORDER BY entity_count DESC
LIMIT 15

docs.title: GDS Projection Management
docs.text:
// Check if the projection exists
CALL gds.graph.exists('codegraph')
YIELD exists

// List all projections
CALL gds.graph.list()
YIELD graphName, nodeCount, relationshipCount, creationTime

// Drop and recreate the projection (needed after graph updates)
CALL gds.graph.drop('codegraph', false)
YIELD graphName

// Create the projection used by PPR
CALL gds.graph.project(
  'codegraph',
  ['Function', 'Method', 'Class', 'File'],
  {
    CALLS:         {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    IMPORTS:       {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    INHERITS_FROM: {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    CONTAINS:      {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'}
  }
)

docs.title: Running PPR Manually
docs.text:
// Run PPR from a specific seed
MATCH (seed:Method {name: 'authenticate'})
CALL gds.pageRank.stream('codegraph', {
  maxIterations: 20,
  dampingFactor: 0.85,
  sourceNodes: [seed],
  relationshipWeightProperty: 'weight'
})
YIELD nodeId, score
RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS name,
       gds.util.asNode(nodeId).file_path AS file,
       score
ORDER BY score DESC
LIMIT 20

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
// Clear all data (destructive — use with caution)
MATCH (n) DETACH DELETE n

// Verify GDS version
RETURN gds.version()

// Check database info
CALL dbms.components()
YIELD name, versions

docs.title: MCP Tools Reference
docs.text: CodeGraph exposes three tools over the Model Context Protocol. This page documents each tool's parameters, behavior, and expected output.

docs.title: What Is MCP?
docs.text:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard (Anthropic, November 2024) that lets AI applications communicate with external tools and data sources. It works like a plugin system: any MCP-compatible AI host (Claude Code, Cursor, Claude Desktop) can discover and use MCP servers.

Transport: CodeGraph uses stdio transport — the MCP server runs as a subprocess of the AI host, communicating over standard input/output. No network configuration needed.

Entry point: 

python -m src.mcp_server

docs.title: Tool 1: get_relevant_context
docs.text: The primary tool. Runs the full pipeline: seed extraction → PPR → post-processing → formatted code snippets.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description task_description string Yes Natural language description of what the AI agent is working on. Used for BM25 seed extraction. mentioned_entities list[string] No Entity names the AI agent has explicitly identified as relevant (function/class names). Used for high-weight seed matching. current_file string No File path the developer is currently viewing. Used as a low-weight seed hint. top_k int No (default: 15) How many results to return. Actual count may be lower due to token budgeting. token_budget int No (default: 6000) Maximum tokens of source code to include in the response.

docs.title: Example Call
docs.text:
{
  "tool": "get_relevant_context",
  "arguments": {
    "task_description": "fix the authentication timeout bug",
    "mentioned_entities": ["authenticate", "AuthService", "TokenExpiredError"],
    "current_file": "src/services/auth_service.py",
    "top_k": 15,
    "token_budget": 6000
  }
}

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text:
A JSON string containing a list of context results, ordered by relevance:

[
  {
    "entity": "AuthService.authenticate",
    "qualified_name": "src/services/auth_service.py::AuthService.authenticate",
    "file": "src/services/auth_service.py",
    "lines": "45-78",
    "relevance_score": 0.1502,
    "source_code": "def authenticate(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool:\n    ..."
  },
  {
    "entity": "TokenManager.validate_token",
    "qualified_name": "src/auth/token_manager.py::TokenManager.validate_token",
    "file": "src/auth/token_manager.py",
    "lines": "12-31",
    "relevance_score": 0.0897,
    "source_code": "def validate_token(self, token: str) -> bool:\n    ..."
  }
]

docs.title: When to Use This Tool
docs.text: Use this tool at the start of any coding task to get structural context. Pass as specific a description as possible, and include entity names if you've already identified relevant functions.

docs.title: Tool 2: query_dependencies
docs.text: Returns raw dependency information for a specific entity — no PPR, just direct connections.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description entity_name string Yes Name of the entity to query (function, class, or method name). direction string No (default: "both") "upstream" (what calls this), "downstream" (what this calls), or "both". depth int No (default: 1) 1 = direct connections only. 2 = include one more hop.

docs.title: Example Call
docs.text:
{
  "tool": "query_dependencies",
  "arguments": {
    "entity_name": "authenticate",
    "direction": "both",
    "depth": 1
  }
}

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text:
{
  "entity": "AuthService.authenticate",
  "file": "src/services/auth_service.py",
  "upstream": [
    {"name": "process_login", "file": "src/controllers/login.py", "relation": "CALLS"}
  ],
  "downstream": [
    {"name": "hash_password", "file": "src/utils/crypto.py", "relation": "CALLS"},
    {"name": "User.find_by_username", "file": "src/models/user.py", "relation": "CALLS"}
  ]
}

docs.title: When to Use This Tool
docs.text: Use when you need to understand exactly what a specific function calls or what calls it — without the broader PPR exploration. Good for impact analysis ("if I change X, what breaks?") or for navigating a call chain step by step.

docs.title: Tool 3: get_graph_stats
docs.text: Returns statistics about the code graph. No parameters.

docs.title: Example Call
docs.text:
{
  "tool": "get_graph_stats",
  "arguments": {}
}

docs.title: Return Value
docs.text:
{
  "total_nodes": 1247,
  "node_counts": {
    "File": 87,
    "Class": 234,
    "Function": 412,
    "Method": 514
  },
  "total_edges": 4831,
  "edge_counts": {
    "CONTAINS": 1247,
    "IMPORTS": 312,
    "CALLS": 2891,
    "INHERITS_FROM": 381
  },
  "most_connected_files": [
    {"file": "src/core/base.py", "connections": 89},
    {"file": "src/utils/helpers.py", "connections": 67}
  ],
  "graph_name": "codegraph",
  "projection_exists": true
}

docs.title: When to Use This Tool
docs.text: Use at the start of a session to understand the codebase size and structure. The most-connected files are often the core modules worth knowing about. The AI agent can also use this to check if the graph has been built before attempting retrieval.

docs.title: How Claude Uses These Tools
docs.text:
A typical Claude Code interaction:

User: "Fix the authentication timeout bug"

Claude:
1. Calls get_graph_stats → confirms graph exists, sees 1247 nodes
2. Calls get_relevant_context(
       task_description="fix authentication timeout bug",
       mentioned_entities=["authenticate", "timeout", "AuthService"]
   )
3. Receives top 12 code snippets with relevance scores
4. Uses this context to understand the authentication flow
5. Writes the fix

The AI agent decides when to call the tools and how many times. For complex tasks, it may call get_relevant_context multiple times with different descriptions as it learns more about the problem.

docs.title: Source File
docs.text:
All three tools are defined in src/mcp_server.py using the FastMCP framework:

from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("codegraph")

@mcp.tool()
def get_relevant_context(task_description: str, ...) -> str:
    ...

@mcp.tool()
def query_dependencies(entity_name: str, ...) -> str:
    ...

@mcp.tool()
def get_graph_stats() -> str:
    ...

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and how to fix them.

docs.title: "Connection refused" / "ServiceUnavailable"
docs.text:
Symptom: 

neo4j.exceptions.ServiceUnavailable: Failed to establish connection to ('localhost', 7687)

Cause: Neo4j isn't running.

Fix: - Neo4j Desktop: Open the app and click Start on your database - Docker: docker start neo4j (or run the full docker command from Installation) - Verify it's running: curl http://localhost:7474 should return a JSON response

docs.title: "Authentication failed"
docs.text:
Symptom: 

neo4j.exceptions.AuthError: {code: Neo.ClientError.Security.Unauthorized}

Fix: Check config.yaml — the password field must match what you set when creating the Neo4j database. If you forgot your password, reset it via Neo4j Desktop (database settings → Reset password).

docs.title: "Database does not exist"
docs.text:
Symptom: 

neo4j.exceptions.ClientError: Database 'mydb' does not exist.

Fix: Set database: "neo4j" in config.yaml. The default Neo4j installation only has a database named neo4j. You only need to change this if you deliberately created a named database.

docs.title: "There is no procedure with the name gds.graph.project"
docs.text:
Symptom: Running any GDS operation fails with a "procedure not found" error.

Cause: The GDS plugin isn't installed.

Fix: - Neo4j Desktop: Go to your database → Plugins tab → Install Graph Data Science Library → restart - Docker: Add -e NEO4J_PLUGINS='["graph-data-science"]' when running the container

Verify fix: Run RETURN gds.version() in the Neo4j Browser — it should return a version string.

docs.title: "The graph 'codegraph' does not exist"
docs.text:
Symptom: PPR fails because the GDS projection hasn't been created.

Fix: Build the graph first (which creates the projection), or create it manually:

CALL gds.graph.project(
  'codegraph',
  ['Function', 'Method', 'Class', 'File'],
  {
    CALLS:         {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    IMPORTS:       {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    INHERITS_FROM: {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'},
    CONTAINS:      {orientation: 'UNDIRECTED', properties: 'weight'}
  }
)

docs.title: "Graph 'codegraph' already exists"
docs.text:
Symptom: When rebuilding the graph, the projection creation fails because an old one exists.

Fix: Drop the old projection first:

CALL gds.graph.drop('codegraph', false)

The false parameter means "don't fail if it doesn't exist." The pipeline's ensure_graph_ready() handles this automatically, but if you're building manually, run this first.

docs.title: "SyntaxError: Could not parse file"
docs.text:
Symptom: A file fails to parse.

Behavior: tree-sitter is error-tolerant and will partially parse broken files. The error is logged as a warning and the file is skipped. Parsing continues for all other files.

Fix: Check the specific file for syntax errors: 

python -m py_compile path/to/file.py

docs.title: "0 entities extracted from X files"
docs.text:
Symptom: Graph build completes but the database is empty.

Causes: 1. project_root in config.yaml points to the wrong directory 2. All files are matched by exclude_patterns 3. The directory contains no .py files

Fix: 

# Verify which files would be parsed
python -c "
from src.parser.python_parser import parse_directory, create_parser
parser = create_parser()
results = parse_directory('.', parser)
print(f'Found {len(results)} files')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(r.file_path)
"

docs.title: "Empty results from get_relevant_context"
docs.text:
Symptom: The MCP tool returns an empty list or very few results.

Check 1: Is the graph built? Run in Neo4j Browser: 

MATCH (n) RETURN count(n)
If this returns 0, the graph hasn't been built.

Check 2: Did seed extraction find anything? 

from src.retrieval.seed_selection import extract_seeds
seeds = extract_seeds(
    task_description="your task",
    mentioned_entities=["some_function"],
    current_file=None,
    all_entities=[],  # pass your FileEntities here
    config=config['seed_selection']
)
print(seeds)

Check 3: Is the GDS projection up to date? Drop and recreate it after rebuilding the graph.

docs.title: "PPR scores are all near 0"
docs.text:
Symptom: PPR runs but returns scores like 0.0001 for everything.

Cause: Seeds weren't matched to actual nodes in the graph. PPR with no valid seed nodes assigns tiny baseline scores to everything.

Fix: Check that the entity names in mentioned_entities match names in the graph: 

MATCH (n {name: "your_entity_name"})
RETURN n.name, n.file_path
Entity names are case-sensitive.

docs.title: Tests fail with "No module named 'src'"
docs.text:
Fix: Install the package in editable mode from the project root: 

python -m pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Neo4j tests are skipped
docs.text:
Behavior: Tests marked @neo4j_required skip automatically when Neo4j isn't running. This is expected. Start Neo4j to run the full test suite.

Check which tests skipped: 

python -m pytest tests/ -v 2>&1 | grep SKIP

docs.title: Tests fail with "graphdatascience version mismatch"
docs.text:
Fix: Upgrade the GDS Python client: 

pip install --upgrade graphdatascience

Make sure the GDS plugin version in Neo4j and the Python client version are compatible. GDS Python client 1.7+ works with GDS plugin 2.x.

docs.title: Claude Code doesn't see the "codegraph" tools
docs.text:
Cause: The MCP server isn't configured in Claude Code's settings.

Fix: See MCP Setup Guide → for the full configuration walkthrough.

docs.title: MCP server starts but tools return errors
docs.text: Check the server logs: When running python -m src.mcp_server, errors are printed to stderr. Look for: - ConnectionRefusedError → Neo4j isn't running - KeyError: 'neo4j' → config.yaml is missing or malformed - gds.graph.exists error → GDS not installed

docs.title: Getting More Help
docs.text:
1. Check the logs: Set logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG) for verbose output
  2. Run tests: python -m pytest tests/test_graph_resolution.py -v (no Neo4j needed)
  3. Inspect the graph: Use the Neo4j Browser at http://localhost:7474
  4. Check decisions: DECISIONS.md explains why specific choices were made
